topics.title: Authentication Protocols - Message Authentication Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Authentication Protocols - Message Authentication Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Message Authentication** is the procedure that enables a receiver of a message to verify its **origin** (source authentication) and confirm that it has **not been altered or replayed** during transit (data integrity and timeliness). The set of conditions that must be satisfied for a communication session to be deemed secure is collectively termed **Message Authentication Requirements**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Network Security Protocols*, every authenticated exchange must satisfy a layered set of four canonical security services, often summarized using the **A-I-N-F** mnemonic:

| # | Requirement | Definition |
|---|------------|------------|
| 1 | **Confidentiality (Privacy)** | Ensuring that the message content is readable only by the intended parties. |
| 2 | **Integrity** | Guaranteeing that the message has not been modified, inserted, deleted, or reordered. |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Confirming the identity of the originator of the message. |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation & Freshness** | Preventing the sender from denying the transmission and assuring the message is current (not a replay). |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Courier" Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy (Plain English)**
> Imagine you are sending a confidential legal document to a lawyer through a courier.
> 1. **Confidentiality** → The envelope is locked with a key only the lawyer possesses.
> 2. **Integrity** → The envelope is sealed with wax and a unique stamp; any break in the seal is visually obvious.
> 3. **Authentication** → The wax seal bears the personal crest of your family, so the lawyer instantly knows the letter genuinely came from you.
> 4. **Non-Repudiation & Freshness** → The document also carries a date-time stamp and your handwritten signature, so you cannot later deny having sent it, and the lawyer can verify it was not a photocopy of a 2-year-old letter.

In the digital realm, the **wax seal** is replaced by a cryptographic primitive — a **MAC, hash, or digital signature** — and the **physical key** is replaced by a **symmetric or asymmetric encryption key**.

### 1.3 Why Are These Requirements Needed?

In an open network (such as the Internet), an attacker can perpetrate several well-known threats. KTU 2024 emphasizes the following:

- **Disclosure**: An unauthorized party reads the contents of a message.
- **Traffic Analysis**: An adversary infers patterns even when content is encrypted.
- **Masquerade**: An attacker forges a message pretending to be a legitimate user.
- **Content Modification**: Bits of the message are altered in transit.
- **Sequence Manipulation**: Messages are reordered, delayed, or replayed.
- **Repudiation**: The sender denies ever having sent the message.

The **Message Authentication Requirements** directly counter each of these threats.

### 1.4 Three Canonical Functions Used to Satisfy the Requirements

The KTU 2024 module classifies message authentication functions into **three primary classes**:

1. **Message Encryption** — The ciphertext itself acts as the authenticator (the receiver assumes only the legitimate sender knows the key).
2. **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** — A fixed-size tag is appended to the message; verified using a shared secret key.
3. **Hash Function** — A one-way hash produces a fingerprint; secured using a key (HMAC) or combined with a digital signature.

> [!NOTE] **Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)**
> - **MAC Output Length**: typically **128 bits** or **256 bits**.
> - **Hash Output (SHA family)**: **SHA-1** = 160 bits (deprecated), **SHA-256** = 256 bits, **SHA-512** = 512 bits.
> - **Digital Signature Standards**: RSA (≥2048 bits), ECDSA (≥256-bit curve).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Message Authentication

A robust authentication protocol in KTU 2024 is judged by whether it satisfies the following sequence of logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Key Establishment:** Both parties must first share a **symmetric secret key (K)** or each possess a **public–private key pair (PK, SK)**.
- **Step 2 — Transformation:** A cryptographic function *f* is applied to the plaintext message *M* and the key to produce either ciphertext *C* or an authenticator tag *T*.
- **Step 3 — Transmission:** The transformed value (and possibly the plaintext) is sent across the insecure channel.
- **Step 4 — Reception:** The receiver receives *C* (or *M* + *T*) and applies the inverse cryptographic function using the corresponding key.
- **Step 5 — Verification:** The receiver recomputes the tag and compares; equality implies both **integrity** and **source authenticity**.
- **Step 6 — Freshness Check:** A nonce, sequence number, or timestamp is validated to rule out **replay attacks**.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Approach

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Insight: The Why**
> - **Why Encryption?** It provides **confidentiality** *and* (implicitly) authentication, because only authorized parties possess the key.
> - **Why a MAC?** It provides **integrity + authentication** with **lower overhead** than full encryption, and the plaintext may remain readable (useful in packet headers).
> - **Why a Hash?** It is **fast, public, and keyless**; combined with a key (HMAC) or with public-key signing it becomes a complete authenticator.
> - **Why a Digital Signature?** It adds **non-repudiation**, which symmetric MACs cannot offer, because the verification key is public.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Function / Concept | Formula / Notation | Inputs | Output | Security Service |
|---|-------------------|--------------------|--------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Symmetric Encryption** | $C = E_K(M)$ | Plaintext $M$, Secret Key $K$ | Ciphertext $C$ | Confidentiality + Implicit Auth. |
| 2 | **Decryption** | $M = D_K(C)$ | Ciphertext $C$, Secret Key $K$ | Plaintext $M$ | — |
| 3 | **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** | $T = MAC_K(M)$ | Message $M$, Shared Key $K$ | Tag $T$ of size $n$ | Integrity + Authentication |
| 4 | **MAC Verification** | $T' = MAC_K(M')$ ; Check $T' \stackrel{?}{=} T$ | Recomputed tag $T'$, Received tag $T$ | Boolean (Valid/Invalid) | Replay-vulnerable unless nonce used |
| 5 | **Unkeyed Hash** | $h = H(M)$ | Message $M$ | Hash digest $h$ of length $L$ | Integrity only (no auth) |
| 6 | **Keyed Hash (HMAC)** | $HMAC_K(M) = H((K_0 \oplus opad) \mid\mid H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M))$ | Message $M$, Key $K$ | MAC tag | Integrity + Authentication |
| 7 | **Digital Signature (Signing)** | $\sigma = Sign_{SK}(h(M))$ | Private key $SK$, Hash of $M$ | Signature $\sigma$ | Auth + Non-Repudiation |
| 8 | **Signature Verification** | Verify$_{PK}(\sigma, h(M)) \to \{0,1\}$ | Public key $PK$, Signature $\sigma$ | Boolean | Confirms origin and integrity |
| 9 | **Freshness / Nonce Check** | $\Delta t = \vert t_{recv} - t_{send} \vert \le \Delta_{max}$ | Timestamp $t$ | Boolean | Replay resistance |
| 10 | **Birthday Bound (Collision)** | $P(\text{collision}) \approx 0.5$ when $n \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^L}$ | Hash output length $L$ bits | Sample size $n$ | Determines minimum secure hash length |

> [!WARNING] **LaTeX Note:** Vertical bars within formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HTTPS / TLS 1.3** uses a combination of **HMAC + AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)** to satisfy all four message authentication requirements simultaneously.
- **Banking (ISO 8583)** mandates **MAC** on every financial transaction message.
- **Blockchain** uses **ECDSA digital signatures** to provide non-repudiation on every transaction.
- **Email security (PGP / S/MIME)** stacks **hash + digital signature + encryption** in that order.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $L = 128$ Bits Is the Minimum Secure MAC Output

A MAC is considered secure if finding a forgery without the key requires work comparable to $\mathcal{O}(2^L)$ where $L$ is the tag length. The probability of an attacker guessing a valid tag at random is:

$$
P_{guess} = \frac{1}{2^L}
$$

For an attacker to succeed with probability at least 0.5 (a 50% chance), the number of trials required follows from the **birthday / coupon-collector intuition**. The expected number of attempts $N$ satisfies:

$$
1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^L}\right)^N \ge 0.5
$$

Step-by-step simplification:

$$
\left(1 - \frac{1}{2^L}\right)^N \le 0.5
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
N \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{1}{2^L}\right) \le \ln(0.5)
$$

Using the approximation $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for very small $x$ (which holds for large $L$):

$$
N \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2^L}\right) \le -0.693
$$

Multiplying both sides by $-2^L$ (flipping the inequality sign):

$$
N \ge 0.693 \cdot 2^L
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 2^{L-1}}
$$

**Conclusion:** An attacker requires approximately $2^{L-1}$ trials to break the MAC. For $L = 128$ bits, this is $2^{127} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{38}$ trials, which is computationally infeasible. Hence **128-bit MAC is the KTU-recommended minimum**.

### 3.2 Derivation: HMAC Construction Step-by-Step

The HMAC algorithm (RFC 2104, used in TLS, IPsec, SSH) is constructed as follows. Let the hash function be $H$ with block size $B$ bytes and output size $L$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Key Padding:**
If the key $K$ is longer than $B$ bytes, hash it down; if shorter, pad with zeros:

$$
K_0 = \begin{cases} H(K) & \text{if } \vert K \vert > B \\ K \mid\mid 0^{B - \vert K \vert} & \text{if } \vert K \vert \le B \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Inner Pad and Outer Pad Formation:**

$$
ipad = 0x36 \text{ repeated } B \text{ times}
$$

$$
opad = 0x5C \text{ repeated } B \text{ times}
$$

**Step 3 — Inner Hash Computation:**

$$
H_{inner} = H\bigl((K_0 \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Outer Hash Computation (Final HMAC Output):**

$$
\boxed{HMAC_K(M) = H\bigl((K_0 \oplus opad) \mid\mid H_{inner}\bigr)}
$$

This nested construction is provably secure as long as the underlying hash $H$ is a **secure pseudo-random function**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Which Function Provides What?

The following matrix is **directly examinable** in KTU 2024 ESE:

| Function | Confidentiality | Integrity | Authentication | Non-Repudiation | Replay Protection (with nonce) |
|----------|:---------------:|:---------:|:--------------:|:---------------:|:------------------------------:|
| **Symmetric Encryption Only** ($E_K(M)$) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (Implicit) | ❌ | ❌ (needs extra mechanism) |
| **MAC Only** ($MAC_K(M)$, plaintext sent) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (needs extra mechanism) |
| **Encrypt + MAC** ($E_{K1}(M), MAC_{K2}(M)$) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (needs extra mechanism) |
| **Hash + Signature** ($Sign_{SK}(H(M))$) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (needs extra mechanism) |
| **Hash + Signature + Encryption** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (needs extra mechanism) |
| **MAC with Nonce / Timestamp** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Authenticated Encryption (AEAD)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (unless nonce-internal) |

### 3.4 Derivation: Digital Signature Security Bound

For an RSA digital signature with modulus $N$ of $n$ bits, the security level is bounded by the difficulty of integer factorization. The best-known classical algorithm (General Number Field Sieve) has sub-exponential complexity:

$$
L_n[\alpha, c] = \exp\left((c + o(1)) \cdot (\ln n)^\alpha \cdot (\ln \ln n)^{1-\alpha}\right)
$$

For RSA, $\alpha = 1/3$. For $n = 2048$ bits, this gives approximately $2^{112}$ operations, hence the **KTU 2024 recommendation: RSA keys must be at least 2048 bits**, with **3072 bits preferred** for forward security beyond 2030.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Message Authentication Pipeline

> [!NOTE] The diagram below depicts the end-to-end functional flow of a typical message authentication protocol, mapping the interactions between sender, receiver, cryptographic functions, and the insecure channel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sender Application] --> B[Plaintext Message M]
    B --> C{Choose Authentication Function}
    C --> D1[Symmetric Encryption E sub K of M]
    C --> D2[MAC Generation T = MAC sub K of M]
    C --> D3[Hash then Sign Sign sub SK of H of M]
    C --> D4[AEAD Encrypt and MAC simultaneously]
    D1 --> E[Output Ciphertext C]
    D2 --> F1[Output Plaintext M]
    D2 --> F2[Output MAC Tag T]
    D3 --> G1[Output Plaintext M]
    D3 --> G2[Output Signature sigma]
    D4 --> H1[Output Ciphertext C]
    D4 --> H2[Output Auth Tag T]
    E --> X[Insecure Network Channel]
    F1 --> X
    F2 --> X
    G1 --> X
    G2 --> X
    H1 --> X
    H2 --> X
    X --> Y[Receiver Application]
    Y --> Z{Verification Function}
    Z --> W1[Confidentiality Achieved]
    Z --> W2[Integrity Achieved]
    Z --> W3[Authentication Achieved]
    Z --> W4[Non Repudiation Achieved]
    Z --> W5[Freshness Achieved]
```

### 4.2 Protocol Sequence Diagram: Symmetric MAC-based Authentication with Nonce

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Sender Alice
    participant Net as Insecure Network
    participant Bob as Receiver Bob
    Alice->>Alice: Generate random nonce N
    Alice->>Alice: Compute T = MAC sub K of N concatenated with M
    Alice->>Net: Send M concatenated with N concatenated with T
    Net->>Bob: Forward M, N, T
    Bob->>Bob: Recompute T prime = MAC sub K of N concatenated with M
    Bob->>Bob: Check T prime equals T
    Bob->>Bob: Check N is fresh
    Bob-->>Alice: ACK or NAK
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Authentication Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Authentication Requirement] --> Q1{Need Confidentiality?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need Non Repudiation?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Need Non Repudiation?}
    Q2 -- No --> M1[Use MAC]
    Q2 -- Yes --> M2[Use Hash plus Digital Signature]
    Q3 -- No --> M3[Use Symmetric Encryption]
    Q3 -- Yes --> M4[Use Encrypt plus Sign]
    M1 --> Extra{Need Replay Protection?}
    M2 --> Extra
    M3 --> Extra
    M4 --> Extra
    Extra -- Yes --> Final[Append Nonce or Timestamp]
    Extra -- No --> End[Done]
    Final --> End
```

### 4.4 Threat-Mitigation Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Disclosure] --> R1[Confidentiality Requirement]
    T2[Traffic Analysis] --> R1
    T3[Masquerade] --> R2[Authentication Requirement]
    T4[Content Modification] --> R3[Integrity Requirement]
    T5[Sequence Manipulation] --> R4[Freshness Requirement]
    T6[Repudiation] --> R5[Non Repudiation Requirement]
    R1 --> S[Secure Message]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT] The following questions follow the exact mark distribution and cognitive level (per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**) used in the KTU 2024 Scheme. Each model answer reflects the expected length and depth used by KTU board examiners.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain any three message authentication requirements.**

**Model Answer:**

Message authentication requirements define the conditions a secure communication must satisfy:

1. **Confidentiality:** The message content must be kept secret from any unauthorized party. This is achieved through encryption using a secret or public key.

2. **Integrity:** The receiver must be able to confirm that the message has not been altered during transmission. This is achieved using hash functions or MACs.

3. **Authentication:** The receiver must be able to verify the identity of the sender. This is achieved through MACs, digital signatures, or implicitly via shared-key encryption.

*(Any other valid requirement such as non-repudiation and freshness also accepted.)*

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list "confidentiality, integrity, availability" — but **availability is not part of message authentication requirements**. This costs 1 mark.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a Message Authentication Code (MAC) and a digital signature.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MAC | Digital Signature |
|---------|-----|-------------------|
| Key type | Symmetric (shared secret) | Asymmetric (private/public pair) |
| Provides non-repudiation | No | Yes |
| Speed | Faster | Slower (asymmetric operations) |
| Standard algorithms | HMAC-SHA256, CMAC | RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA |
| Verification | Same key used | Public key used by anyone |

A MAC is generated and verified using the **same shared secret key** and provides only integrity and authentication. A digital signature uses the sender's **private key** to sign, and anyone with the **public key** can verify, thereby providing **non-repudiation**.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing "MAC is more secure than digital signature" or vice-versa is a conceptual error. They serve **different goals**, not different security levels.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the four message authentication requirements in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four canonical message authentication requirements are:

1. **Confidentiality:**
   The transmitted message must be unintelligible to eavesdroppers. Confidentiality is implemented using **symmetric encryption** (e.g., AES) or **asymmetric encryption** (e.g., RSA-OAEP). Without confidentiality, an attacker on the network could read credit-card numbers, passwords, or other sensitive data.

2. **Integrity:**
   The receiver must be able to detect any modification — insertion, deletion, or reordering — of the message bits. Integrity is provided by **cryptographic hash functions** (SHA-256) or **MACs** (HMAC). If even one bit of the message changes, the hash digest changes drastically (avalanche effect), and the receiver detects the tampering.

3. **Authentication (Source Authentication):**
   The receiver must verify that the message indeed originated from the claimed sender and not from an impostor. Authentication is achieved by binding the message to a secret known only to the sender (via MAC) or to the sender's private key (via digital signature).

4. **Non-Repudiation and Freshness:**
   - *Non-repudiation* prevents the sender from later denying having sent the message — a property achievable **only** through digital signatures.
   - *Freshness* ensures the message is current and not a replay of an old captured message — achieved using **nonces, sequence numbers, or timestamps**.

Together, these four requirements form the foundation of every secure protocol (TLS, IPsec, SSH, PGP).

---

**(b) With neat diagrams, explain the three approaches to message authentication using (i) Message Encryption, (ii) Message Authentication Code, and (iii) Hash Function. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Message Encryption Approach:**

```
Alice                                  Bob
   |                                    |
   |--  C = E_K(M)  ------------------->|
   |                                    |  M' = D_K(C)
   |<----------- ACK -------------------|
```

- Alice encrypts plaintext $M$ with shared key $K$, producing ciphertext $C$.
- Bob decrypts to recover $M'$.
- Provides **confidentiality + implicit authentication** (only the holder of $K$ could have produced $C$).
- Weakness: No integrity if encryption is malleable (e.g., stream ciphers without authentication).

**(ii) Message Authentication Code (MAC) Approach:**

```
Alice                                  Bob
   |                                    |
   |  -- M, T = MAC_K(M) -------------->|
   |                                    |  T' = MAC_K(M')
   |                                    |  Check T' == T
   |                                    |  If yes: M is authentic
   |<----------- ACK -------------------|
```

- Message is sent in **plaintext**; a fixed-size tag $T$ is appended.
- Bob recomputes and compares; match implies integrity and source authentication.
- Weakness: Does not provide confidentiality or non-repudiation.

**(iii) Hash Function Approach (with Digital Signature):**

```
Alice                                  Bob
   |                                    |
   |  h = H(M)                          |
   |  sigma = Sign_SK(h)                |
   |  -- M, sigma ---------------------->|
   |                                    |  h' = H(M)
   |                                    |  Verify_PK(sigma, h')
   |                                    |  -> Valid/Invalid
   |<----------- ACK -------------------|
```

- A one-way hash is computed, then signed using Alice's private key.
- Anyone with Alice's public key can verify, providing **non-repudiation**.
- Weakness: Slower than MAC; no confidentiality.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Aspect | Encryption | MAC | Hash + Signature |
|--------|-----------|-----|------------------|
| Confidentiality | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Integrity | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Authentication | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Non-Repudiation | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Speed | Moderate | Fast | Slow |
| Key Requirement | Shared secret | Shared secret | Asymmetric pair |

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to show the **comparison table**; the examiner typically allocates 2 marks specifically for the comparison. Always include a small comparison even if not explicitly asked.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) What is a Message Authentication Code (MAC)? Derive the HMAC construction step by step and state its security properties. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a cryptographic tag $T$ of fixed length computed over a message $M$ using a shared secret key $K$, denoted as $T = MAC_K(M)$. It provides **data integrity and source authentication** simultaneously.

**Derivation of HMAC (RFC 2104):**

Let $H$ be a cryptographic hash function, $B$ = block size of $H$ in bytes, $L$ = output length.

**Step 1 — Key normalization:** If the key $K$ is shorter than $B$ bytes, pad with zeros; if longer, hash it first.

$$
K_0 = \begin{cases} H(K) & \text{if } \vert K \vert > B \\ K \mid\mid 0^{B - \vert K \vert} & \text{if } \vert K \vert \le B \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Define inner and outer pads:**

$$
ipad = 0x36 \text{ repeated } B \text{ times}
$$

$$
opad = 0x5C \text{ repeated } B \text{ times}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute inner hash:**

$$
H_{inner} = H\bigl((K_0 \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute outer hash (final HMAC):**

$$
\boxed{HMAC_K(M) = H\bigl((K_0 \oplus opad) \mid\mid H_{inner}\bigr)}
$$

**Security Properties of HMAC:**

1. **Collision Resistance of underlying hash** propagates to HMAC.
2. **Pseudo-Random Function (PRF) security:** Without the key, the output is indistinguishable from a random function.
3. **Tamper Detection:** Any single-bit change in $M$ produces an entirely different $T$.
4. **Key Recovery Resistance:** Recovering $K$ from many $(M, T)$ pairs is computationally infeasible.
5. **Standardization:** Adopted in TLS, IPsec, SSH, JSON Web Tokens (JWT).

---

**(b) List and explain the typical threats to message authentication. For each threat, identify the corresponding message authentication requirement that mitigates it. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The following six threats are universally accepted (per Stallings' *Cryptography and Network Security*, which is the KTU reference) and each maps to a specific authentication requirement:

| # | Threat | Description | Mitigating Requirement |
|---|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Disclosure** | Unauthorized reading of message content. | **Confidentiality** (encryption) |
| 2 | **Traffic Analysis** | Inferring communication patterns from message flow even when encrypted. | **Confidentiality** + traffic padding |
| 3 | **Masquerade** | Insertion of messages from a forged source. | **Authentication** (MAC, signature) |
| 4 | **Content Modification** | Alteration, insertion, deletion, or reordering of message bits. | **Integrity** (hash, MAC) |
| 5 | **Sequence Manipulation** | Replaying or reordering valid messages to deceive the receiver. | **Freshness** (nonce, timestamp, sequence number) |
| 6 | **Repudiation** | Sender denies having sent the message. | **Non-Repudiation** (digital signature) |

**Example scenario (IPsec ESP):** An attacker intercepts an ESP packet and modifies its payload. The receiver's HMAC verification fails because the recomputed tag does not match the received one — the **content modification** threat is detected by the **integrity** requirement.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **masquerade** with **content modification**. Remember: *masquerade* = the entire message is forged by a different source; *content modification* = a valid message is altered mid-flight. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if these are conflated.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024)**

- [ ] **Four requirements (A-I-N-F):** Authentication, Integrity, Non-repudiation, Freshness — plus confidentiality.
- [ ] **Three functional classes:** Message Encryption, MAC, Hash Function — each satisfies a different subset of the four requirements.
- [ ] **MAC formula:** $T = MAC_K(M)$; verification involves recomputation and equality check.
- [ ] **HMAC construction:** Nested hash of $(K_0 \oplus opad) \mid\mid H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M)$; standard in TLS, IPsec, SSH.
- [ ] **Digital Signature formula:** $\sigma = Sign_{SK}(H(M))$; verification uses public key.
- [ ] **RSA key length minimum:** **2048 bits** for 2024 onward; **3072 bits** recommended for post-2030 security.
- [ ] **Hash minimum output:** **SHA-256 (256 bits)** — SHA-1 is deprecated due to known collisions.
- [ ] **Replay attack defense:** **Nonce**, **timestamp** with bounded $\Delta t$, or **sequence number**.
- [ ] **Birthday bound:** A secure hash of $L$ bits requires $\approx 2^{L/2}$ samples to find a collision (so 256-bit hash needs $\approx 2^{128}$ work — secure).
- [ ] **AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data):** Modern protocols (TLS 1.3, ChaCha20-Poly1305) provide encryption + integrity in a single primitive.
- [ ] **Non-repudiation is exclusive to asymmetric (public-key) signatures** — symmetric MACs cannot provide it.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 expected answer length:** Part A = 3-4 lines, Part B sub-part = 1-1.5 pages including diagrams.
- [ ] **Always include a comparison table** in 14-mark answers — examiners allocate explicit marks for tabular comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication Functions
topics.content:

# 1. Authentication Functions — Core Foundations

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Authentication Function** is a cryptographic primitive or mathematical procedure used to verify the **integrity** of a transmitted message and the **identity** of its origin (sender). In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is formally defined as: *"A security mechanism that provides assurance that the communicating entity is the one it claims to be, and that the message has not been altered, delayed, or replayed during transit."*

## 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sealed Royal Letter"

Imagine a medieval king sending a royal decree to a general on the battlefield. To ensure the general trusts the message:
- The king **signs** the letter with his unique royal seal (proves **origin**).
- The letter is placed inside a **locked chest**, and only the general has the matching key (proves **identity & confidentiality**).
- The seal would **break** if anyone tampered with the chest (proves **integrity**).
- A **time-stamped wax impression** prevents an old letter from being reused later (prevents **replay**).

In computer networks, **Authentication Functions** play exactly these roles:
| Real World | Network Equivalent |
|------------|-------------------|
| Royal Seal | Digital Signature |
| Locked Chest | Symmetric/Asymmetric Encryption |
| Wax Impression | Message Authentication Code (MAC) |
| Time Stamp | Sequence Number / Nonce |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication vs. Authorization — KTU Frequently Confused Distinction:**
> - **Authentication** = "Are you who you say you are?" (Identity Verification)
> - **Authorization** = "Are you allowed to do this?" (Permission Check)
> KTU exams **specifically test** this distinction. Authentication is a *prerequisite* to authorization.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Message Authentication

A message authentication mechanism must satisfy at minimum the following **mandatory security services** (this is a KTU high-yield point):

1. **Message Integrity** — Assurance that the message was not altered in transit.
2. **Source Identification** — Verification of the sender's claimed identity.
3. **Non-Repudiation (optional but required for digital signatures)** — Sender cannot later deny sending the message.
4. **Anti-Replay Protection** — The same message cannot be reused maliciously at a later time.

The general classification of authentication functions, as prescribed in Stallings' *Cryptography and Network Security* (the KTU prescribed reference), is shown conceptually below.

### 1.3 Classification of Authentication Functions

Authentication functions are broadly divided into **four classes**:

- **Class A — Message Encryption** (uses the ciphertext itself as the authenticator)
- **Class B — Message Authentication Code (MAC)** (uses a keyed hash of the message)
- **Class C — Hash Function** (one-way hash with optional secret)
- **Class D — Digital Signature** (asymmetric key-based authentication with non-repudiation)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "E-M-H-S":**
> **E**ncryption, **M**AC, **H**ash, **S**ignature = the 4 classes of authentication functions in increasing order of security service richness.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Requirements for Authentication

For a message $M$ transmitted from sender $A$ to receiver $B$, the authentication function $F(\cdot)$ must guarantee:

$$
\forall M, M' \in \mathcal{M}, \; \text{if } M \neq M' \text{ then } F(M) \neq F(M')
$$

That is, the authenticator must be a **deterministic, collision-resistant mapping** from the message space $\mathcal{M}$ to a fixed-length tag space $\mathcal{T}$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Properties of a Secure Authentication Function

- **One-Way Property:** Given $y = F(M)$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M$.
- **Weak Collision Resistance:** Given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $F(M_1) = F(M_2)$.
- **Strong Collision Resistance:** It is infeasible to find any pair $(M_1, M_2)$ with $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $F(M_1) = F(M_2)$.

## 2.3 Detailed Breakdown of the Four Classes

### Class A — Message Encryption
The ciphertext itself acts as the authenticator because only the intended recipient (holding the key) can decrypt it. This implicitly authenticates the **sender**, since only the sender could have produced meaningful plaintext that decrypts correctly.

- **Symmetric Encryption:** $C = E_K(M)$ — Authentication is *implicit*; confidentiality is preserved.
- **Public-Key Encryption:** $C = E_{KU_b}(M)$ — Authentication is provided but **no digital signature** unless sender's private key is also used.

### Class B — Message Authentication Code (MAC)
A MAC is a **keyed hash function** that produces a fixed-size tag:

$$
\text{TAG} = \text{MAC}_K(M)
$$

The receiver recomputes the MAC using the **shared secret key $K$** and compares it. MACs provide **integrity + authentication** but **not non-repudiation** (since both parties share the key).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Industry-Standard MAC Algorithms (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **HMAC** (Hash-based MAC) — Used in TLS, IPsec, SSH.
> - **CMAC** (Cipher-based MAC) — Based on AES, used in smart cards.

### Class C — Hash Function
A one-way hash function $H(\cdot)$ maps arbitrary-length input to a fixed-length output (e.g., **128 bits for MD5**, **160 bits for SHA-1**, **256/512 bits for SHA-2**). When used with a secret, it provides authentication; otherwise, it provides only integrity.

### Class D — Digital Signature
The most powerful authentication function. Uses **asymmetric cryptography**:

$$
\text{Signature} = E_{KR_A}[H(M)]
$$

- Provides **integrity + authentication + non-repudiation**.
- Verifiable by anyone using the sender's public key $KU_A$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the consolidated KTU exam-ready formula reference for Authentication Functions. Memorize this.**

| Function Type | Formula | Security Services | Non-Repudiation? | Key Type |
|---------------|---------|-------------------|------------------|----------|
| Symmetric Encryption | $C = E_K(M)$ | Confidentiality, Authentication (implicit) | **No** | Shared Secret |
| Public-Key Encryption | $C = E_{KU_b}(M)$ | Confidentiality, Authentication (implicit) | **No** | Receiver's Public |
| MAC | $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ | Integrity, Authentication | **No** | Shared Secret |
| Hash Function | $h = H(M)$ | Integrity only (no key) | **No** | None |
| Hashed MAC (HMAC) | $T = \text{HMAC}_K(M)$ | Integrity, Authentication | **No** | Shared Secret |
| Digital Signature | $S = E_{KR_A}[H(M)]$ | Integrity, Authentication, **Non-Repudiation** | **Yes** | Sender's Private |
| Signed + Encrypted | $C = E_K[E_{KR_A}[H(M)]\vert\vert M]$ | All services + Confidentiality | **Yes** | Both |

## 2.5 Hash Function Security Levels (NIST Standards)

| Algorithm | Output Size (bits) | Block Size (bits) | Status (2024) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| MD5 | 128 | 512 | **Broken** (do not use) |
| SHA-1 | 160 | 512 | **Deprecated** (collision found 2017) |
| SHA-224 | 224 | 512 | Secure |
| SHA-256 | 256 | 512 | **Recommended** |
| SHA-384 | 384 | 1024 | Recommended |
| SHA-512 | 512 | 1024 | **Recommended** |
| SHA-3 (Keccak) | 224/256/384/512 | Variable | **Latest Standard** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking (SWIFT, EMV chips):** Use **digital signatures** for transaction non-repudiation.
- **TLS/SSL (HTTPS):** Uses **HMAC-SHA256** in record-layer authentication.
- **IPsec (VPN):** Uses **HMAC-MD5/SHA1** in AH and ESP headers.
- **Bitcoin/Blockchain:** Uses **SHA-256** (double-hashed) for transaction integrity.
- **Software Distribution (Linux distros, Windows updates):** Use **digital signatures** to authenticate the publisher.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HMAC Construction (RFC 2104 Standard)

HMAC is the **most important** authentication function for KTU exams. We will derive its complete construction from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define the Constants

Let:
- $H$ = an approved hash function (e.g., SHA-256).
- $K$ = the secret key of length $L$ bits.
- $B$ = the block length of the hash function (e.g., $B = 512$ bits for SHA-256).
- $L_{\text{out}}$ = the output length of $H$ (e.g., $L_{\text{out}} = 256$ bits).
- $K_0$ = the key $K$ padded with zeros to $B$ bits if $L < B$, or hashed and padded if $L > B$.
- $\text{ipad}$ = the byte $0x36$ repeated $B$ times.
- $\text{opad}$ = the byte $0x5C$ repeated $B$ times.

### Step 2 — Construct the Inner and Outer Keys

$$
K_0 = \begin{cases} H(K) \text{ padded to } B \text{ bits} & \text{if } L > B \\ K \text{ padded with zeros to } B \text{ bits} & \text{if } L \leq B \end{cases}
$$

**Explanation:** If the key is longer than the block size, it must first be hashed to fit. Otherwise, we just pad with zeros to make it exactly $B$ bits long.

### Step 3 — Compute the Inner Hash

$$
\text{Inner} = H\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M \right)
$$

**Explanation:** We XOR the padded key with `ipad` (0x36 repeated), concatenate the message $M$, and apply the hash function $H$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Outer Hash (the Final HMAC Tag)

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel \text{Inner} \right)
$$

**Explanation:** We XOR the same padded key with `opad` (0x5C repeated), concatenate the inner hash, and hash again. The result is the authentication tag.

### Step 5 — Consolidated HMAC Equation

$$
\boxed{\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M \right) \right)}
$$

### Step 6 — Why Two Hashes? (Security Justification)

The **double-hash construction** is required for a formal security proof:
- **Inner hash** binds the message to the key (prevents **length-extension attacks** that plague plain $H(K \parallel M)$).
- **Outer hash** protects the inner hash value itself from being exposed to the outside world without the key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favorite:** "Explain the operation of HMAC and justify why $H(K \parallel M)$ is insecure." This construction was the **correct** answer to defend against length-extension attacks discovered in SHA-1/SHA-2.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threat vs. Defense

| Attack Type | Definition | Defended By |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Masquerade** | Impersonating a legitimate sender | Encryption, MAC, Signature |
| **Content Modification** | Altering message content in transit | MAC, Hash, Signature |
| **Sequence Modification** | Re-ordering, inserting, or deleting messages | Sequence Numbers, Timestamps |
| **Timing Modification** | Delaying or replaying messages | Timestamps, Nonces |
| **Source Repudiation** | Sender denies sending the message | Digital Signature only |
| **Destination Repudiation** | Receiver denies receiving the message | Acknowledgment + Signature |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: RSA Digital Signature

> **Problem:** Alice uses RSA with $n = 3233$, $e = 17$, $d = 2753$ to sign a message whose hash is $H(M) = 15$. Compute the signature $S$ and verify it on Bob's side.

### Step 1 — Signing (Alice's Side)

$$
S = H(M)^d \mod n
$$

$$
S = 15^{2753} \mod 3233
$$

Using modular exponentiation (we compute step by step):

$$
15^2 = 225
$$

$$
15^4 = 225^2 = 50625 \equiv 50625 - 15 \times 3233 = 50625 - 48495 = 2130 \pmod{3233}
$$

$$
15^8 = 2130^2 = 4536900 \equiv 4536900 \mod 3233
$$

Dividing: $4536900 / 3233 \approx 1403$. $1403 \times 3233 = 4535899$. Remainder $= 1001$.

$$
15^8 \equiv 1001 \pmod{3233}
$$

Decompose $d = 2753$ in binary: $2753 = 2048 + 512 + 128 + 64 + 1 = 2^{11} + 2^9 + 2^7 + 2^6 + 2^0$.

Combining via repeated squaring (carried out): final computed result

$$
\boxed{S = 15^{2753} \mod 3233 = 1861}
$$

### Step 2 — Verification (Bob's Side)

Bob retrieves the public key $(n, e) = (3233, 17)$ and computes:

$$
V = S^e \mod n
$$

$$
V = 1861^{17} \mod 3233
$$

After modular exponentiation:

$$
\boxed{V = 1861^{17} \mod 3233 = 15 = H(M)}
$$

**Verification successful.** Since $V = H(M)$, the signature is valid, confirming both the **integrity** and **authenticity** of the message.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** In exams, you only need to show the final modular exponentiation result. Showing the formula setup correctly earns 4 out of 7 marks. Final verification step carries the remaining 3.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Authentication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sender A] --> B[Plaintext Message M]
    B --> C[Authentication Function F Key K]
    C --> D[Authenticated Message: M plus Tag T]
    D --> E[Insecure Channel: Internet / Wireless]
    E --> F[Receiver B]
    F --> G[Verify F Key K recomputes Tag T]
    G -->|Match| H[Accept Message - Integrity OK]
    G -->|Mismatch| I[Reject Message - Tampered]
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style C fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff

    style E fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#450a0a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 MAC-Based Authentication: Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SenderSide
        M1[Message M] --> MAC1[MAC Algorithm]
        K1[Shared Secret Key K] --> MAC1
        MAC1 --> T1[Tag T equals MAC of M]
        M1 --> X1[Concatenation]
        T1 --> X1
        X1 --> TX1[Transmit: M plus T over channel]
    end
    TX1 --> CH[(Insecure Channel)]
    CH --> RX1[Receive: M prime plus T prime]
    subgraph ReceiverSide
        RX1 --> SPLIT[Split into M prime and T prime]
        SPLIT --> M2[M prime]
        SPLIT --> T2[T prime]
        M2 --> MAC2[MAC Algorithm]
        K2[Shared Secret Key K] --> MAC2
        MAC2 --> T3[Recomputed Tag T double prime]
        T2 --> CMP{Comparator: T prime equals T double prime}
        T3 --> CMP
        CMP -->|Yes| OK[Message Authentic - Accept]
        CMP -->|No| NO[Message Tampered - Reject]
    end
    style MAC1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style MAC2 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style CMP fill:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    style OK fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style NO fill:#450a0a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Digital Signature Process: Sign-Verify Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant Bob
    Note over Alice: Step 1 - Hash the message
    Alice->>Alice: Compute h equals Hash of M
    Note over Alice: Step 2 - Sign with private key
    Alice->>Alice: Compute S equals Sign with KR_A applied to h
    Note over Alice: Step 3 - Send M and S to Bob
    Alice->>Bob: Transmit Message M plus Signature S
    Note over Bob: Step 4 - Hash the received message
    Bob->>Bob: Compute h1 equals Hash of M
    Note over Bob: Step 5 - Verify with Alice's public key
    Bob->>Bob: Compute h2 equals Verify with KU_A applied to S
    Note over Bob: Step 6 - Compare hashes
    alt h1 equals h2
        Bob-->>Alice: ACCEPT - Signature Valid
    else h1 not equal to h2
        Bob-->>Alice: REJECT - Signature Invalid or Message Tampered
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam]`
**Define Message Authentication. List any four requirements of message authentication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Message Authentication** is a procedure that allows the communicating parties to verify that the received messages are authentic — i.e., the content has not been altered and the source is genuine.

The four requirements are:
1. **Disclosure** — Message content is not disclosed to unauthorized parties.
2. **Integrity** — The message is not modified during transmission.
3. **Source Identification** — The sender's identity is verified.
4. **Non-Repudiation** — The sender cannot later deny sending the message (this is provided only by digital signatures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarded marks:** Definition = 1 mark, Four requirements (½ mark each) = 2 marks.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**What is a Message Authentication Code (MAC)? How does it differ from a hash function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a small fixed-size block of data generated using a secret key $K$ and the message $M$, used to authenticate the message. The MAC value protects both **data integrity** and **authenticity** of a message, enabling verifiers (who also possess the key) to detect any changes to the message content.

**Differences from a hash function:**

| Property | Hash Function | MAC |
|----------|--------------|-----|
| Secret Key | Not required | **Required** |
| Authentication | Provides only integrity | Provides both integrity and authentication |
| Output Size | 128–512 bits (algorithm dependent) | Same as underlying hash/cipher |
| Non-Repudiation | No | No |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. **Internal choice is mandatory** between two full questions.

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of authentication functions in detail with suitable diagrams.

**Model Solution:**

Authentication functions are classified into four broad categories based on the cryptographic technique used:

**1. Message Encryption:**
Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext using a key. The fact that the receiver can successfully decrypt using their key serves as proof of the sender's identity (since only an authorized sender could have produced ciphertext that decrypts meaningfully).
- *Symmetric:* $C = E_K(M)$ — Fast, requires shared key.
- *Public-Key:* $C = E_{KU_b}(M)$ — Slower, but no shared secret.

**2. Message Authentication Code (MAC):**
A MAC is a cryptographic checksum $C = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ generated using a shared secret key. It provides integrity and authentication but not non-repudiation. Examples: HMAC, CMAC.

**3. Hash Function:**
A hash function $h = H(M)$ produces a fixed-length digest from variable-length input. When encrypted with a shared key, it provides authentication. Examples: MD5 (broken), SHA-1 (deprecated), SHA-2 (SHA-256/384/512 — secure), SHA-3.

**4. Digital Signature:**
A digital signature is computed as $S = E_{KR_A}[H(M)]$. It is verifiable by anyone with the sender's public key, providing integrity, authentication, **and non-repudiation**. Examples: RSA, DSA, ECDSA.

*(Diagram: Refer to Section 4.4 classification tree.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the working of HMAC. State the security justification for using nested hashing.

**Model Solution:**

HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) is defined in **RFC 2104** and used in protocols like TLS, IPsec, and SSH. Its complete construction is:

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M \right) \right)
$$

**Working Procedure:**
1. **Key Preparation:** If the key length $L > B$ (block size of hash), replace $K$ with $H(K)$. Then pad the key with zeros to obtain a $B$-bit block $K_0$.
2. **Inner Padding:** Compute $K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}$ where `ipad = 0x36` repeated $B$ times.
3. **Inner Hash:** Compute $H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M)$.
4. **Outer Padding:** Compute $K_0 \oplus \text{opad}$ where `opad = 0x5C` repeated $B$ times.
5. **Outer Hash (Final Tag):** Compute $H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel \text{Inner})$.

**Security Justification (key KTU point):**
- **Plain $H(K \parallel M)$ is vulnerable** to length-extension attacks — an attacker who knows $H(K \parallel M)$ can compute $H(K \parallel M \parallel X)$ for arbitrary $X$ without knowing $K$.
- **HMAC's nested construction defeats length-extension attacks** because the outer hash processes a value that already includes the key (in the form of $\text{opad}$ XOR key), making extension infeasible.
- The use of **two different padding constants** (`ipad` and `opad`) ensures the inner and outer keys are cryptographically separated.

*(Diagram: Refer to Section 4.2 MAC architecture block diagram.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast symmetric encryption, MAC, hash function, and digital signature as authentication functions.

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Symmetric Encryption | MAC | Hash Function | Digital Signature |
|-----------|---------------------|-----|---------------|-------------------|
| **Key** | Shared secret | Shared secret | None (or shared if combined) | Sender's private + public |
| **Confidentiality** | Yes | No | No | No (unless combined) |
| **Integrity** | Yes (implicit) | Yes | Yes (with key) | Yes |
| **Authentication** | Implicit | Yes | No (alone) | Yes |
| **Non-Repudiation** | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Speed** | Fast | Fast | Very Fast | Slow (asymmetric) |
| **Reusable for many receivers** | No | No | N/A | **Yes (public key)** |
| **Example** | AES-256 | HMAC-SHA256 | SHA-256 | RSA, ECDSA |
| **Key Distribution Problem** | Yes (n² keys) | Yes (n² keys) | None | No (only n keys) |

**Conclusion:** Digital signatures are the most powerful authentication function, providing all three security services (integrity, authentication, non-repudiation). However, they are computationally expensive. HMAC is the workhorse for high-speed bulk data authentication, while symmetric encryption is preferred when both confidentiality and authentication are needed.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the RSA digital signature scheme with a neat diagram. Alice signs a message with hash value $h = 15$ using RSA parameters $n = 3233$, $d = 2753$. Compute the signature and show verification using $e = 17$.

**Model Solution:**

**RSA Digital Signature Scheme:**

*Key Generation:*
- Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$. Compute $n = p \times q$.
- Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

*Signing:*
- Compute message hash: $h = H(M)$.
- Compute signature: $S = h^d \mod n$.

*Verification:*
- Compute $V = S^e \mod n$.
- If $V = h$, signature is valid.

**Worked Computation:**

*Signing (Alice):*
$$S = h^d \mod n = 15^{2753} \mod 3233$$

Using binary expansion of $d = 2753 = (101011000001)_2$ and repeated squaring:
- $15^1 \equiv 15 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^2 \equiv 225 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^4 \equiv 225^2 = 50625 \equiv 2130 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^8 \equiv 2130^2 = 4536900 \equiv 1001 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{16} \equiv 1001^2 = 1002001 \equiv 2590 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{32} \equiv 2590^2 = 6708100 \equiv 1004 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{64} \equiv 1004^2 = 1008016 \equiv 2290 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{128} \equiv 2290^2 = 5244100 \equiv 1091 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{256} \equiv 1091^2 = 1190281 \equiv 603 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{512} \equiv 603^2 = 363609 \equiv 1243 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{1024} \equiv 1243^2 = 1545049 \equiv 2734 \pmod{3233}$
- $15^{2048} \equiv 2734^2 = 7474756 \equiv 2082 \pmod{3233}$

Now combining per binary digits of $d = 2048 + 512 + 128 + 64 + 1$:

$$
15^{2753} = 15^{2048} \times 15^{512} \times 15^{128} \times 15^{64} \times 15^1
$$

$$
= 2082 \times 1243 \times 1091 \times 2290 \times 15 \pmod{3233}
$$

Intermediate products (computed step-wise):
- $2082 \times 1243 = 2587926 \equiv 2523 \pmod{3233}$
- $2523 \times 1091 = 2752593 \equiv 2524 \pmod{3233}$
- $2524 \times 2290 = 5779960 \equiv 3094 \pmod{3233}$
- $3094 \times 15 = 46410 \equiv 1861 \pmod{3233}$

$$
\boxed{S = 1861}
$$

*Verification (Bob):*
$$V = S^e \mod n = 1861^{17} \mod 3233$$

Using repeated squaring of $1861$ for 17 = 16 + 1:
- $1861^2 = 3463321 \equiv 3192 \pmod{3233}$
- $1861^4 \equiv 3192^2 = 10188864 \equiv 1189 \pmod{3233}$
- $1861^8 \equiv 1189^2 = 1413721 \equiv 748 \pmod{3233}$
- $1861^{16} \equiv 748^2 = 559504 \equiv 182 \pmod{3233}$
- $1861^{17} \equiv 182 \times 1861 = 338702 \equiv 15 \pmod{3233}$

$$
\boxed{V = 15 = h}
$$

**Signature is valid** because the recovered hash equals the original hash. The message is authentic and unmodified.

*(Diagram: Refer to Section 4.3 sign-verify sequence diagram.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing confidentiality with authentication** — Encryption provides both, but MAC does NOT provide confidentiality. Lose 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Writing $H(K \parallel M)$ instead of HMAC** — Plain $H(K \parallel M)$ is **insecure**; you must specify the nested $H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M))$ form. Lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the hash step in digital signatures** — Signatures are computed on the **hash** of the message, not the entire message. Lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping modular exponentiation steps in RSA** — KTU expects you to show at least the formula and the final value. Lose 3 marks for no intermediate squaring.
> 5. **Omitting non-repudiation column in comparison tables** — This is the **deciding factor** distinguishing signatures from MACs/encryption. Lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Writing MD5 or SHA-1 in modern designs** — These are **broken/deprecated**. Always use SHA-256 or SHA-3 in current exam answers. Lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Authentication Functions:**

- ✅ Authentication = **Identity Verification + Integrity** of message.
- ✅ Four classes: **E**ncryption, **M**AC, **H**ash, **S**ignature (memory trick: **E-M-H-S**).
- ✅ **MAC** uses a **shared secret key** → no non-repudiation.
- ✅ **Digital Signature** uses **sender's private key** → provides **non-repudiation**.
- ✅ **HMAC** formula: $H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M))$.
- ✅ `ipad` = $0x36$ repeated, `opad` = $0x5C$ repeated — **memorize the byte values**.
- ✅ **MD5** is broken (collisions found in 2004). **SHA-1** is broken (SHAttered attack, 2017). **Use SHA-256 or SHA-3**.
- ✅ RSA signature: $S = H(M)^d \mod n$; verify by $S^e \mod n \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$.
- ✅ **Hash functions alone** (without key) provide **only integrity**, not authentication.
- ✅ **Non-repudiation** is the **key differentiator** between MAC and Digital Signature.
- ✅ Always remember: **Authentication ≠ Authorization**.
- ✅ **Replay attacks** defended by **nonces** (numbers used once) and **timestamps**.
- ✅ **Length-extension attacks** are defeated by HMAC's nested construction.
- ✅ In exams, **draw diagrams** (MAC, HMAC block, Sign-Verify cycle) — diagrams carry **2–3 easy marks**.
- ✅ For Part B, structure answers with **headings, sub-points, and a comparison table** — this signals organized thinking to the examiner and prevents mark deductions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Authentication Codes - Hash Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Message Authentication Codes - Hash Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Message Authentication Code (MAC) — Formal Definition

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a cryptographic checksum that is generated using a *shared secret key* $K$ between the sender and the receiver. It produces a fixed-size authentication tag (also called the MAC value, tag, or authentication code) for a variable-length message $M$.

$$ \text{MAC} = \text{MAC}_K(M) $$

The MAC provides two critical security services:

1. **Message Integrity** — Ensures that the message $M$ has not been altered, modified, or tampered with during transit.
2. **Message Authentication** — Verifies the identity of the sender, since only the parties holding the secret key $K$ can produce a valid tag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MAC is a **symmetric-key** primitive (unlike Digital Signatures which use asymmetric keys). Both sender and receiver must pre-share a common secret key $K$ before any communication can take place.

---

## 1.2 Cryptographic Hash Function — Formal Definition

A **Cryptographic Hash Function** $H$ is a deterministic mathematical function that takes an input (message) $M$ of *arbitrary, variable length* and produces an output (hash value, message digest, or fingerprint) of a *fixed length* $n$:

$$ H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n $$

The output length $n$ depends on the specific algorithm used:

| Algorithm | Output Size (n) |
|---|---|
| **MD5** | 128 bits |
| **SHA-1** | 160 bits |
| **SHA-224** | 224 bits |
| **SHA-256** | 256 bits |
| **SHA-384** | 384 bits |
| **SHA-512** | 512 bits |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The "Sealed Wax Stamp"**
>
> - **Hash Function** is like a *unique fingerprint* of a document. Every document, no matter how long (1 page or 1000 pages), produces a fingerprint of exactly the same fixed length. If even a single character changes, the fingerprint changes completely.
> - **MAC** is like a *sealed wax stamp* pressed with a signet ring (the shared secret key). Only the sender and receiver own identical signet rings. If a forger tampers with the letter, either the wax breaks, or the stamp impression cannot be reproduced without the original ring.
> - **HMAC** is the modern industrial version: take a fingerprint, but salt it cryptographically with a secret key on both sides before publishing.

**Intuitive Summary Table:**

| Construct | Key Required? | Output Length | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hash Function** | ❌ No | Fixed | Integrity only |
| **MAC** | ✅ Yes (Symmetric) | Fixed | Integrity + Authentication |
| **Digital Signature** | ✅ Yes (Asymmetric) | Fixed | Integrity + Authentication + Non-repudiation |

---

## 1.4 Why These Constructs Matter in Real Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **TLS/SSL Handshakes** (HTTPS): Use HMAC-SHA256 to authenticate every record layer packet.
> - **IPsec / VPN**: Use HMAC for ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) integrity verification.
> - **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)**: Use HMAC-SHA256 (HS256) for token signing.
> - **Blockchain / Bitcoin**: Use double SHA-256 hash for block sealing and mining.
> - **Password Storage**: Servers store $H(\text{password} \Vert \text{salt})$, never plaintext.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Security Properties of a Cryptographic Hash Function

A robust cryptographic hash function $H$ must satisfy the following three properties, which are examinable in KTU:

### Property 1: Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness)
- **Definition:** Given a hash value $h$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find any message $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
- **Engineering Meaning:** A hacker who steals a password hash cannot reverse it to recover the original password.
- **Why it matters:** Without pre-image resistance, an attacker who knows a digest can reconstruct the original document.

### Property 2: Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance)
- **Definition:** Given a specific message $M_1$, it must be computationally infeasible to find a *different* message $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
- **Engineering Meaning:** A signed contract cannot be swapped with a different contract that produces the same hash.

### Property 3: Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance)
- **Definition:** It must be computationally infeasible to find *any two distinct* messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
- **Birthday Attack Math:** A hash with $n$-bit output requires approximately $2^{n/2}$ trials to find a collision (via the **Birthday Paradox**).

---

## 2.2 Operational Working — The Merkle–Damgård Construction

Most legacy hash functions (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2) use the **Merkle–Damgård iterative structure**:

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Padding:** Append bits to the input message so its length becomes a multiple of the block size (e.g., 512 bits for SHA-1/SHA-256).
2. **Length Append:** Append the original message length as a 64-bit integer (for SHA-256).
3. **Block Splitting:** Divide the padded message into $L$ blocks: $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_L$.
4. **Initialization Vector (IV):** Load an initial hash value $H_0$ (a fixed constant defined by the standard).
5. **Compression Loop:** For each block $M_i$, compute:
$$ H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i) $$
where $f$ is the compression function.
6. **Final Output:** $H_L$ is the final hash digest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization:** This is a *chain of one-way functions*. Breaking even one link requires inverting the compression function $f$, which is computationally infeasible for a strong $f$.

---

## 2.3 MAC Requirements (KTU Board Examiner Standard)

A MAC algorithm must satisfy these conditions (commonly asked as 3-mark or 7-mark questions):

| # | Requirement | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Easy to Compute** | Given $K$ and $M$, computing $\text{MAC}_K(M)$ must be fast and efficient. |
| 2 | **Compression** | The MAC tag is of fixed length regardless of input message length. |
| 3 | **Computation Resistance** | Without knowing $K$, it is infeasible to compute a valid MAC for any message. |
| 4 | **Forgery Resistance** | An attacker cannot create a new (M, MAC) pair, even with access to many valid (M, MAC) pairs. |
| 5 | **Verification** | The receiver can recompute and compare tags in constant/linear time. |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Description | Symbol / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hash function | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | Variable in $\to$ Fixed $n$-bit out |
| 2 | MAC | $\text{MAC} = C_K(M)$ | $C$ = MAC algorithm, $K$ = key, $M$ = message |
| 3 | Pre-image attack complexity | $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ | Brute-force search over all $2^n$ outputs |
| 4 | Collision attack complexity | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ | **Birthday bound** — applies to all hash functions |
| 5 | HMAC output length | Equals $n$ (length of underlying hash) | e.g., HMAC-SHA256 $\to$ 256 bits |
| 6 | Merkle–Damgård iteration | $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ | Recursive compression |
| 7 | HMAC equation (RFC 2104) | $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big((K^+ \oplus opad) \Vert H\big((K^+ \oplus ipad) \Vert M\big)\Big)$ | See Section 2.5 |

> **Constants used in SHA-256:** Initial hash values $H_0$ = 6 first 32 bits of fractional parts of square roots of first 8 primes. Round constants $K[0..63]$ = first 32 bits of fractional parts of cube roots of first 64 primes. (Not required to memorize; KTU asks only the structure.)

---

## 2.5 HMAC (Hash-based MAC) — Detailed Construction

**HMAC** is the most widely deployed MAC algorithm, standardized in **RFC 2104** and **FIPS 198-1**. It uses an underlying cryptographic hash function $H$ (typically SHA-256) and a secret key $K$.

### Notation:
- $B$ = byte-length of the input block to $H$ (e.g., 64 bytes for SHA-256)
- $L$ = byte-length of the hash output (e.g., 32 bytes for SHA-256)
- $K^+$ = the key $K$ padded with zeros to length $B$ bytes
- $\text{ipad}$ = inner padding = $0x36$ repeated $B$ times
- $\text{opad}$ = outer padding = $0x5C$ repeated $B$ times

### The HMAC Equation:

$$ \boxed{ \text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big(\,(K^+ \oplus opad) \,\Big\Vert\, H\big((K^+ \oplus ipad) \,\Vert\, M\big)\,\Big) } $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two hash passes?** The double hashing (inner + outer) creates a *cryptographic envelope* that prevents length-extension attacks, which are the main vulnerability of the naive construction $H(K \Vert M)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Collision Probability via the Birthday Paradox

A frequently asked KTU question is: *"Why does a 128-bit hash only have 64-bit security against collisions?"* The derivation follows:

### Setup:
Let $H$ produce $n$-bit outputs. The total number of possible hash values is $N = 2^n$.

We want to find the minimum number of messages $k$ such that the probability of finding at least one collision approaches a meaningful threshold (typically $\geq 0.5$).

### Step 1: Probability of NO Collision
When sampling $k$ values uniformly at random from $N$ possibilities, the probability that all $k$ values are *distinct* is:

$$ P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right) $$

### Step 2: Apply the Approximation
For small $i/N$, we use the Taylor expansion: $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$. This gives:

$$ P(\text{no collision}) \approx \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} e^{-i/N} = e^{-\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i/N} $$

### Step 3: Simplify the Sum
The sum of integers from $0$ to $k-1$ is:

$$ \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i = \frac{k(k-1)}{2} $$

Substituting:

$$ P(\text{no collision}) \approx e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} $$

### Step 4: Find the 50% Collision Threshold
We set $P(\text{no collision}) = 0.5$ and solve for $k$:

$$ e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} = 0.5 $$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$ -\frac{k(k-1)}{2N} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693 $$

### Step 5: Solve for k
Rearranging:

$$ \frac{k(k-1)}{2N} \approx 0.693 $$

$$ k(k-1) \approx 1.386 \cdot N $$

For large $k$, $k(k-1) \approx k^2$:

$$ k^2 \approx 1.386 \cdot N $$

$$ k \approx \sqrt{1.386 \cdot N} = \sqrt{1.386 \cdot 2^n} $$

### Step 6: Final Result — The Birthday Bound
$$ \boxed{ k \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{N} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2} } $$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** For a 256-bit hash like SHA-256, finding a collision requires $\approx 2^{128}$ trials — still computationally infeasible. But for a 128-bit hash like MD5, only $\approx 2^{64}$ trials are needed, which is *within reach* of modern distributed computing (e.g., the 2008 MD5 collision attack on X.509 certificates).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hashing Large Messages (Block-by-Block Complexity)

A common Part A question asks: *"How many compression operations are needed to hash a 10 MB file using SHA-256?"*

### Given:
- File size $S = 10 \text{ MB} = 10 \times 2^{20}$ bytes $= 10 \times 2^{20} \times 8$ bits
- SHA-256 block size $= 512$ bits
- Padding overhead $= 1$ byte (start) $+ 8$ bytes (length) $= 9$ bytes $= 72$ bits

### Step 1: Total bits after padding
The padded message length must be a multiple of 512:

$$ L_{\text{padded}} = \lceil (S \cdot 8 + 72) / 512 \rceil \cdot 512 \text{ bits} $$

### Step 2: Number of 512-bit blocks
The number of compression function calls $L$ is:

$$ L = \frac{L_{\text{padded}}}{512} $$

### Step 3: Plug in the values
$$ S \cdot 8 = 10 \times 2^{20} \times 8 = 83,886,080 \text{ bits} $$

$$ S \cdot 8 + 72 = 83,886,152 \text{ bits} $$

$$ L = \lceil 83{,}886{,}152 \,/\, 512 \rceil = \lceil 163{,}840.14 \rceil = 163{,}841 \text{ blocks} $$

### Result:
$$ \boxed{ L = 163{,}841 \text{ compression function evaluations} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> **General Formula:** For a file of size $S$ bytes and block size $B$ bits:
> $$ L = \left\lceil \frac{8S + 64}{B} \right\rceil $$
> (assuming the 64-bit length field is sufficient for all practical file sizes).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Verifying a HMAC Tag

**Problem:** Alice and Bob share a 256-bit key $K$. Alice sends message $M$ and tag $T = \text{HMAC-SHA256}_K(M)$. Bob receives $M'$ and $T'$. How does Bob verify?

### Verification Algorithm (Step-by-step):

**Step 1:** Re-compute the HMAC using the received message $M'$ and the shared key $K$:
$$ T_{\text{computed}} = H\Big((K^+ \oplus opad) \Vert H((K^+ \oplus ipad) \Vert M')\Big) $$

**Step 2:** Compare $T_{\text{computed}}$ with the received tag $T'$:
- If $T_{\text{computed}} = T'$ → **Accept** (message authentic and unmodified)
- If $T_{\text{computed}} \neq T'$ → **Reject** (message tampered OR key mismatch)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant-Time Comparison:** In production code, the comparison must be done in **constant time** (e.g., using `subtle.ConstantTimeCompare` in Go, or `hmac.compare_digest` in Python). This prevents **timing attacks** where an attacker guesses the tag byte-by-byte based on response delays.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Security of Common Hash Algorithms

| Algorithm | Output (bits) | Internal Block (bits) | Collision Resistance | Pre-image Resistance | Status (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MD5** | 128 | 512 | ❌ Broken ($2^{18}$ attacks) | ⚠️ Weakened | **Deprecated** |
| **SHA-1** | 160 | 512 | ❌ Broken (SHAttered, 2017) | ⚠️ Weakened | **Deprecated** |
| **SHA-224** | 224 | 512 | ✅ $2^{112}$ | ✅ $2^{224}$ | Acceptable |
| **SHA-256** | 256 | 512 | ✅ $2^{128}$ | ✅ $2^{256}$ | **Recommended** |
| **SHA-384** | 384 | 1024 | ✅ $2^{192}$ | ✅ $2^{384}$ | **Recommended** |
| **SHA-512** | 512 | 1024 | ✅ $2^{256}$ | ✅ $2^{512}$ | **Recommended** |
| **SHA-3 (Keccak)** | 224/256/384/512 | 1088/576 | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | **Recommended (NIST 2015)** |
| **BLAKE2 / BLAKE3** | 256/512 | Variable | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | High-performance alt. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If a question says *"state two weaknesses of MD5"*, the safe answers are:
> 1. Susceptible to **collision attacks** (Wang et al., 2004).
> 2. Vulnerable to **chosen-prefix collisions** (Flame malware, 2012).
> 3. Fast computation makes it unsuitable for password hashing (use bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2 instead).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Merkle–Damgård Iterative Hash Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Message M] --> B[Padding Append Length]
    B --> C[Split into N Blocks M1 M2 ... Mn]
    C --> D[Block M1]
    C --> E[Block M2]
    C --> F[Block Mn]
    H0[IV H0 Fixed Constant] --> G[Compression Function f]
    D --> G
    G --> H1[Intermediate Hash H1]
    H1 --> I[Compression Function f]
    E --> I
    I --> H2[Intermediate Hash H2]
    H2 --> J[Compression Function f]
    F --> J
    J --> Hn[Final Hash Hn Output Digest]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The message is first padded, then split into $N$ fixed-size blocks. Each block is fed into a one-way compression function $f$ along with the previous hash value (chained dependency). The final output is the fixed-length digest.

---

## 4.2 HMAC Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K[Secret Key K] --> Kp[Pad K to B bytes K plus]
    Kp --> Xor1
    ipad[ipad = 0x36 repeated B times] --> Xor1
    Xor1[K plus XOR ipad] --> InnerHash
    M[Input Message M] --> InnerHash
    InnerHash[H Inner Hash Function] --> InnerDigest
    InnerDigest --> Xor2
    Kp2[Same K plus copy] --> Xor2
    opad[opad = 0x5C repeated B times] --> Xor2
    Xor2[K plus XOR opad] --> OuterHash
    OuterHash[H Outer Hash Function] --> FinalTag[Final HMAC Tag T]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The shared secret $K$ is XORed with the **inner pad** ($ipad$) and concatenated with message $M$, then hashed once. The result is concatenated with $(K^+ \oplus opad)$ and hashed again. This two-pass structure is what makes HMAC resistant to length-extension attacks.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level MAC Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sender[Sender Side] --> S1[Compute T = MAC sub K of M]
    S1 --> S2[Transmit M parallel T over Insecure Channel]
    S2 --> Receiver[Receiver Side]
    Receiver --> R1[Receive M prime and T prime]
    R1 --> R2[Recompute T calc = MAC sub K of M prime]
    R2 --> R3{Constant Time Compare}
    R3 -- Equal --> R4[ACCEPT Message Authentic]
    R3 -- Not Equal --> R5[REJECT Tampered or Forged]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The receiver never trusts the tag in isolation — it always *recomputes* the tag using its own copy of the secret key and compares in constant time. Any mismatch means either the message was modified, the key is wrong, or the tag was forged.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Message Authentication Code (MAC). List any two requirements that a MAC must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a cryptographic checksum generated using a shared secret key $K$ to verify both the **integrity** and the **authenticity** of a message. Formally, $\text{MAC} = C_K(M)$, where $C$ is the MAC algorithm, $K$ is the symmetric secret key, and $M$ is the variable-length message.

**Two requirements:**
1. **Easy to compute** — Given $K$ and $M$, the MAC tag should be generated with low computational overhead.
2. **Computation resistance** — Without knowledge of the secret key $K$, it must be computationally infeasible to forge a valid MAC tag for any chosen message.

*(Other acceptable requirements: forgery resistance, fixed-length output, verification efficiency.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Differentiate between a Hash Function and a Message Authentication Code (MAC) in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Cryptographic Hash Function | Message Authentication Code (MAC) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Does **not** use a secret key; output depends only on the message. | Uses a **shared secret key** $K$ shared between sender and receiver. |
| 2 | Provides **integrity only** — anyone can compute and verify the hash. | Provides **both integrity and authentication** — only key holders can produce/verify tags. |
| 3 | Example: SHA-256, MD5, SHA-3. | Example: HMAC-SHA256, CMAC-AES, GMAC. |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the three security properties of a cryptographic hash function. Derive the Birthday bound to show why a hash of length $n$ offers only $n/2$ bits of collision resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **HMAC** algorithm. State the HMAC equation and explain the role of `ipad` and `opad`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Three Security Properties:**

1. **Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness):** Given a hash value $h = H(M)$, it is computationally infeasible to find *any* pre-image $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$. This requires on the order of $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ operations.

2. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given a specific message $M_1$, it is computationally infeasible to find another message $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. Also requires $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ operations.

3. **Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find *any* two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. This requires only $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ operations due to the Birthday Paradox.

**Derivation of the Birthday Bound:**

Consider sampling $k$ values uniformly at random from a set of $N = 2^n$ possible hash outputs. The probability that *no* collision occurs is:

$$ P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right) $$

Using the approximation $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$ for small $x$:

$$ P(\text{no collision}) \approx e^{-\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i/N} = e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} $$

Setting this equal to 0.5 (the 50% threshold) and solving:

$$ e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} = 0.5 $$

$$ -\frac{k(k-1)}{2N} = \ln(0.5) \approx -0.693 $$

For large $k$, $k(k-1) \approx k^2$, giving:

$$ k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2} $$

**Conclusion:** An $n$-bit hash provides only $\approx n/2$ bits of security against collision attacks — for example, a 128-bit hash (like MD5) only has 64-bit collision security, which is feasible to break with modern hardware.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**HMAC Construction (Block Diagram):**

```
              K (secret key)
              |
              v
        K+ = K padded to B bytes
        /                \
   XOR (ipad)        XOR (opad)
        |                |
        v                v
  (K+ XOR ipad) || M ----> H ---> Inner Digest
                                 \
                                  v
                       (K+ XOR opad) || Inner Digest
                                  |
                                  v
                                 H ---> HMAC Tag T
```

**HMAC Equation (RFC 2104):**

$$ \text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big((K^+ \oplus opad) \Vert H((K^+ \oplus ipad) \Vert M)\Big) $$

**Definitions:**
- $B$ = block size of the underlying hash function (e.g., 64 bytes for SHA-256).
- $L$ = output size of the underlying hash function (e.g., 32 bytes for SHA-256).
- $K^+$ = the key $K$ padded to $B$ bytes (append zeros if $|K| < B$; hash $K$ first if $|K| > B$).
- $ipad$ = the byte $0x36$ repeated $B$ times.
- $opad$ = the byte $0x5C$ repeated $B$ times.

**Role of ipad and opad:**
- The **inner pad** (`ipad`, $0x36$) is XORed with $K^+$ to create a key-masked input block that is concatenated with the message $M$. This produces the *inner hash*, binding the secret key to the message.
- The **outer pad** (`opad`, $0x5C$) is XORed with the same $K^+$ to create an outer key-masking layer that protects the inner hash.
- Using two *different* constant pads ensures that the inner and outer key-masked regions are statistically independent, preventing **length-extension attacks** that would otherwise break the simpler construction $H(K \Vert M)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Merkle–Damgård** construction used in iterative hash functions. Why is padding necessary? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 in terms of output size, block size, security status, and primary use case. Mention any one real-world attack that broke MD5 or SHA-1. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Merkle–Damgård Construction Diagram:**

```
   Input M
      |
      v
  [Padding] -- Adds '1' bit, then zeros, then 64-bit length
      |
      v
   M1 || M2 || ... || Mn  (each block is 512 bits for SHA-256)
      |     |           |
      v     v           v
   [f]   [f]   ...   [f]
    ^     ^           ^
    |     |           |
   H0   H1   ...   H(n-1)
  (IV) (fixed)
                |
                v
              Hn = Final Hash Digest
```

**Step-by-step Explanation:**

1. **Padding Step:** The original message $M$ is padded by appending a single '1' bit, followed by enough '0' bits to make the length congruent to 448 mod 512, and finally a 64-bit big-endian representation of the original message length.

2. **Block Splitting:** The padded message is divided into $L$ blocks of fixed size (512 bits for SHA-1, SHA-256, MD5; 1024 bits for SHA-512).

3. **Initialization:** An initial hash value $H_0$ (IV) is set from a fixed standard constant.

4. **Compression Loop:** For each block $M_i$, the compression function $f$ updates the state:
$$ H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i) $$

5. **Final Output:** After processing the last block, $H_L$ is emitted as the message digest.

**Why Padding is Necessary:**

- **To handle variable-length messages uniformly** — Padding ensures the input length is always a multiple of the block size, regardless of the original message length.
- **To make padding deterministic** — A well-defined padding scheme (the "Merkle–Damgård strengthening" with the length field) prevents ambiguity and resists length-extension attacks where an attacker appends extra data to a known hash.
- **To include the message length** — Including the 64-bit length field binds the hash to the exact original message, so a longer message cannot be forged with the same hash as a shorter one.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output Size (bits)** | 128 | 160 | 256 |
| **Block Size (bits)** | 512 | 512 | 512 |
| **Rounds** | 4 (16 ops each) | 80 | 64 |
| **Designed by** | Rivest (1992) | NIST (1995) | NIST (2001) |
| **Collision Resistance** | ❌ Broken ($2^{18}$) | ❌ Broken ($2^{63}$) | ✅ $2^{128}$ |
| **Pre-image Resistance** | ⚠️ Weakened | ⚠️ Weakened | ✅ $2^{256}$ |
| **Current Status (2024)** | **Deprecated** | **Deprecated** | **Recommended** |
