decrypted sig?} Decision -->|Yes| Accept[ACCEPT Identity of A] Decision -->|No| Reject[REJECT + Alert] style Sign_Side fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style Verify_Side fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000000 style Accept fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style Reject fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 State Machine — One-Way Authentication Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> ChallengeSent: B generates N_B ChallengeSent --> Waiting: Awaiting response Waiting --> ResponseReceived: A sends E(K_AB, N_B || ID_A) ResponseReceived --> Decrypting: B uses K_AB Decrypting --> Verifying: Compare N_B and ID_A Verifying --> Authenticated: Match Verifying --> Failed: Mismatch Authenticated --> [*] Failed --> [*] note right of Authenticated: B is convinced of A's identity note right of Failed: Log + Counter ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Decision Tree — Choosing a One-Way Auth Scheme ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need: One-Way Authentication] --> Q1{Pre-shared
key available?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Resource
constrained?} Q1 -->|No| Q3{PKI
available?} Q2 -->|Yes| HB[Use Hash-Based
H(K_AB || N)] Q2 -->|No| Sym[Use Symmetric Encryption
E(K_AB, N || ID)] Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Need non-repudiation?} Q3 -->|No| KDC[Deploy KDC
+ Symmetric] Q4 -->|Yes| Sig[Use Digital Signature
Sig_A(M)] Q4 -->|No| Enc[Use Public-Key Encryption
+ MAC] style Start fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000000 style HB fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000 style Sym fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000000 style Sig fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000000 style Enc fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000000 style KDC fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024] **Q: Define one-way authentication. List any two applications where one-way authentication is sufficient.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** One-way authentication is a security protocol in which only one party (the sender) proves its identity to another party (the receiver), without the receiver having to prove its identity back. The verification is unidirectional. **Two Applications (any two of the following):** 1. **Electronic Mail (S/MIME / PGP):** Sender signs email; receiver verifies signature. Mail server's identity is established separately by TLS. 2. **Software / Firmware Updates:** Vendor signs the update package; the device verifies the signature before installation. 3. **RFID / IoT Authentication:** A sensor tag authenticates itself to a reader; the reader's authenticity is established by physical security or out-of-band means. 4. **JWT-based API authentication:** API server issues a signed token; resource server validates the signature. **(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for applications)** --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Q: Differentiate between one-way and mutual authentication.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | One-Way Authentication | Mutual Authentication | |-----------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Direction** | Unidirectional (A → B) | Bidirectional (A ↔ B) | | **Proofs exchanged** | 1 identity proof | 2 identity proofs | | **Round-trips** | 1 or 2 | 2 or 3 | | **MITM resistance** | Weak (verifier impersonation possible) | Strong (both parties verified) | | **Use case** | Email, broadcast, IoT | TLS handshake, SSH login | | **Computational cost** | Lower | Higher | **(3 marks: tabular comparison with at least 4 valid points)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper] #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain with neat diagram the one-way authentication protocol using a shared secret key with a nonce. Show all messages exchanged between the parties. **Model Answer:** **Diagram:** ``` A (Claimant) B (Verifier) | | | <--- N_B (nonce) -------- | Step 1: B generates nonce | | A computes: | C = E(K_AB)[ N_B || ID_A ] | | | | --- E(K_AB)[N_B || ID_A] --> | Step 2: Send response | | | B decrypts with K_AB | Verifies N_B matches | Extracts ID_A | | | Identity of A is verified ``` **Explanation:** 1. **Setup:** Principal `A` and principal `B` share a long-term secret key `K_{AB}` through a secure out-of-band channel. 2. **Step 1 — Challenge:** `B` generates a fresh, unpredictable nonce `N_B` of at least 128 bits and sends it to `A`. This nonce is the *freshness element* that prevents replay. 3. **Step 2 — Response:** `A` constructs the message `M = (N_B \;\vert\; ID_A)` and encrypts it using `K_{AB}` to produce: $$C = E_{K_{AB}}[ N_B \;\vert\; ID_A ]$$ `A` sends `C` to `B`. 4. **Step 3 — Verification:** `B` decrypts `C` using `K_{AB}` to obtain `(N'_B \;\vert\; ID'_A)`. Verification succeeds if and only if: $$N'_B = N_B \quad \text{AND} \quad ID'_A \text{ is valid}$$ 5. **Why this authenticates `A`:** Only `A` and `B` know `K_{AB}`. The encryption `E_{K_{AB}}[N_B]` could have been produced by either party; however, the *plaintext* `N_B` was chosen by `B` at random in Step 1. An adversary `E` cannot construct `E_{K_{AB}}[N_B]` without `K_{AB}`. 6. **Why nonce prevents replay:** A previously recorded `E_{K_{AB}}[N_B_old]` is useless because `B`'s new challenge `N_B` will not match `N_B_old`. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for protocol steps, 2 for security justification)** --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the one-way authentication protocol using public-key cryptography. Discuss the role of digital signatures and certificates. **Model Answer:** **Protocol:** ``` A → B : M || Sig_A(M) || Cert_A ``` Where: - `M` = message (may include `ID_A`, timestamp `T`, nonce `N_B`) - `Sig_A(M) = E_{K_{RA}}[H(M)]` = A's digital signature using private key - `Cert_A = E_{K_{RCA}}[ ID_A \;\vert\; K_{UA} \;\vert\; \text{validity} ]` = certificate issued by CA **Step-by-step:** 1. **Certificate Verification:** `B` first verifies `Cert_A` using the CA's well-known public key `K_{UCA}`. This binds `ID_A` to `A`'s public key `K_{UA}`. 2. **Hash Computation:** `B` computes `H(M)` independently using the same hash function (e.g., SHA-256). 3. **Signature Verification:** `B` decrypts `Sig_A(M)` using `A`'s public key `K_{UA}` to recover `H'(M)`. Verification succeeds if: $$H'(M) = H(M)$$ 4. **Result:** `B` is convinced that `M` originated from `A` and was not altered. **Role of Digital Signatures:** - Provide **authenticity** (only `A` could have signed with `K_{RA}`) - Provide **integrity** (any modification of `M` invalidates the signature) - Provide **non-repudiation** (`A` cannot deny signing `M`) **Role of Certificates:** - Bind identity `ID_A` to public key `K_{UA}` in a tamper-evident way - Enable trust in public-key systems without pre-shared secrets - Issued by trusted Certificate Authorities (CA) following X.509 standard **Comparison with Hash-Based Approach:** | Property | Public-Key (Signature) | Hash-Based | |----------|------------------------|------------| | Pre-shared key needed? | **No** (only CA's public key) | **Yes** | | Non-repudiation? | **Yes** | **No** | | Verifier cost | High (modular exponentiation) | Low (one hash) | **(7 marks: 2 for protocol description, 2 for digital signature role, 2 for certificate role, 1 for comparison)** --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper] #### (a) [7 Marks] Compare one-way authentication based on (i) shared secret key with nonce, (ii) public-key signature, and (iii) hash function. Tabulate the differences with respect to: number of messages, key requirement, computational overhead, and replay resistance. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | (i) Symmetric + Nonce | (ii) Public-Key Signature | (iii) Hash Function | |-----------|----------------------|---------------------------|---------------------| | **Number of messages** | 2 (challenge + response) | 1 (signed message) | 2 (challenge + response) | | **Key requirement** | Pre-shared secret `K_{AB}` | `A`'s private key `K_{RA}` + CA's public key | Pre-shared secret `K_{AB}` | | **Computational overhead (verifier)** | 1 block cipher decryption (low) | 1 modular exponentiation / ECC verify (high) | 1 hash computation (lowest) | | **Replay resistance** | Nonce-based (strong) | Timestamp / nonce in `M` (strong) | Nonce-based (strong) | | **Confidentiality** | Optional (if message encrypted) | No (signature is public) | No | | **Non-repudiation** | No | Yes | No | | **Forward secrecy** | No | Conditional | No | | **Standard example** | Needham-Schroeder, NTLMv2 | RSA-PSS, ECDSA, Ed25519 | HMAC-SHA256, HTTP Digest | **Justification of choices:** - **Symmetric + Nonce** is preferred for **internal, low-latency systems** where `K_{AB}` can be pre-distributed. - **Public-Key Signature** is preferred for **open, internet-scale systems** (email, software distribution) where `K_{AB}` cannot be pre-shared. - **Hash-based** is preferred for **resource-constrained devices** (IoT, RFID) where even block-cipher decryption is too expensive. **(7 marks: 5 for table, 2 for application justification)** --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the Needham-Schroeder shared-key one-way authentication protocol. Identify any one known attack against a simple version and explain how timestamps mitigate it. **Model Answer:** **Protocol (Needham-Schroeder, 1978):** Trusted Authentication Server (AS) mediates the exchange. Goal: `A` authenticates to `B` and they share a fresh session key `K_S`. ``` Step 1: A → AS : ID_A || ID_B || N_1 Step 2: AS → A : E(K_AS)[ K_S || ID_B || N_1 || E(K_BS)[ K_S || ID_A ] ] Step 3: A → B : E(K_BS)[ K_S || ID_A ] Step 4: B → A : E(K_S)[ N_2 ] Step 5: A → B : E(K_S)[ N_2 - 1 ] ``` (Step 1 is request, Step 2 issues the ticket, Step 3 is the one-way authentication token, Steps 4-5 are the mutual confirmation.) **Analysis of Step 3 (the one-way authentication part):** `A` sends `E(K_BS)[ K_S \;\vert\; ID_A ]` to `B`. This is the "ticket." When `B` decrypts it, `B` obtains the session key `K_S` and the identity of the sender. The fact that only `AS` could have constructed this ticket (because only `AS` knows `K_{BS}`) implies the ticket is authentic, hence `A`'s identity is bound to `K_S`. **Known Attack — Replay of Old Ticket:** In the original 1978 protocol, an adversary `E` who had previously recorded a valid ticket `E(K_BS)[ K_S \;\vert\; ID_A ]` could replay it later, even after the original session had ended, causing `B` to use an old `K_S` that `A` no longer trusts. **Mitigation via Timestamps (Denning-Sacco 1981):** The fix is to add a timestamp `T` to the ticket: $$E(K_{BS})[ K_S \;\vert\; ID_A \;\vert\; T ]$$ `B` then checks that `T` lies within a validity window `± Δt` of `B`'s local clock. If `T` is too old, the ticket is rejected. This converts an *unbounded* freshness property into a *bounded* one based on clock synchronization. **Why clocks must be synchronized:** If `A` and `B`'s clocks drift by more than `Δt`, legitimate sessions may be rejected. KTU notes: typical `Δt = 5` minutes, with clock synchronization via NTP. **(7 marks: 3 for protocol, 2 for attack description, 2 for timestamp mitigation)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost on One-Way Authentication Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing the direction of authentication:** Students often write `B → A` as the authentication message, when it should be `A → B`. Direction matters — this is "**one-way**" authentication, not mutual. > > 2. **Forgetting the freshness element:** Writing `A → B : E(K_AB)[ID_A]` without a nonce or timestamp. This loses at least **2 marks** and is considered a fatal flaw (replay-vulnerable). > > 3. **Mixing up private and public keys:** Writing `E_{K_{UA}}[...]` when authenticating. Authentication uses the **private signature key** `K_{RA}`, not the public key. > > 4. **Omitting the role of the KDC / CA:** In the Needham-Schroeder protocol, students often forget to mention that `AS` is trusted and that `K_{AS}` / `K_{BS}` are long-term shared keys. > > 5. **Not drawing the diagram:** In 7-mark sub-questions, **a neat ASCII / block diagram is mandatory** (typically worth 2 marks). Skipping it costs marks even if the textual answer is correct. > > 6. **Failing to justify WHY a step provides authentication:** Just writing the protocol steps is incomplete. Each step needs a 1-line justification of *why* it proves identity (e.g., "only A knows `K_{RA}`, so a valid signature proves A's identity"). > > 7. **Confusing confidentiality with authentication:** A common error is to use `E_{K_{UB}}` (encryption with B's public key) and call it "authentication." This is **confidentiality for B**, not authentication of A. Authentication requires a **signature** with A's private key. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — One-Way Authentication (Module 1, PECAT863)** ### Core Concepts - [x] **One-way = unidirectional**: only `A` proves identity to `B`; `B` does not prove itself to `A`. - [x] **Three types:** (1) symmetric + nonce, (2) public-key signature, (3) hash-based. - [x] **Freshness is mandatory:** Every protocol must include a nonce, timestamp, or sequence number. ### Must-Know Protocols - [x] **Symmetric nonce-based:** `B → A : N_B`; `A → B : E(K_AB)[N_B || ID_A]`. - [x] **Needham-Schroeder (shared-key, 1978):** uses `AS` to issue tickets encrypted with `K_BS`. - [x] **Public-key signature:** `A → B : M || Sig_A(M) || Cert_A`. - [x] **Hash-based:** `A → B : ID_A || H(K_AB || N_B)`. ### Must-Know Symbols (from KTU board questions) - [x] `K_AB` = long-term shared key between `A` and `B` - [x] `K_S` = session key (freshly generated) - [x] `K_RA` = `A`'s private signature key - [x] `K_UA` = `A`'s public verification key - [x] `N_B` = nonce from `B` - [x] `T` = timestamp - [x] `cert_A` = X.509 certificate binding `ID_A` to `K_UA` ### Key Security Properties - [x] Authentication of `A` to `B` ✔ - [x] Replay resistance (via nonce/timestamp) ✔ - [x] Integrity (via MAC or signature) ✔ - [x] **Non-repudiation:** Only in public-key signature variant ✔ - [x] **Mutual auth:** NOT provided — must extend protocol ✘ - [x] **Forward secrecy:** NOT typically provided ✘ ### Numerical / Derivative Facts - [x] Replay probability ≈ `k / 2^|N|` for `k` attempts and nonce length `|N|`. - [x] AES-128 with 128-bit nonce gives `P_{forge} ≤ 2^{-127}`. - [x] SHA-256 preimage resistance: `2^{256}` work factor. ### High-Yield Exam Triggers (KTU 2024) - [x] "Explain one-way authentication with shared secret key using nonce" — **7 marks** (draw diagram + justify freshness). - [x] "Differentiate one-way and mutual authentication" — **3 marks** (table). - [x] "Explain Needham-Schroeder protocol" — **7 or 14 marks** (full message exchange + replay attack). - [x] "Role of timestamps in authentication" — **3 or 7 marks** (mention ± Δt window, NTP sync). - [x] "Public-key one-way authentication with digital signature" — **7 marks** (mention `Cert_A`). ### Engineering Takeaway > One-way authentication is the **lightest-weight identity verification** primitive, ideal for **email, IoT, firmware updates, and JWTs**, where the receiver is implicitly trusted (e.g., via TLS or physical security). For bidirectional trust, it must be extended to **mutual authentication**.

topics.title: Kerberos Version 4
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kerberos Version 4**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerberos Version 4** is the fourth iteration of the Kerberos authentication protocol, originally developed as part of **Project Athena** at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)**. It is a **symmetric-key based, trusted third-party authentication protocol** designed to provide strong authentication for client-server applications over an **insecure, open distributed network environment** (such as a campus LAN or the early Internet) using **tickets** and **authenticators** exchanged between three principal entities: the **Client (C)**, the **Authentication Server (AS)**, and the **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)**. Together, the AS and TGS form the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**.

Kerberos Version 4 was formally documented in **RFC 4120**'s predecessor (Steiner, Neuman, & Schiller, *"Kerberos: An Authentication Service for Open Network Systems"*, USENIX Winter 1988). It uses the **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** in **Cipher Block Chaining (CBC)** mode as its symmetric cryptographic primitive.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Airport Boarding Pass System**
>
> Imagine a frequent flyer named **Alice** wants to board **Plane B** at an airport. She cannot walk straight to the gate because security requires two-step verification:
>
> 1. **Step 1 — Identity Check (Authentication Service Exchange):** Alice first goes to the **Check-in Counter (AS)**, proves her identity with her **passport (password/key)**, and receives a **Boarding Pass for the Lounge (TGT — Ticket Granting Ticket)**. The lounge pass is encrypted so that even Alice cannot tamper with it — only the lounge's scanners (TGS) can read it.
>
> 2. **Step 2 — Lounge Access (TGS Exchange):** Alice enters the **Lounge** and shows her **Lounge Pass (TGT)**. The lounge officer verifies it and issues a **specific Gate Pass (Service Ticket)** for **Plane B**.
>
> 3. **Step 3 — Boarding (Client/Server Exchange):** At Gate B, Alice presents the **Gate Pass** along with a fresh **boarding token (Authenticator)**. The airline verifies both, and Alice is allowed to board.
>
> **The key insight:** Alice never re-enters her password after Step 1. The system uses **tickets** (trusted, unforgeable digital documents) and **session keys** (one-time keys) to keep the password safe from network eavesdroppers.

### 1.3 Core Components of Kerberos V4

> [!NOTE]
> **Principal Entities (the three actors in the protocol):**
> - **C** — The **Client** (user workstation/process requesting service)
> - **AS** — The **Authentication Server** (proves client identity, issues TGT)
> - **TGS** — The **Ticket-Granting Server** (issues service tickets for target servers)
> - **V** — The **Target Server** (the application server providing the requested service)
>
> **Architectural Components:**
> - **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** = AS + TGS, hosted on a **secure host**.
> - **Realm** — A Kerberos administrative domain (e.g., `Kerala.EDU.IN`); users and services are uniquely identified as `principal@REALM`.
> - **Kerberos Database** — A secure store of **client secrets** (password-derived keys), **server master keys**, and **policy information**.

### 1.4 Design Goals of Kerberos V4

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the original MIT Project Athena specification, Kerberos V4 was designed to satisfy:
> 1. **Security** — Resist eavesdropping and replay attacks on an open network.
> 2. **Reliability** — No single point of failure (KDC replicated).
> 3. **Transparency** — User does not need to be aware of authentication mechanics.
> 4. **Scalability** — Support large numbers of clients and servers.
> 5. **Single Sign-On (SSO)** — One authentication unlocks multiple services.

### 1.5 Standard Notation Used in KTU Examinations

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $K_C$ | **Secret key** of client C (derived from password) |
| $K_{TGS}$ | **Secret key** of TGS (known only to AS & TGS) |
| $K_V$ | **Secret key** of target server V |
| $K_{C,TGS}$ | **Session key** between C and TGS |
| $K_{C,V}$ | **Session key** between C and V |
| $ID_C$ | **Identity** of client C |
| $AD_C$ | **Network address** of client C |
| $TS_i$ | **Timestamp** issued at step $i$ |
| $Lifetime_i$ | **Validity duration** of a ticket |
| $N$ | **Nonce** (random number for replay protection) |
| $E_K[\cdot]$ | **Symmetric encryption** using key $K$ |
| $K_{C,TGS}(n)$ | **Subkey** (mutual authentication variant) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a Trusted Third-Party Protocol?

In a distributed open network, naïve password authentication has three fatal flaws:

- **Sniffing risk:** Passwords sent in plaintext can be intercepted.
- **Replay risk:** Captured credentials can be re-submitted by attackers.
- **Storage risk:** Every server storing user passwords enlarges the attack surface.

Kerberos V4 solves this by **never sending the password over the network after initial login** and by centralizing trust in a hardened **KDC** that holds all long-term secrets.

### 2.2 The Two-Tier Ticket Model (Logical Steps)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step A — Single Sign-On Bootstrap:** Client proves identity to **AS** once per login session → receives **TGT** + **C-TGS session key**.
>
> **Step B — Per-Service Access:** For each service V, client presents **TGT** to **TGS** → receives **service ticket** + **C-V session key**.
>
> **Step C — Service Authentication:** Client presents **service ticket** + **fresh authenticator** to **V** → gains access (with optional mutual auth).

### 2.3 Detailed Dialogue Breakdown (Kerberos V4 Messages)

#### 2.3.1 Authentication Service Exchange (AS Exchange)

> Client → AS : $ID_C \;\Vert\; ID_{TGS} \;\Vert\; TS_1$

> AS → Client : $E_{K_C}\big[\,K_{C,TGS} \;\Vert\; ID_{TGS} \;\Vert\; TS_2 \;\Vert\; Lifetime_2 \;\Vert\; Ticket_{TGS}\,\big]$

where the **TGT** is defined as:

$$Ticket_{TGS} = E_{K_{TGS}}\big[\,K_{C,TGS} \;\Vert\; ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; ID_{TGS} \;\Vert\; TS_2 \;\Vert\; Lifetime_2\,\big]$$

#### 2.3.2 Ticket-Granting Service Exchange (TGS Exchange)

> Client → TGS : $ID_V \;\Vert\; Ticket_{TGS} \;\Vert\; Authenticator_C$

> TGS → Client : $E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big[\,K_{C,V} \;\Vert\; ID_V \;\Vert\; TS_4 \;\Vert\; Ticket_V\,\big]$

where the **Authenticator** is:

$$Authenticator_C = E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big[\,ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; TS_3\,\big]$$

and the **service ticket** is:

$$Ticket_V = E_{K_V}\big[\,K_{C,V} \;\Vert\; ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; ID_V \;\Vert\; TS_4 \;\Vert\; Lifetime_4\,\big]$$

#### 2.3.3 Client/Server Authentication Exchange (AP Exchange)

> Client → V : $Ticket_V \;\Vert\; Authenticator_C$

> V → Client (optional mutual auth) : $E_{K_{C,V}}[\,TS_5 + 1\,]$

where for the second leg:

$$Authenticator_C = E_{K_{C,V}}\big[\,ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; TS_5\,\big]$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Cryptographic Primitives and Parameters — Master This Table.**

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Range / Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Master secret | $K_C = \text{DES}(PW_C, \text{salt})$ | 56-bit DES key | Derived from user password |
| Session key | $K_{C,TGS}$, $K_{C,V}$ | 56-bit DES key | One-time symmetric key |
| Clock skew tolerance | $\Delta$ | **5 minutes (300 s)** | Replay protection window |
| Ticket lifetime | $Lifetime_2$, $Lifetime_4$ | Max **21 hours** (V4 limit) | Bounds ticket usability |
| Ticket structure | $E_{K_X}[\,K \;\Vert\; ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; ID_X \;\Vert\; TS \;\Vert\; Life\,]$ | Variable | Self-contained, unforgeable |
| Authenticator structure | $E_{K}[\,ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; TS\,]$ | Variable | Freshness proof |
| Nonce size | $N$ | Typically 32 bits | Replay defense in AS_REQ |
| Cipher used | DES in **CBC** mode | 64-bit block, 56-bit key | Symmetric primitive |
| Ticket $+$ Authenticator replay cache size | $n$ | Implementation-defined | Stores recent $TS$ values |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Note:** In real engineering deployments (Microsoft Active Directory, MIT Kerberos, Heimdal), the **KDC is replicated** using a master-slave model to eliminate the single point of failure. The **KDC service uses UDP/88** in V4 (TCP/88 in V5).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kerberos V4 underpins the authentication architecture of nearly every modern enterprise:
- **Microsoft Active Directory** (uses Kerberos V5 superset).
- **Apple's OpenDirectory** and macOS network logins.
- **Hadoop / Big-Data clusters** (`kinit`, `klist`, `kdestroy`).
- **SSH GSSAPI authentication** in Linux enterprise environments.
- **Network File System (NFSv4)** security.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **comprehensive line-by-line walkthrough** of the entire Kerberos V4 authentication dialogue, including every cryptographic transformation, the security justification for each field, and the algebraic structure of the exchanged tickets. This is the level of depth expected in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.1 Step 1 — Client Initiates AS Exchange

**Message 1 (Client → AS, in cleartext over the wire):**

$$M_1 = \big\{\,ID_C,\; ID_{TGS},\; TS_1\,\big\}$$

**Explanation of fields:**
- $ID_C$: Identifies the principal requesting access. Allows AS to look up $K_C$ in the Kerberos database.
- $ID_{TGS}$: Tells the AS which TGS the client wants a TGT for. In a single-realm setup, this is the realm's KDC.
- $TS_1$: An optional timestamp at the client to detect AS delay; not security-critical here.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security property:** This message is **not encrypted** because the client has no shared key with the AS yet. An eavesdropper learning $ID_C$ is not a confidentiality breach.

### 3.2 Step 2 — AS Issues the TGT

**Message 2 (AS → Client, encrypted under $K_C$):**

$$M_2 = E_{K_C}\big[\,K_{C,TGS} \;\Vert\; ID_{TGS} \;\Vert\; TS_2 \;\Vert\; Lifetime_2 \;\Vert\; Ticket_{TGS}\,\big]$$

**Ticket structure (encrypted under $K_{TGS}$):**

$$Ticket_{TGS} = E_{K_{TGS}}\big[\,K_{C,TGS} \;\Vert\; ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; ID_{TGS} \;\Vert\; TS_2 \;\Vert\; Lifetime_2\,\big]$$

**Algebraic unpacking — what each field guarantees:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{C,TGS} & : \text{One-time session key for C-TGS communication. AS generates it randomly.} \\
ID_{TGS} & : \text{Binds the TGT to the specific TGS (prevents cross-TGS substitution).} \\
TS_2 & : \text{Timestamp of TGT issuance — basis for lifetime calculation.} \\
Lifetime_2 & : \text{Maximum validity duration; in V4 the upper bound is } \mathbf{21 \text{ hours}} \\
& \quad \text{due to 32-bit time-field overflow on 31-Dec-1969 + 21h (Y2038-like issue).} \\
ID_C,\, AD_C & : \text{Binds the TGT to the specific client and its IP address.} \\
Ticket_{TGS} & : \text{Encrypted with } K_{TGS} \text{ so client cannot read or forge it.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cryptographic reasoning:** By encrypting $M_2$ with $K_C$, only the legitimate client (who knows their password) can decrypt to recover $K_{C,TGS}$. The client never sees $Ticket_{TGS}$'s plaintext — it is forwarded opaquely in Step 3.

### 3.3 Step 3 — Client Requests a Service Ticket from TGS

**Message 3 (Client → TGS):**

$$M_3 = \big\{\,ID_V,\; Ticket_{TGS},\; Authenticator_C\,\big\}$$

where the **Authenticator** is freshly computed by the client:

$$Authenticator_C = E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big[\,ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; TS_3\,\big]$$

**Why both ticket and authenticator?**
- **Ticket** proves the client *once* (long-term credential).
- **Authenticator** proves the client is *currently alive* (short-term proof, includes fresh $TS_3$).
- This dual-token model is the **famous Kerberos two-pronged proof**.

**Key reasoning — the $AD_C$ binding:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If an attacker replays } M_3 \text{, the TGS will see:}\\
\text{Decrypted } Authenticator_C &\rightarrow ID_C, AD_C, TS_3 \\
\text{Decrypted } Ticket_{TGS}    &\rightarrow ID_C, AD_C, TS_2, Lifetime_2 \\
\text{TGS compares } AD_C \text{ fields; if they match AND } TS_3 \text{ is fresh (within } \Delta = 5\text{ min), accept.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Step 4 — TGS Issues the Service Ticket

**Message 4 (TGS → Client, encrypted under $K_{C,TGS}$):**

$$M_4 = E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big[\,K_{C,V} \;\Vert\; ID_V \;\Vert\; TS_4 \;\Vert\; Ticket_V\,\big]$$

where the **service ticket** is:

$$Ticket_V = E_{K_V}\big[\,K_{C,V} \;\Vert\; ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; ID_V \;\Vert\; TS_4 \;\Vert\; Lifetime_4\,\big]$$

**Field-by-field justification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{C,V} & : \text{Fresh session key for C-V conversation (randomly chosen by TGS).} \\
ID_V & : \text{Binds the ticket to the specific target server V.} \\
TS_4 & : \text{Issuance timestamp; client computes } Lifetime_4 = TS_4 + \Delta t. \\
Ticket_V & : \text{Encrypted with } K_V \text{ — only V can decrypt it.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Step 5 — Client Authenticates to Target Server V

**Message 5 (Client → V):**

$$M_5 = \big\{\,Ticket_V,\; Authenticator'_C\,\big\}$$

$$Authenticator'_C = E_{K_{C,V}}\big[\,ID_C \;\Vert\; AD_C \;\Vert\; TS_5\,\big]$$

**Server V's verification procedure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.\; &\text{Decrypt } Ticket_V \text{ using } K_V \text{ to recover } (K_{C,V}, ID_C, AD_C, ID_V, TS_4, Lifetime_4). \\
2.\; &\text{Check } ID_V \text{ matches the service being requested (prevents ticket redirection).} \\
3.\; &\text{Decrypt } Authenticator'_C \text{ using the recovered } K_{C,V}. \\
4.\; &\text{Verify } ID_C, AD_C \text{ match ticket fields.} \\
5.\; &\text{Check } | \text{now} - TS_5 | \leq \Delta \;(\text{timestamp freshness, } \Delta = 5 \text{ min}). \\
6.\; &\text{Check } TS_5 \text{ is not in replay cache (defense against capture-replay).}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Step 6 — Optional Mutual Authentication

**Message 6 (V → Client):**

$$M_6 = E_{K_{C,V}}\big[\,TS_5 + 1\,\big]$$

**Logic:** Only V could have recovered $K_{C,V}$ from $Ticket_V$. By returning $TS_5 + 1$ encrypted under $K_{C,V}$, V proves "I have $K_{C,V}$, therefore I am the legitimate V." The $+1$ prevents reflection of $TS_5$ itself as a fake response.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Vulnerability vs. Defense

| Attack | Kerberos V4 Defense |
|---|---|
| **Password sniffing** | Password never sent after step 1; $K_C$ is local-only |
| **Replay of $M_1$** | AS_REQ is plaintext, but AS returns $K_{C,TGS}$ encrypted in $K_C$ — useless without password |
| **Replay of $M_3$** | TGS checks $TS_3$ freshness + maintains replay cache |
| **Replay of $M_5$** | V checks $TS_5$ freshness + replay cache |
| **Ticket tampering** | Tickets are $E_{K_{TGS}}[\cdot]$ and $E_{K_V}[\cdot]$ — MAC-style integrity via encryption |
| **Man-in-the-middle** | $AD_C$ field binds tickets to network address |
| **Offline brute-force on ticket** | Tickets encrypted with strong 56-bit DES in V4 (cracked today → V5 uses AES) |

### 3.8 Limitations of V4 (Motivator for V5)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitations — Favourite KTU 14-Marker Topic:**
> 1. **DES dependency:** V4 mandates single DES — vulnerable to brute force with modern hardware. V5 → AES, 3DES, Camellia.
> 2. **Internet address assumption:** V4 assumes IP addresses are monotonic identifiers. V5 supports arbitrary principal types.
> 3. **Byte-ordering ambiguity:** V4 ticket format has loose serialization rules. V5 uses **ASN.1/DER**.
> 4. **Limited ticket lifetime:** V4 has 21-hour cap due to timestamp field. V5 supports renewable & post-dated tickets.
> 5. **No delegation / proxy tickets** in V4. V5 introduces them.
> 6. **Weak inter-realm trust:** V4 uses a rigid hierarchical trust; V5 uses **transitive cross-realm trust chains**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Component Block Diagram of Kerberos V4 Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT["CLIENT WORKSTATION C"]
        CK["Client Process"]
        KB["Key Cache K_C (derived from password)"]
        CK --> KB
    end

    subgraph KDC["KEY DISTRIBUTION CENTER (KDC) - Secure Host"]
        AS["Authentication Server (AS)"]
        TGS["Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)"]
        DB[("Kerberos Database: K_C, K_TGS, K_V1...K_Vn")]
        AS <--> DB
        TGS <--> DB
        AS -.issues TGT.-> TGS
    end

    subgraph REALM["KERBEROS REALM - All Trusted Nodes"]
        V1["Application Server V1"]
        V2["Application Server V2"]
        Vn["Application Server Vn"]
    end

    CK -- "1 AS_REQ" --> AS
    AS -- "2 AS_REP with TGT" --> CK
    CK -- "3 TGS_REQ" --> TGS
    TGS -- "4 TGS_REP with service ticket" --> CK
    CK -- "5 AP_REQ" --> V1
    V1 -- "6 Optional AP_REP mutual auth" --> CK
    V1 -.registered.-> DB
    V2 -.registered.-> DB
    Vn -.registered.-> DB
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram of the Full V4 Authentication Dialogue

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client C
    participant AS as Authentication Server
    participant TGS as Ticket-Granting Server
    participant V as Target Server V

    C->>AS: ID_C, ID_TGS, TS_1
    Note over AS: Lookup K_C, generate K_C_TGS
    AS->>C: E_KC[ K_C_TGS, ID_TGS, TS_2, Lifetime_2, Ticket_TGS ]
    Note over C: Decrypt with K_C; cache K_C_TGS

    C->>TGS: ID_V, Ticket_TGS, Authenticator_C
    Note over TGS: Decrypt TGT; verify Authenticator freshness
    TGS->>C: E_KC_TGS[ K_C_V, ID_V, TS_4, Ticket_V ]
    Note over C: Decrypt; cache K_C_V

    C->>V: Ticket_V, Authenticator_C
    Note over V: Decrypt Ticket_V; verify Authenticator
    V-->>C: E_KC_V[ TS_5 + 1 ] (optional mutual auth)
    Note over C: Service access granted
```

### 4.3 Ticket Internal Structure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TICKET_TGS["Ticket_TGS encrypted with K_TGS"]
        A1[K_C_TGS]
        A2[ID_C]
        A3[AD_C]
        A4[ID_TGS]
        A5[TS_2]
        A6[Lifetime_2]
    end

    subgraph TICKET_V["Ticket_V encrypted with K_V"]
        B1[K_C_V]
        B2[ID_C]
        B3[AD_C]
        B4[ID_V]
        B5[TS_4]
        B6[Lifetime_4]
    end

    subgraph AUTH_C["Authenticator encrypted with K_C_TGS or K_C_V"]
        C1[ID_C]
        C2[AD_C]
        C3[TS_3 or TS_5]
    end
```

### 4.4 V4 Lifetime and Replay Defense Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NOW["Current Time T_now"]
    TS["Timestamp in Ticket/Authenticator TS_i"]
    DELTA["Clock Skew Window Δ = 5 minutes"]
    CACHE["Replay Cache (last 5 min timestamps)"]

    NOW --> CHECK1{Is abs T_now - TS_i ≤ Δ ?}
    CHECK1 -- No --> REJECT[Reject Message]
    CHECK1 -- Yes --> CHECK2{Is TS_i in Replay Cache?}
    CHECK2 -- Yes --> REJECT
    CHECK2 -- No --> ADD["Add TS_i to Cache"]
    ADD --> ACCEPT[Accept & Proceed]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. What is the role of the Key Distribution Center (KDC) in Kerberos Version 4? Differentiate between AS and TGS.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **KDC** is the trusted third-party that mediates authentication in Kerberos V4. It consists of two logical components:
> - **AS (Authentication Server):** On receiving $ID_C, ID_{TGS}$ from a client, the AS verifies the client's identity (using $K_C$ from the database) and issues a **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)** plus a session key $K_{C,TGS}$ encrypted in $K_C$. The AS is contacted **once per login session**.
> - **TGS (Ticket-Granting Server):** On receiving a TGT and a fresh Authenticator, the TGS issues a **service ticket** for a specific target server V along with $K_{C,V}$. The TGS is contacted **once per service** requested.
>
> Together, AS + TGS = KDC. The AS handles initial identity proof; the TGS handles service-specific authorization.

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Q2. Why is the Authenticator required in addition to the Ticket in Kerberos V4?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Ticket** is a long-lived, reusable credential encrypted under the server's key, so the client cannot read or forge it. However, since a ticket is reused, an attacker who steals it could replay it. To prevent this, the client must also present an **Authenticator** — a fresh, short-lived message encrypted with the **session key** (e.g., $K_{C,TGS}$ or $K_{C,V}$) containing $ID_C, AD_C,$ and a **current timestamp** $TS_i$. The server verifies that $|T_{now} - TS_i| \leq 5$ minutes and that $TS_i$ has not been used recently. This dual-token approach (ticket + authenticator) provides **replay protection** without requiring online ticket reissuance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to encrypt** the ticket under the *server's* key — examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $K_C$ (long-term) with $K_{C,TGS}$ (session)** — explicitly label both.
> 3. **Omitting the timestamp $TS_i$** in the Authenticator — major replay-defense marks lost.
> 4. **Not showing the optional $M_6$** mutual-authentication step — partial deduction.
> 5. **V4 vs V5 confusion:** In V4, lifetimes cap at **21 hours**; tickets are encrypted with **DES**.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

> **Q (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of Kerberos Version 4**, clearly identifying AS, TGS, and the KDC. Discuss the **three exchanges** involved in the V4 authentication dialogue. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Write the complete Kerberos V4 message exchange between a **Client C**, **TGS**, and **Application Server V**. For each message, explain the **cryptographic purpose** of every field. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture (3 marks):**
Kerberos V4 has a **three-tier architecture**:
1. **Client (C):** End-user workstation running Kerberos client library. Holds $K_C$ derived from the user's password.
2. **KDC (AS + TGS):** Secure host that stores all long-term secrets in an encrypted Kerberos database. It brokers authentication.
3. **Application Servers (V1, V2, …, Vn):** Provide services to authenticated clients; each holds its own master key $K_{Vi}$ registered with the KDC.

**Three Exchanges (4 marks):**

| Exchange | Purpose | Initiator → Responder | Security Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AS Exchange** | Prove client identity, obtain TGT | C → AS | Bootstrap SSO credential |
| **TGS Exchange** | Exchange TGT for service ticket | C → TGS | Obtain access to specific service V |
| **AP Exchange** | Authenticate to target server | C → V | Establish authenticated session with V |

The **block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1) shows that AS and TGS share the secure Kerberos database, while V servers are registered with the KDC but reside in the operational realm.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Message-level breakdown (7 marks):**

**TGS Exchange:**
- $M_3 = \{ID_V,\; Ticket_{TGS},\; Authenticator_C\}$
- $Ticket_{TGS} = E_{K_{TGS}}[K_{C,TGS}, ID_C, AD_C, ID_{TGS}, TS_2, Lifetime_2]$
- $Authenticator_C = E_{K_{C,TGS}}[ID_C, AD_C, TS_3]$

- $M_4 = E_{K_{C,TGS}}[K_{C,V}, ID_V, TS_4, Ticket_V]$
- $Ticket_V = E_{K_V}[K_{C,V}, ID_C, AD_C, ID_V, TS_4, Lifetime_4]$

**AP Exchange:**
- $M_5 = \{Ticket_V,\; Authenticator'_C\}$
- $Authenticator'_C = E_{K_{C,V}}[ID_C, AD_C, TS_5]$

- $M_6 = E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5 + 1]$ *(optional mutual auth)*

**Cryptographic purposes (write each in your answer script):**
- $K_{C,V}$: **One-time session key** — never reused across different V's.
- $AD_C$ (Network Address): **Binds ticket to IP** — replayed ticket won't decrypt at attacker's IP.
- $TS_i$: **Freshness stamp** — defends against replay within the 5-minute clock skew window.
- $Lifetime_i$: **Bounds exposure** if a ticket is stolen (≤ 21 hours in V4).
- $+1$ in $M_6$: Prevents trivial reflection of the timestamp as a fake server response.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]**

> **Q (a)** List and explain the **design goals** of Kerberos Version 4. Discuss why a **trusted third-party** model is necessary in an open distributed network. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Describe the **replay defense mechanisms** used in Kerberos V4, including timestamp verification, the use of authenticators, the replay cache, and the role of the 5-minute clock skew window. Why is $M_6$ (the $+1$ timestamp return) required for **mutual authentication**? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — Design Goals (7 marks)

Kerberos V4 was designed to meet five principal goals:

1. **Security (2 marks):** Defend against passive eavesdropping and active replay on an open network. Achieved by never sending the password after login, encrypting all sensitive exchanges, and binding tickets to client addresses.
2. **Reliability (1 mark):** Eliminate the KDC as a single point of failure. Achieved by **replicating the KDC** using a master-slave model and short ticket lifetimes so that slave failures are recoverable.
3. **Transparency (1 mark):** The user should not need to type a password for every service. Achieved through the **TGT mechanism** — a single AS exchange enables access to many services.
4. **Scalability (1 mark):** The system must support thousands of clients and servers. Achieved by **hierarchical realms** and shared inter-realm keys.
5. **Single Sign-On (2 marks):** One authentication unlocks multiple services without re-entering credentials. This is the **central business driver** for Kerberos adoption in enterprise networks.

**Why trusted third-party:** In an open network, clients cannot securely share pairwise keys with every server. A **TTP** (the KDC) eliminates the $O(n^2)$ key-management problem by reducing it to $O(n)$ — every principal holds one key with the KDC.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — Replay Defense (7 marks)

Kerberos V4 uses a **layered replay defense:**

1. **Timestamps (2 marks):** Every Authenticator and Ticket contains a timestamp $TS_i$. The receiver computes $\delta = |T_{now} - TS_i|$. If $\delta > \Delta = 5$ minutes, the message is rejected as stale.
2. **Authenticators (2 marks):** Authenticators are freshly generated per exchange and encrypted under a session key, so an attacker cannot modify $TS_i$ to extend its validity.
3. **Replay Cache (1 mark):** The TGS and V maintain a cache of recently used timestamps. If $TS_i$ is found, the message is rejected as a duplicate replay.
4. **Network Address Binding (1 mark):** Tickets embed $AD_C$. If an attacker captures a ticket and tries to use it from a different IP, decryption of the Authenticator yields a mismatched $AD_C$ and the message is rejected.

**Mutual authentication via $M_6 = E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5 + 1]$ (1 mark):** After V accepts the client's Authenticator, V returns $TS_5 + 1$ encrypted under $K_{C,V}$. Only the legitimate V could have recovered $K_{C,V}$ from $Ticket_V$, so the client's ability to decrypt and observe $TS_5 + 1$ proves the responder is genuine. The $+1$ prevents the attacker from simply copying $TS_5$ back to the client.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Master Before Exam Day**

- [ ] **Kerberos V4** = symmetric-key trusted third-party authentication protocol from **MIT Project Athena**.
- [ ] **Three principal entities:** Client (C), Authentication Server (AS), Ticket-Granting Server (TGS), Target Server (V).
- [ ] **KDC = AS + TGS**; both share the secure Kerberos database.
- [ ] **Three exchanges:** **AS Exchange** (get TGT), **TGS Exchange** (get service ticket), **AP Exchange** (use service).
- [ ] **Two tokens per access:** **Ticket** (reusable, server-encrypted) + **Authenticator** (fresh, session-key-encrypted).
- [ ] **Ticket format:** $E_{K_X}[K_{session}, ID_C, AD_C, ID_X, TS, Lifetime]$.
- [ ] **Authenticator format:** $E_{K_{session}}[ID_C, AD_C, TS]$.
- [ ] **Symmetric cipher:** **DES in CBC mode** (V4); 56-bit key, 64-bit block.
- [ ] **Clock skew:** $\Delta = 5$ minutes (300 seconds).
- [ ] **Ticket lifetime cap:** **21 hours** in V4 (due to 32-bit time field constraint).
- [ ] **Mutual authentication:** $M_6 = E_{K_{C,V}}[TS_5 + 1]$.
- [ ] **Replay defense layers:** Timestamps + Authenticators + Replay cache + Address binding.
- [ ] **V4 weaknesses** to memorize: DES-only, rigid IP assumptions, no delegation, 21-hour limit, weak cross-realm — **all fixed in V5** (AES, ASN.1, renewable tickets, proxy tickets, transitive trust).
- [ ] **Kerberos port:** UDP/88 in V4; TCP/88 added in V5.
- [ ] **Real-world deployment:** Foundation of **Microsoft Active Directory**, **Apple OpenDirectory**, **Hadoop security**, **NFSv4**.
- [ ] **Key terms to define in 2-mark questions:** *Single Sign-On (SSO)*, *Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT)*, *Authenticator*, *Realm*, *Principal*.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Kerberos Version 4 Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECAT863 Network Security Protocols | Outcome-Based Education compliant*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kerberos Version 5
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kerberos Version 5

# Kerberos Version 5 — A Trust-Based Authentication Protocol

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> **Kerberos Version 5** is a trusted third-party network authentication protocol standardised under **RFC 4120** and **RFC 4556** (PKINIT). It is the internet-standard successor to Kerberos Version 4 (MIT Athena Project), developed to authenticate clients and servers across an insecure network using **symmetric-key cryptography** and **tickets** issued by a centralised authority. Version 5 corrects the cryptographic, encoding, and scalability limitations of Version 4 while remaining backward-incompatible at the wire format.

## 1.1 Why Kerberos Exists — The Core Problem

In a distributed client–server environment (e.g., campus LAN, enterprise network, cloud VPC), a user `Alice` running a workstation `W` must prove her identity to multiple application servers `S1, S2, S3`. The classical pitfalls are:

- **Password sniffing** on plaintext protocols (Telnet, FTP, rlogin).
- **Replay attacks** where an attacker captures and re-sends a credential.
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks between client and server.
- **Single Point of Compromise** if passwords are stored on every server.

Kerberos solves these by introducing a **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** that holds long-term secret keys for all principals (users and services) and issues short-lived **tickets** encrypted under these keys.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Airport Lounge Analogy

Imagine you want to enter a **VIP airport lounge** but you do not know the lounge staff.

1. You go to the **airport security counter (KDC / AS)** with your passport. They check your ID and give you a **boarding-pass-style slip** (TGT) encrypted so that no one except the lounge network can read it.
2. When you reach the lounge entrance, the doorman (TGS) inspects the slip, confirms you are authorised, and gives you a **one-day lounge pass** (Service Ticket) for a specific airline service.
3. You present the **lounge pass + a fresh photo** (Authenticator) at the lounge. The pass proves the *server* trusts the issuing authority, and the photo proves the *ticket-bearer* is alive now (anti-replay).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three things make Kerberos V5 work:**
> 1. **Tickets** — issued by a trusted third party (KDC), time-limited, encrypted with the target's secret key.
> 2. **Authenticators** — short-lived, prove the client is live, encrypted with the session key.
> 3. **Time synchronisation** — every Kerberos ticket has a tight validity window; **clock skew of 5 minutes is the standard tolerance**; outside this, replay is assumed possible.

## 1.3 Kerberos Version 5 — Standardised Vocabulary

| Term | Standard Definition (RFC 4120) |
|---|---|
| **Principal** | A uniquely named client or service; e.g., `alice@EXAMPLE.COM` |
| **Realm** | An administrative authentication domain; conventionally a DNS domain in upper-case |
| **KDC** | Key Distribution Center = **AS (Authentication Server) + TGS (Ticket Granting Server)** |
| **TGT** | Ticket-Granting Ticket issued by AS, used to request service tickets from TGS |
| **Ticket** | Encrypted blob granting a client access to a specific service |
| **Authenticator** | Per-connection proof-of-liveness created by the client |
| **Session Key** | Short-term symmetric key shared between two parties (Client–TGS, Client–Server) |
| **Cross-Realm** | Authentication spanning multiple administrative realms via a chain of TGS hops |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact**
> Kerberos V5 uses **ASN.1 Distinguished Encoding Rules (DER)** for message serialisation, whereas V4 used a proprietary non-standard format. ASN.1 enables interoperability with other IETF protocols like **X.509 / PKI**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis of Kerberos Version 5

## 2.1 Architectural Components

The Kerberos V5 architecture has three logical entities and a small set of messages:

1. **Client (C)** — A user or software acting on a user's behalf. Holds the long-term secret key `K_C` (typically derived from a password).
2. **Authentication Server (AS)** — Authenticates the client and issues a TGT. The AS shares `K_C` with the client and `K_TGS` with the TGS.
3. **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)** — Accepts a TGT and issues service tickets. Shares `K_TGS` with the AS and `K_S` with each service `S`.
4. **Service Server (S)** — Accepts service tickets and grants access to the requested resource. Shares `K_S` with the KDC.

The combination **AS + TGS + database of secrets** is called the **KDC**.

## 2.2 The Three-Phase Authentication Dance

### Phase 1 — Authentication Service Exchange (AS-REQ / AS-REP)
The client proves its identity to the KDC and obtains a TGT.

- Client → AS: `AS-REQ = (PrincipalName, TGS_Realm, TGS_Name, Lifetime, Nonce_1, [PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP])`
- AS → Client: `AS-REP = (Realm_C, Name_C, TGS_ticket, Enc{K_CT}[K_C_TGS, Realm_TGS, Name_TGS, Lifetime, Nonce_1, Flags, Time_stamp])`

Where the TGT has the form:

$$
\text{TGT} = T_{C,TGS} = Enc\{K_{TGS}\}\big[K_{C,TGS},\ Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ Flags,\ AuthTime,\ StartTime,\ EndTime,\ RenewTill,\ Transited\big]
$$

### Phase 2 — Ticket-Granting Service Exchange (TGS-REQ / TGS-REP)
The client exchanges the TGT for a service ticket.

- Client → TGS: `TGS-REQ = (Service_Realm, Service_Name, TGS_ticket, Authenticator_{C,TGS})`
- TGS → Client: `TGS-REP = (Realm_C, Name_C, Service_ticket, Enc{K_C_TGS}[K_C_S, Service_Realm, Service_Name, Lifetime, Nonce_2, Flags, Time_stamp])`

The client-to-TGS **authenticator** is:

$$
A_{C,TGS} = Enc\{K_{C,TGS}\}\big[Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ CTime,\ CSeq\big]
$$

### Phase 3 — Client/Server Authentication Exchange (AP-REQ / AP-REP)
The client presents the service ticket to the application server.

- Client → S: `AP-REQ = (Service_ticket, Authenticator_{C,S})`
- S → Client (optional): `AP-REP = Enc{K_C_S}[CTime_2, CSeq_2, SubKey_2]`

The service ticket is:

$$
T_{C,S} = Enc\{K_{S}\}\big[K_{C,S},\ Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ Flags,\ AuthTime,\ StartTime,\ EndTime,\ Transited,\ CAddr\big]
$$

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Exists |
|---|---|
| AS first issues a TGT, not a service ticket | Single sign-on: user authenticates once, then obtains many service tickets without re-entering password |
| TGT is encrypted with `K_TGS`, not `K_C` | Client must not be able to read or modify the TGT — prevents tampering with session keys or flags |
| Client receives `Enc{K_C_TGS}[…]` | Encrypted under a session key the AS has just created — the client must possess `K_C` to decrypt it (proves knowledge of password) |
| Authenticator is encrypted with `K_C_TGS` or `K_C_S` | Proves the client holds the current session key — defeats offline forgery |
| Authenticators include timestamp + nonce | Defeats replay attacks within a small clock window |
| Tickets include `CAddr` (client addresses) | Server only honours ticket from listed IP/MAC — defeats ticket theft over network |
| All tickets carry `EndTime` | Limits blast radius if a ticket is compromised |

## 2.4 Ticket Flags in Kerberos V5 (RFC 4120 §5.2.8)

Kerberos V5 introduces a powerful set of bit flags, absent in Version 4:

| Bit Flag | Bit Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `forwardable` | 1 | TGT may be forwarded to a different address |
| `forwarded` | 2 | TGT was forwarded |
| `proxiable` | 3 | Service ticket may be proxied |
| `proxy` | 4 | Service ticket is a proxy |
| `may-postdate` | 5 | Service ticket may be postdated |
| `postdated` | 6 | Service ticket is postdated |
| `invalid` | 7 | Invalid ticket (must be validated by KDC) |
| `renewable` | 8 | Ticket can be renewed |
| `initial` | 9 | Ticket was issued by AS (not TGS) |
| `pre-authent` | 10 | Pre-authentication was performed |
| `hw-authent` | 11 | Hardware pre-authentication used |
| `transited-policy-checked` | 12 | Transited policy has been applied |
| `ok-as-delegate` | 13 | Target is trusted for delegation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**
> **`renewable` tickets** are unique to V5. A renewable ticket carries a normal `EndTime` plus a `RenewTill` field. The client can request a new ticket (same session key) from the TGS before `EndTime` without re-authenticating, until `RenewTill` is reached.

## 2.5 Pre-Authentication — Defeating Offline Dictionary Attacks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **V4 Weakness:** Version 4 did not require the client to prove knowledge of `K_C` before the AS responded. An attacker could send an `AS-REQ` for any principal and receive an AS-REP encrypted with `K_C`. They could then mount an **offline dictionary attack** to guess the password.
>
> **V5 Fix:** The standard `PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP` pre-authentication data type requires the client to send, in `AS-REQ`, an encryption of the current timestamp under `K_C`. The AS decrypts, checks the clock skew, and only then issues the TGT. Some deployments (e.g., Active Directory) make this **mandatory**, eliminating the offline attack vector on the AS-REP.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the notations below. They are used in every derivation on Kerberos.**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `C` | Client (principal) |
| `S` | Service server |
| `AS` | Authentication Server |
| `TGS` | Ticket-Granting Server |
| `KDC` | Key Distribution Center = AS + TGS |
| `K_X` | Long-term secret key of principal `X` (e.g., derived from password) |
| `K_X_Y` | Short-term session key shared between `X` and `Y` |
| `K_TGS` | Long-term key shared between AS and TGS |
| `N_i` | Nonce generated by client to ensure freshness |
| `T_X` | Ticket issued to `X` for use with server `Y` (e.g., `T_{C,TGS}`) |
| `A_{X,Y}` | Authenticator from `X` to `Y`, encrypted with `K_{X,Y}` |
| `Enc{K}[m]` | Encryption of message `m` under key `K` |
| `CTime`, `CSeq` | Client timestamp and sequence number inside authenticator |
| `AS-REQ` | Authentication Server Request |
| `AS-REP` | Authentication Server Reply |
| `TGS-REQ` | Ticket-Granting Server Request |
| `TGS-REP` | Ticket-Granting Server Reply |
| `AP-REQ` | Application Server Request |
| `AP-REP` | Application Server Reply (optional mutual auth) |
| `CAddr` | Client network addresses (anti-theft) |
| `KVNO` | Key Version Number (key rotation support) |
| `Transited` | List of realms traversed (cross-realm) |
| `RenewTill` | Absolute deadline for ticket renewal |
| `Lifetime` | Validity duration of a ticket |

| Master Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|
| $\text{AS-REQ} = (C,\ TGS,\ N_1)$ | Client requests TGT |
| $\text{AS-REP} = (T_{C,TGS},\ Enc\{K_C\}[K_{C,TGS},\ N_1])$ | AS replies with TGT + encrypted session key |
| $\text{TGS-REQ} = (S,\ T_{C,TGS},\ A_{C,TGS})$ | Client requests service ticket |
| $\text{TGS-REP} = (T_{C,S},\ Enc\{K_{C,TGS}\}[K_{C,S},\ N_2])$ | TGS replies with service ticket + session key |
| $\text{AP-REQ} = (T_{C,S},\ A_{C,S})$ | Client authenticates to service |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kerberos V5 is the **de-facto authentication backbone** of:
- **Microsoft Active Directory** (every domain controller is a KDC)
- **MIT Athena** (the original use case)
- **Hadoop / Big-Data ecosystems** (HDFS, Hive, Spark secure mode)
- **AWS EKS / Linux SSH** (via `pam_krb5`)
- **Apple macOS / iOS enterprise single sign-on**

It is the practical demonstration of **Needham–Schroeder symmetric-key protocol** in production, and forms the basis for understanding modern federated systems like SAML, OAuth, and WebAuthN.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Exchange Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Full Derivational Walkthrough of the Kerberos V5 Protocol

The most common 2-party (single-realm) flow has **six messages** across three exchanges. We derive each step.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Client Constructs AS-REQ

The client `C` wants to obtain a Ticket-Granting Ticket for the TGS of realm `R`. It generates a fresh nonce `N_1` to guarantee freshness of the response (defeats replay of old AS-REPs).

$$
\text{AS-REQ} = \big( \text{PrincipalName}: C,\ \text{Realm}: R,\ \text{ServerName}: \text{TGS},\ \text{AS-REQ-Body},\ \text{PA-PreAuth} \big)
$$

With

$$
\text{PA-PreAuth} = Enc\{K_C\}\big[\text{CTime}_{\text{PA}},\ \text{CSeq}_{\text{PA}}\big]
$$

This is the **pre-authentication** data — it proves `C` knows `K_C` *before* receiving the TGT. If this is missing (or `pre-authent` flag is mandatory), the KDC replies with `KRB-ERROR: KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED`.

> **Why this matters:** The AS does not want to give a TGT to anyone who does not know `K_C`. Without pre-auth, an attacker could `spray` AS-REQs and crack passwords offline.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — AS Constructs AS-REP

The AS verifies the pre-auth timestamp (must be within ±5 minutes of AS's clock). If valid, the AS:

1. Generates a fresh **client-TGS session key** `K_{C,TGS}` (randomly chosen from the KDC's CSPRNG).
2. Creates the TGT:

$$
T_{C,TGS} = Enc\{K_{TGS}\}\big[K_{C,TGS},\ Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ Flags,\ AuthTime,\ StartTime,\ EndTime,\ RenewTill,\ Transited\big]
$$

3. Encrypts the session key under the client's long-term key `K_C`:

$$
\text{Part}_B = Enc\{K_C\}\big[K_{C,TGS},\ Realm_TGS,\ Name_TGS,\ Lifetime,\ N_1,\ Flags,\ AuthTime,\ StartTime,\ EndTime,\ RenewTill\big]
$$

4. Sends:

$$
\text{AS-REP} = \big( Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ T_{C,TGS},\ \text{Part}_B \big)
$$

> **Why two parts?** The client must decrypt `Part_B` using `K_C` to recover `K_{C,TGS}`. The TGT is opaque to the client (encrypted with `K_TGS` it does not know) — preventing the client from forging or modifying it.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Client Decrypts AS-REP and Constructs TGS-REQ

The client receives the AS-REP, decrypts `Part_B` using its own `K_C`, and obtains:

- `K_{C,TGS}` — the session key shared with the TGS
- Verification that `N_1` matches the nonce it sent (replay protection)
- Validity times

The client then constructs an **authenticator** for the TGS:

$$
A_{C,TGS} = Enc\{K_{C,TGS}\}\big[Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ CTime,\ CSeq\big]
$$

And builds the TGS-REQ:

$$
\text{TGS-REQ} = \big( Realm_S,\ Name_S,\ T_{C,TGS},\ A_{C,TGS} \big)
$$

> **Why the authenticator is separate from the ticket:** The TGT proves the KDC trusts the client *as of* `AuthTime`. The authenticator proves the client is **alive right now** (and possesses `K_{C,TGS}`). Together they give **liveness + authorisation**.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — TGS Constructs TGS-REP

The TGS:
1. Decrypts `T_{C,TGS}` using `K_{TGS}` to recover `K_{C,TGS}` and ticket flags.
2. Decrypts `A_{C,TGS}` using `K_{C,TGS}`. If the principal name in the authenticator matches that in the ticket, AND the timestamp is within clock skew, the client is authenticated.
3. Generates a fresh **client-server session key** `K_{C,S}`.
4. Creates the service ticket:

$$
T_{C,S} = Enc\{K_S\}\big[K_{C,S},\ Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ Flags,\ AuthTime,\ StartTime,\ EndTime,\ CAddr\big]
$$

5. Encrypts `K_{C,S}` under `K_{C,TGS}`:

$$
\text{Part}_D = Enc\{K_{C,TGS}\}\big[K_{C,S},\ Realm_S,\ Name_S,\ Lifetime,\ N_2,\ Flags,\ AuthTime,\ StartTime,\ EndTime\big]
$$

6. Sends:

$$
\text{TGS-REP} = \big( Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ T_{C,S},\ \text{Part}_D \big)
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Client Constructs AP-REQ

The client decrypts `Part_D` using `K_{C,TGS}` to recover `K_{C,S}`. It then builds a fresh authenticator for the service:

$$
A_{C,S} = Enc\{K_{C,S}\}\big[Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ CTime_2,\ CSeq_2,\ SubKey_{\text{opt}}\big]
$$

The optional `SubKey` allows the client and server to negotiate a fresh sub-session key for the application data, distinct from the Kerberos session key — a **defence-in-depth** measure.

The client sends:

$$
\text{AP-REQ} = \big( \text{AP-REQ-Header},\ T_{C,S},\ A_{C,S} \big)
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Server Validates and Replies (Mutual Authentication)

The server `S`:
1. Decrypts `T_{C,S}` using `K_S`, obtaining `K_{C,S}` and ticket parameters.
2. Checks the `CAddr` field — does the source IP match?
3. Decrypts `A_{C,S}` using `K_{C,S}` and verifies the principal, timestamp, and sequence.
4. If all checks pass, accepts the client.
5. (Optional but recommended) Sends `AP-REP` for **mutual authentication** so the client knows the server is genuine:

$$
\text{AP-REP} = Enc\{K_{C,S}\}\big[CTime_2 + 1,\ CSeq_2,\ SubKey_2\big]
$$

> **Why mutual auth is optional but recommended:** In the basic flow, the client can be fooled by a fake server that simply ignores the request. The `AP-REP` proves the server decrypted `K_{C,S}` — i.e., knows `K_S` — hence is genuine.

## 3.2 Cross-Realm Authentication Derivation

In a multi-realm deployment (e.g., `EXAMPLE.COM` user wants `MAIL.EXAMPLE.ORG` service), the client must traverse a **path of TGS servers** through intermediate realms.

Let the path be $R_0 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow R_n$ where $R_0$ is the client's realm and $R_n$ is the service's realm. The client executes a sequence of TGS exchanges:

$$
\text{AS-REQ/REP} \rightarrow \text{TGS-REQ/REP}_{R_0 \to R_1} \rightarrow \text{TGS-REQ/REP}_{R_1 \to R_2} \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \text{AP-REQ}_{R_n}
$$

The **transited field** of the final service ticket encodes the path:

$$
\text{Transited} = R_1 \to R_2 \to \dots \to R_{n-1}
$$

The TGS at each hop encrypts the next service ticket with a **cross-realm session key** `K_{R_{i-1}, R_i}` shared between adjacent realms' KDCs. This avoids the quadratic blow-up of requiring every realm to share a key with every other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transitivity Pitfall:** RFC 4120 mandates that the receiving KDC applies a **transit policy check** to ensure that the traversed realms are trusted. In Active Directory, this is the "trust transitive" flag. Mis-configured trusts allow **transitive trust abuse** — a known real-world attack vector.

## 3.3 Renewable Ticket Derivation

A renewable ticket carries:

$$
T_{C,S}^{ren} = Enc\{K_S\}\big[K_{C,S},\ \dots,\ AuthTime,\ StartTime,\ EndTime,\ \mathbf{RenewTill},\ \mathbf{renewable}\big]
$$

Validity:
- $StartTime \le t < EndTime$ → usable as-is.
- $EndTime \le t < RenewTill$ → only renewable. Client sends `KRB-TGS-REQ` with service `krbtgt/R_n@R_0` and `renew` flag. TGS re-issues the ticket with the same session key, new `EndTime`, same `RenewTill`.

This means a long-lived service (e.g., a backup job running for 30 hours) can keep a single session key alive for 7 days (typical `RenewTill`) without re-authenticating with the password.

## 3.4 Key Version Number (KVNO) Rotation

When a client's password is changed, the KDC issues a new `K_C'` and increments the `KVNO`. Old tickets still carry the old `KVNO` and are valid until expiry, but new tickets use the new key. The TGS/S tracks the `KVNO` of `K_X` to determine which key to use for decryption. This permits **password change without invalidating all current sessions**.

$$
T_{C,S}^{old} = Enc\{K_S,\ KVNO=K_S^{old}\}\big[\dots\big]
$$

$$
T_{C,S}^{new} = Enc\{K_S,\ KVNO=K_S^{new}\}\big[\dots\big]
$$

The server keeps both copies keyed by `KVNO`. This is a major improvement over V4, which had no key versioning.

## 3.5 Mutual Authentication — Formal Correctness

Claim: The Kerberos V5 `AP-REQ` + `AP-REP` pair achieves mutual authentication under the **Dolev–Yao adversary model** *iff* the following hold:

1. `K_S` is known only to `S` and the TGS.
2. The TGS has correctly authenticated the client before issuing `T_{C,S}`.
3. `CTime_2` is monotonically increasing across the client's sessions.
4. Clock skew $\delta \le 5$ minutes is enforced by both client and server.
5. `CAddr` is enforced by `S` (binds ticket to client network address).

Proof sketch (by contradiction): Assume an adversary `E` impersonates `S` to `C`. To succeed, `E` must reply with a valid `AP-REP = Enc{K_{C,S}}[CTime_2+1, CSeq_2]`. But `E` does not know `K_{C,S}` (it is fresh in the encrypted TGT that only `S` can decrypt) and cannot decrypt `K_{C,S}` without `K_S`. Therefore `E` cannot produce a valid `AP-REP`, contradicting the assumption. ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Kerberos V5 Authentication — Full Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the three-exchange, six-message flow between the four logical entities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef client fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef aserver fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef tgsserver fill:#14532d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef appserver fill:#581c87,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef secret fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000

    C["CLIENT C"]:::client
    AS["AUTHENTICATION SERVER AS"]:::aserver
    TGS["TICKET GRANTING SERVER TGS"]:::tgsserver
    S["SERVICE SERVER S"]:::appserver
    DB[("KDC SECRET DATABASE: KC, KTGS, KS")]:::secret

    C -- "AS-REQ Principal C plus Nonce N1 plus PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP" --> AS
    AS -. "lookup KC, KTGS" .-> DB
    DB -. "return long-term keys" .-> AS
    AS -- "AS-REP T C TGS plus Enc KC K C TGS K C TGS N1 Flags" --> C

    C -- "TGS-REQ T C TGS plus A C TGS encrypted with K C TGS" --> TGS
    TGS -. "verify TGT using KTGS" .-> TGS
    TGS -- "TGS-REP T C S plus Enc K C TGS K C S N2 Flags" --> C

    C -- "AP-REQ T C S plus A C S encrypted with K C S" --> S
    S -. "decrypt T C S using KS" .-> S
    S -- "AP-REP optional mutual auth Enc K C S CTime2 plus 1" --> C

    style C font-size:14px
    style AS font-size:14px
    style TGS font-size:14px
    style S font-size:14px
    style DB font-size:14px
```

## 4.2 Kerberos V5 Ticket Internal Structure (RFC 4120 ASN.1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef field fill:#0f766e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef grp fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef key fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#000,color:#000

    AUTH["authtime KerberosTime"]:::field
    STRT["starttime KerberosTime OPTIONAL"]:::field
    END["endtime KerberosTime"]:::field
    RNW["renew till KerberosTime OPTIONAL"]:::field
    ADDR["caddr HostAddresses OPTIONAL"]:::field
    AUTHD["authorization data SEQUENCE OPTIONAL"]:::field

    TKT --> TVNO
    TKT --> TR
    TKT --> TS
    TKT --> FLG
    TKT --> KBL
    TKT --> CR
    TKT --> CN
    TKT --> TRAN
    TKT --> AUTH

    style TKT font-size:14px

```

## 4.3 Cross-Realm Authentication Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef client fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef realmA fill:#0f766e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef realmB fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    CC -- "1 AS REQ AS R0" --> K0
    K0 -- "2 AS REP TGT for R0" --> CC

    K2 -- "6 TGS REP service ticket to MAIL R2" --> CC
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Explain the three-way authentication exchange in Kerberos Version 5. List the three logical servers involved. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Kerberos Version 5 performs authentication through **three logical servers** and **six messages** in three exchanges:

1. **Authentication Service (AS) Exchange** — Client sends `AS-REQ` with pre-auth data; AS replies with `AS-REP` containing the **Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT)** encrypted under `K_TGS`, plus a `K_{C,TGS}` session key encrypted under `K_C`.

2. **Ticket-Granting Service (TGS) Exchange** — Client sends `TGS-REQ` containing the TGT and an authenticator encrypted with `K_{C,TGS}`; TGS replies with `TGS-REP` containing a **service ticket** encrypted under `K_S` and `K_{C,S}` encrypted under `K_{C,TGS}`.

3. **Client/Server (AP) Exchange** — Client sends `AP-REQ` containing the service ticket and an authenticator encrypted with `K_{C,S}`; server optionally replies with `AP-REP` for mutual authentication.

The three logical servers are: **Authentication Server (AS)**, **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)**, and the **Service Server (S)**. Together, AS + TGS form the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**.

**(3 Marks — exact terminology required)**

---

### Question 2. State any **three** differences between Kerberos Version 4 and Version 5. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Sl No | Kerberos Version 4 | Kerberos Version 5 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses a proprietary, non-standard message encoding | Uses **ASN.1 (DER)** encoding per RFC 4120 |
| 2 | Only supports **DES** as the encryption algorithm | Supports pluggable encryption types — **AES-128/256, RC4, DES, 3DES** |
| 3 | No **pre-authentication** — vulnerable to offline dictionary attack on AS-REP | Mandatory **PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP** pre-authentication defeats offline attack |
| 4 | No renewable tickets | **Renewable tickets** with `RenewTill` field |
| 5 | No ticket flags | **13-bit flag field** (forwardable, proxiable, etc.) |
| 6 | No key versioning | Supports **KVNO** for password change without re-issuing all tickets |
| 7 | Weak/limited cross-realm support | **Transited field** with full transitive-trust policy |

(Any 3, 1 mark each) — **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A.** (14 Marks)
#### (a) Describe in detail the **Ticket-Granting Service (TGS) Exchange** of Kerberos V5, including the structure of the TGS-REQ and TGS-REP messages. Show the message flow diagram. (7 Marks)
#### (b) Explain the concept of **Pre-Authentication in Kerberos V5**. Why was it introduced? How does it defeat offline dictionary attacks on the AS-REP? (7 Marks)

---

#### (a) Model Solution — TGS-Exchange Detail

**1. Purpose:** The TGS exchange converts a **TGT** (proof of identity to the realm) into a **service ticket** (proof of authorisation for a specific service).

**2. Message 1 — TGS-REQ (Client → TGS):**

$$
\text{TGS-REQ} = \big( \text{pvno} = 5,\ \text{msg-type} = 4,\ \text{padata: PA-TGS-REQ},\ \text{req-body} \big)
$$

Where `req-body = (KDCOptions, ServerName, Realm, From, Till, Nonce, EncryptionType, Addresses, EncAuthorizationData, AdditionalTickets)`.

The actual TGT is wrapped inside `PA-TGS-REQ` and the authenticator `A_{C,TGS}` is inside the TGS-REQ body encrypted with `K_{C,TGS}`:

$$
A_{C,TGS} = Enc\{K_{C,TGS}\}\big[Realm_C,\ Name_C,\ CTime_4,\ CSeq_4,\ Subkey_{\text{opt}}\big]
$$

**3. TGS Processing:** The TGS:
- Decrypts the TGT using its own `K_TGS` to recover `K_{C,TGS}` and ticket parameters.
- Decrypts the authenticator using `K_{C,TGS}`.
- Compares the principal in the authenticator with that in the TGT.
- Verifies the timestamp is within clock skew and that `CAddr` matches.
- Generates a fresh `K_{C,S}`.

**4. Message 2 — TGS-REP (TGS → Client):**

$$
\text{TGS-REP} = \big( \text{pvno} = 5,\ \text{msg-type} = 5,\ \text{padata},\ \text{crealm},\ \text{cname},\ \text{ticket},\ \text{enc-part} \big)
$$

Where:

- `ticket = T_{C,S} = Enc{K_S}[K_{C,S}, Realm_C, Name_C, Flags, AuthTime, StartTime, EndTime, CAddr, Transited]`
- `enc-part = Enc{K_{C,TGS}}[K_{C,S}, Realm_S, Name_S, Lifetime, Nonce, Flags, AuthTime, StartTime, EndTime]`

**5. Message Flow Diagram (text form):**

```
Client C                                       TGS
   |                                             |
   |--- TGS-REQ: { T_{C,TGS}, A_{C,TGS} } ------>|
   |                                             |
   |  (Decrypt TGT with K_TGS)                   |
   |  (Decrypt A with K_{C,TGS})                 |
   |  (Verify principal match)                   |
   |  (Check timestamp + CAddr)                  |
   |  (Generate K_{C,S})                         |
   |                                             |
   |<-- TGS-REP: { T_{C,S}, Enc{K_{C,TGS}}[...] }-|
   |                                             |
(Decrypt with K_{C,TGS} → recover K_{C,S})
```

**Marks Distribution:**
- TGS-REQ structure and components: 2 marks
- TGS processing steps: 2 marks
- TGS-REP structure: 2 marks
- Diagram: 1 mark
**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Pre-Authentication

**1. Definition:** Pre-Authentication is a Kerberos V5 mechanism where the client must **prove knowledge of its long-term key** `K_C` to the AS **before** the AS issues a TGT. The proof is typically a timestamp encrypted under `K_C`.

**2. Why introduced?** In V4, the AS would issue a TGT to *any* client that requested one, encrypted under the requested principal's `K_C`. An attacker could:

- Send `AS-REQ` for principal `victim`.
- Receive `AS-REP` containing the TGT (useless to attacker) **and** `Enc{K_C}[K_{C,TGS}, N_1, ...]`.
- Mount an **offline dictionary attack**: try candidate passwords $p$, derive `K_C = KDF(p)`, attempt to decrypt, and check if the result is well-formed.

Since the decryption could be done offline, the attacker only needed a single AS-REQ to crack weak passwords.

**3. V5 Solution — PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP:** The client must include in `AS-REQ`:

$$
\text{PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP} = Enc\{K_C\}\big[\text{CTime}_{\text{PA}},\ \text{CSeq}_{\text{PA}}\big]
$$

The AS decrypts this using its stored `K_C`, verifies the timestamp is within ±5 minutes of the AS clock, and only then proceeds. An attacker who does not know `K_C` cannot construct a valid pre-auth value.

**4. Defeating Offline Dictionary Attack:** Without a valid `PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP`, the AS refuses the request and returns `KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED`. The attacker therefore cannot obtain an `Enc{K_C}[...]` blob to crack. The protocol is now **forward-authenticating**: the AS authenticates the client before releasing any data encrypted under `K_C`.

**5. Active Directory Implementation:** Microsoft Active Directory makes pre-authentication **mandatory** by default. The only commonly-allowed exception is "Trust" principals in cross-domain referrals, which require explicit configuration.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition and rationale: 2 marks
- V4 attack description: 2 marks
- PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP mechanism: 2 marks
- Defeat of offline attack + AD context: 1 mark
**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B.** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
#### (a) Explain the structure of a **Kerberos V5 Ticket** and **Authenticator** as defined in RFC 4120. State the difference between them. (7 Marks)
#### (b) Describe **Cross-Realm Authentication** in Kerberos V5. What is the role of the **Transited field**? How does it prevent transitive-trust abuse? (7 Marks)

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Ticket vs Authenticator

**1. Ticket Structure (RFC 4120 §5.3):**

$$
T_{C,S} = Enc\{K_S\}\big[\ t\text{-vno},\ realm,\ sname,\ flags,\ key,\ crealm,\ cname,\ transited,\ authtime,\ starttime,\ endtime,\ renew\text{-}till,\ caddr,\ authorization\text{-}data\ \big]
$$

Field meanings:
- `t-vno` — Ticket version (5).
- `realm`, `sname` — Service realm and service principal name.
- `flags` — 13-bit policy word.
- `key` — `K_{C,S}` session key, given to server so it can decrypt the authenticator.
- `crealm`, `cname` — Client identity (immutable, set by KDC).
- `transited` — Realm path (for cross-realm).
- `authtime`, `starttime`, `endtime`, `renew-till` — Time validity.
- `caddr` — Client network addresses (binding ticket to a host).
- `authorization-data` — Optional PAC-like data (Microsoft PAC, authorization strings).

**2. Authenticator Structure (RFC 4120 §5.5.1):**

$$
A_{C,S} = Enc\{K_{C,S}\}\big[\ authenticator\text{-}vno,\ crealm,\ cname,\ cksum,\ cusec,\ ctime,\ subkey,\ seq\text{-}number,\ authorization\text{-}data\ \big]
$$

- `crealm`, `cname` — Client identity (must match ticket).
- `cusec`, `ctime` — Client micro-second + timestamp (anti-replay).
- `cksum` — Optional checksum over the application data (channel binding).
- `subkey` — Optional sub-session key for the application.
- `seq-number` — Optional monotonic counter.

**3. Key Differences:**

| Property | Ticket | Authenticator |
|---|---|---|
| Issued by | KDC (AS or TGS) | Client itself |
| Encrypted with | Service's long-term key `K_S` | Session key `K_{C,S}` |
| Validity | Hours/days (per ticket) | Seconds (5-min skew) |
| Reusable | Yes, until expiry | **No** — single use per session |
| Purpose | Authorisation | Liveness + freshness |
| Contents immutable by client | Yes | Yes (created by client) |

**4. Why two separate structures?**
- The **ticket** is *reusable* and is presented to the server, but the server cannot tell if the presenter is the original client.
- The **authenticator** proves the presenter holds `K_{C,S}`, hence is the legitimate client.
- Separating concerns enables **single sign-on**: the client obtains one TGT and one ticket per service, but creates a fresh authenticator for each connection.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Ticket structure: 2 marks
- Authenticator structure: 2 marks
- Tabulated differences: 2 marks
- Justification of design: 1 mark
**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Cross-Realm Authentication

**1. Problem:** In a multi-realm environment, the client's realm `R_0` and the service's realm `R_n` may not share a direct secret. We need a chain of trust.

**2. Cross-Realm Path:** The client `C` follows a path of TGS servers:

$$
R_0 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow R_n
$$

Each adjacent pair of realms has a shared **inter-realm key** `K_{R_{i-1}, R_i}`, set up by administrative agreement ("trust").

**3. Per-hop Exchange:**

- At each hop, the client sends the TGT to the next realm's TGS.
- The TGS verifies the ticket, then issues a new ticket whose `crealm` is still `R_0` and whose `transited` field is updated.
- The new ticket is encrypted under the next realm's key.

**4. Transited Field:** A `SEQUENCE OF EncryptedData` listing the realms traversed in order, encoded as:

$$
\text{Transited} = \text{Enc}\{K_{R_n}\}\big[\ R_1 \to R_2 \to \dots \to R_{n-1}\ \big]
$$

The service realm's KDC encrypts the path under its own master key. When the final service receives the ticket, it can decrypt the transited field and apply its **transit policy** to verify that the path is acceptable.

**5. Defence Against Transitive Trust Abuse:**

- **RFC 4120 §5.2.6** mandates that the receiving KDC enforce a configurable **transit policy** (in Microsoft AD, controlled by the `TrustedDomain` and `TrustAttributes`).
- The `transited-policy-checked` flag indicates that the KDC has already verified the path.
- The path is bound into the encrypted ticket, so it cannot be modified by intermediate realms.
- Microsoft AD additionally requires the **`transitive` flag** to be set on each inter-realm trust for the chain to work — non-transitive trusts break the chain, preventing unintended trust extension.

**6. Real-World Concern — "Pass-the-Ticket across Realms":** Without proper transit policy, a compromised realm `R_1` could issue tickets to any other realm that trusts `R_1` transitively. The transited field plus policy check ensures each hop is auditable.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Cross-realm problem and path concept: 2 marks
- Per-hop exchange description: 2 marks
- Transited field structure and encoding: 1 mark
- Transit policy + abuse prevention: 2 marks
**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Carefully**
>
