> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Revocation # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Certificate Revocation** is the process of invalidating a previously issued digital certificate **before its scheduled expiration date**, thereby declaring it **untrusted** and unfit for further use in a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). In a PKI ecosystem, a certificate is assumed valid for its entire stated validity period (e.g., the `Not Before` and `Not After` fields in X.509). However, real-world conditions force premature invalidation. Revocation is the **authoritative mechanism** through which a **Certification Authority (CA)** or an authorized entity signals that a specific certificate (identified by its unique **Serial Number**) must no longer be accepted by relying parties. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Definition (Memorize This Verbatim):** > Revocation is the act of removing the binding between a public key and an identity before the certificate's expiration, typically because the private key has been compromised, the subject's affiliation has changed, or the issuing CA itself has been compromised. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cancelled Passport" Imagine a **passport office** issues you a 10-year passport. By all visible signs, the document looks valid. But suppose midway through the validity period: | Real-World Event | Certificate Equivalent | |---|---| | Your passport is **stolen** | Private key is **compromised** | | You legally **change your name** | Subject's **identity attributes** change | | The government **recalls** specific passports for fraud | **CA compromise** or policy violation | | You pass away | **Subject ceases** to exist (employee leaves) | In the physical world, the passport office publishes a **"Revoked Passports"** gazette or maintains a hotline. In the digital world of **PKI**, this gazette is replaced by structured protocols: **CRLs**, **OCSP**, or **OCSP Stapling**. Without revocation, an attacker holding a stolen private key could impersonate the legitimate owner for the **entire remaining lifetime** of the certificate — a catastrophic security failure. > [!TIP] > **Think of revocation as a "kill switch"** that overrides the certificate's printed expiration date. Just because a certificate says "valid until 2030" doesn't mean it's trustworthy *today*. --- ## 1.3 Why Revocation is Necessary (Fundamental Triggers) A certificate may be revoked for any of the following engineering/situational reasons: 1. **Private Key Compromise** — The corresponding private key is suspected or known to be exposed. 2. **CA Compromise** — The issuing CA's own private signing key is compromised. 3. **Affiliation Change** — The subject no longer represents the organization (e.g., employee resignation). 4. **Certificate Superseded** — A new certificate replaces the old one. 5. **Cessation of Operation** — The subject entity is permanently shut down. 6. **Privilege Withdrawn** — The subject no longer holds the rights asserted in the certificate. 7. **Key Compromise (CA)** — The CA's key used to sign certificates is compromised. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *expiration* with *revocation*. **Expiration** is automatic and time-based; **revocation** is manual/policy-driven and *premature*. Both lead to certificate invalidity, but the mechanisms differ. --- ## 1.4 Scope of This Module Note This note systematically covers: - **CRL (Certificate Revocation List)** — RFC 5280 - **ARL (Authority Revocation List)** - **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)** — RFC 6960 - **OCSP Stapling** and **OCSP Must-Staple** - **Delta CRLs** and **Partitioned CRLs** - **CRL Distribution Points (CDP)** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Workflow — "The Revocation Lifecycle" The revocation pipeline can be broken into **5 distinct phases**: 1. **Trigger Event Occurs** — A reason (key compromise, employee exit, etc.) activates the need to revoke. 2. **Subscriber/RA Reports** — The entity or Registration Authority (RA) notifies the CA. 3. **CA Updates Revocation State** — The CA marks the certificate's serial number as revoked in its database. 4. **Status Publication** — The CA disseminates this status through CRL, OCSP responder, or both. 5. **Relying Party Validation** — The client application queries the status before trusting the certificate. > [!NOTE] > **Time-to-Revoke (TTR)** is a critical metric in PKI. The shorter the TTR, the more secure the system, but the higher the operational overhead. Typical targets: **< 24 hours** for enterprise CAs, **< 1 hour** for high-security e-commerce CAs. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **examination-critical**. Memorize the symbol definitions and typical values. | Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $T_{validity}$ | Certificate validity period | $T_{NotAfter} - T_{NotBefore}$ | seconds (or days) | | $T_{revoked}$ | Time at which CA marks revoked | System clock value | RFC 5280 GeneralizedTime | | $T_{nextUpdate}$ | Next scheduled CRL issuance | $T_{thisUpdate} + \Delta_{crl}$ | seconds | | $\Delta_{crl}$ | CRL issuance interval | Configurable (e.g., **86400 s** = 24 h) | seconds | | $N_{crl}$ | Number of entries in a CRL | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbb{1}\{cert_i \text{ is revoked}\}$ | count | | $S_{crl}$ | Average CRL size | $\alpha \cdot N_{crl} + \beta$ | bytes | | $\alpha$ | Average bytes per entry | ~**40–80** bytes | bytes/entry | | $\beta$ | CRL header/footer overhead | ~**200–300** bytes | bytes | | $L_{path}$ | Revocation latency (worst case) | $T_{nextUpdate} - T_{revoked}$ | seconds | | $T_{ocspCache}$ | OCSP response cache lifetime | $\min(\text{signatureValidity}, \text{cacheMaxAge})$ | seconds | > [!CAUTION] > **Critical Table Rule (Strictly Enforced):** In the above table, the vertical pipe symbol `|` was deliberately replaced with `\vert` and `\cdot` in the math expressions to prevent the markdown table from breaking. In your **own answer sheets**, use parentheses, e.g., *the size of the CRL equals the number of entries times the average size per entry* — don't try to embed a formula with pipes inside a table cell. --- ## 2.3 CRL (Certificate Revocation List) — RFC 5280 A **CRL** is a **digitally signed**, **time-stamped** data structure issued by a CA containing a list of serial numbers of certificates that have been revoked. ### 2.3.1 Structure of a CRL (X.509 v2) The CRL is itself an ASN.1-encoded structure with the following key fields: | Field | Meaning | Mandatory? | |---|---|---| | `Version` | CRL format version (v1 or v2) | Yes | | `Signature Algorithm ID` | Hash + signature algorithm (e.g., SHA-256 with RSA) | Yes | | `Issuer` | Distinguished Name (DN) of the issuing CA | Yes | | `This Update` | Time at which this CRL was generated | Yes | | `Next Update` | Time at which the next CRL will be issued | Optional but recommended | | `Revoked Certificates` | List of revoked cert entries | Conditional | | `CRL Extensions` | v2 extensions (e.g., CRL Number, Authority Key Identifier) | Optional | | `Signature` | CA's digital signature over the CRL | Yes | ### 2.3.2 Per-Entry Fields (RevokedCertificate) Each revoked certificate inside the CRL contains: - **User Certificate Serial Number** — the unique identifier - **Revocation Date** — when the revocation took effect - **CRL Entry Extensions** — optional, e.g., `ReasonCode`, `InvalidityDate`, `CertificateIssuer` > [!NOTE] > **Reason Codes (CRL Reason Code Extension, RFC 5280 §5.3.1):** > - `0` = unspecified > - `1` = keyCompromise > - `2` = cACompromise > - `3` = affiliationChanged > - `4` = superseded > - `5` = cessationOfOperation > - `6` = certificateHold > - `8` = removeFromCRL (only in Delta CRLs) > - `9` = privilegeWithdrawn > - `10` = aACompromise ### 2.3.3 Why CRLs Have a "Freshness Problem" A relying party that downloads a CRL is trusting the information **only as of the `thisUpdate` timestamp**. Between the moment the CA revokes a certificate and the next CRL publication, a **vulnerability window** exists: $$L_{window} = T_{nextUpdate} - T_{revoked}$$ For a CRL issued every 24 hours, a key compromised **5 minutes** after publication may remain valid for up to **23 hours and 55 minutes**. This is the **CRL freshness problem**. --- ## 2.4 OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) — RFC 6960 To address the CRL freshness problem, OCSP provides **real-time, on-demand** certificate status. ### 2.4.1 How OCSP Works (Step-by-Step) 1. **Client** sends an **OCSP request** to the **OCSP Responder** (a trusted server operated by the CA or a delegated responder). 2. The request contains the **target certificate's serial number** (or its issuer DN + serial). 3. The **OCSP Responder** looks up the status in its **authoritative database** (which is updated by the CA in real time). 4. The responder returns a **digitally signed OCSP response** with one of three statuses: - **`good`** — certificate is not revoked (but **does not guarantee** it was ever issued!) - **`revoked`** — certificate has been revoked - **`unknown`** — responder has no information about this certificate 5. The client **caches** the response (typically for a few hours) to reduce latency. ### 2.4.2 OCSP Response Fields - `version` — OCSP version (v1 per RFC 6960) - `producedAt` — when the responder generated the response - `responses` — one or more `SingleResponse` blocks - `responseStatus` — overall protocol status (e.g., `successful`, `malformedRequest`) - `certID` — identifies the certificate being checked - `certStatus` — `good` / `revoked` / `unknown` - `thisUpdate` — when status was known to be correct - `nextUpdate` — when newer info will be available - `nonce` — random value to prevent replay (optional, EXTENSION REQUIRED in practice for some clients) > [!TIP] > **OCSP vs CRL — Mental Model:** A CRL is like a **newspaper subscription** (bulk, periodic, can be stale). OCSP is like **calling the issuing office directly** (real-time, targeted, but creates a privacy/load problem). --- ## 2.5 OCSP Stapling (RFC 6066, TLS 1.2 Extension) ### 2.5.1 The Problem OCSP Solves Poorly Plain OCSP has two major flaws: 1. **Privacy Leak** — The OCSP responder learns which websites the user visits. 2. **Performance Penalty** — Every TLS handshake requires an extra round-trip to the OCSP server. 3. **Availability Dependency** — If the OCSP server is down, the client may either fail-closed (insecure) or fail-open (less secure). ### 2.5.2 The Stapling Solution In **OCSP Stapling**, the **web server itself** periodically fetches a fresh OCSP response from the CA's responder, **caches it**, and **staples** (attaches) it to its TLS `CertificateStatus` message during the TLS handshake. The client verifies the responder's signature but never contacts the OCSP responder directly. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Instead of every student calling the university registrar to verify their degree, the registrar **mails the proof directly to the student**, and the student presents the sealed envelope to the employer. ### 2.5.3 OCSP Must-Staple The **`must-staple` extension** (defined in RFC 7633) is set in the certificate itself and signals: *"If you cannot verify my revocation status via a stapled OCSP response, REJECT this connection entirely."* This forces fail-closed behavior and eliminates the soft-fail vulnerability. --- ## 2.6 Delta CRLs and Partitioned CRLs ### 2.6.1 Delta CRLs A **Delta CRL** contains only certificates revoked **since the last full (base) CRL** was issued. The client combines the base CRL with the latest delta to obtain a complete revocation list. This dramatically reduces bandwidth and parse time. $$\text{Complete Revocation List} = \text{Base CRL} \cup \text{Delta CRL}_1 \cup \text{Delta CRL}_2 \cup \ldots$$ ### 2.6.2 Partitioned (Indirect) CRLs Large CAs (e.g., root CAs of browser vendors) may issue **CRL partitions** by certificate range or policy. Clients fetch only the relevant partition. --- ## 2.7 ARL (Authority Revocation List) An **Authority Revocation List** is a special CRL that lists revoked **CA certificates** (rather than end-entity certificates). This is critical when a **subordinate CA's signing key** is compromised, requiring all certificates chained to that CA to be flagged. --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Browser PKI:** Chrome, Firefox, Edge all check OCSP stapling during TLS handshakes. - **S/MIME Email:** Outlook/Thunderbird verify revocation before trusting signing certificates. - **Code Signing:** Microsoft's Authenticode checks revocation before executing signed binaries. - **IoT:** OTA firmware updates reject updates signed by revoked device certificates. - **Banking PKI:** Smart card certificates use CRL or OCSP for transaction signing. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: CRL Size Estimation Formula ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement A CA has revoked **N = 5,000** certificates since the last CRL issuance. The average size of a single CRL entry (serial number + revocation date + extensions) is **E = 75 bytes**. The CRL envelope (header, footer, signature, issuer DN) has a fixed overhead of **H = 250 bytes**. Estimate the total CRL size $S_{crl}$. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the components of the CRL.** A CRL consists of: - A fixed **envelope** (version, algorithm, issuer, validity dates, signature) → $H$ bytes - A list of **N revoked certificate entries** → $N \cdot E$ bytes **Step 2 — Write the total size formula.** $$S_{crl} = H + N \cdot E$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$S_{crl} = 250 + 5000 \cdot 75$$ **Step 4 — Compute the multiplication.** $$5000 \cdot 75 = 5000 \cdot 75 = 375{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5 — Add the envelope overhead.** $$S_{crl} = 250 + 375{,}000 = 375{,}250 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to kilobytes for clarity.** $$S_{crl} = \frac{375{,}250}{1024} \approx 366.5 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 7 — Interpret the result.** > A CRL with 5,000 entries occupies roughly **366 KB**. A client device must download this periodically to validate certificates. If a CRL is published every 24 hours, the daily bandwidth is **~366 KB** per client. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Revocation Latency (Worst-Case Window) ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement A CA issues CRLs every 6 hours. A private key compromise occurs **2 hours** after a CRL is published. What is the **worst-case window of vulnerability** before the compromise is publicly advertised? ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Define the variables.** - $\Delta_{crl} = 6 \text{ hours}$ (CRL publication interval) - $t_{post} = 2 \text{ hours}$ (time elapsed after CRL publication when compromise occurs) - $L_{window}$ = worst-case latency **Step 2 — Write the vulnerability window formula.** $$L_{window} = \Delta_{crl} - t_{post}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values.** $$L_{window} = 6 - 2 = 4 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret.** During these **4 hours**, a relying party downloading the most recent CRL will see the certificate as "valid" because the compromise has not yet been published. The compromised key can be used to impersonate the subject for the entire 4-hour interval. **Step 5 — Derive the average case.** If the compromise is equally likely at any moment in the interval: $$\bar{L}_{window} = \frac{\Delta_{crl}}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 6 — Generalize.** For any CRL interval, the **average** vulnerability window is: $$\bar{L}_{window} = \frac{\Delta_{crl}}{2}$$ This is a **standard result** in PKI design. Reducing $\Delta_{crl}$ reduces $\bar{L}_{window}$ linearly, but increases network/CA load. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: OCSP Response Latency vs CRL Bulk Fetch ### 3.3.1 Problem Statement Compare the **time to validate** a single certificate using: - **Method A:** Downloading a 366 KB CRL over a 1 Mbps link. - **Method B:** Sending an OCSP request (1 KB) and receiving a 2 KB response over a 100 Mbps link with 50 ms RTT. ### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute CRL fetch time (Method A).** Transfer time: $$t_{transfer}^{CRL} = \frac{S_{crl}}{B} = \frac{366{,}250 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps}}$$ $$t_{transfer}^{CRL} = \frac{2{,}930{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} \approx 2.93 \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 2 — Compute OCSP time (Method B).** Total round-trip time approximation: $$t_{ocsp} = RTT + \frac{S_{req} + S_{resp}}{B} = 0.05 + \frac{(1024 + 2048) \cdot 8}{10^8}$$ $$t_{ocsp} = 0.05 + \frac{24{,}576}{10^8} = 0.05 + 0.000246 \approx 0.0502 \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 3 — Compare.** $$t_{ocsp} \approx 50 \text{ ms} \quad \text{vs} \quad t_{CRL} \approx 2930 \text{ ms}$$ $$\text{Speedup Factor} = \frac{t_{CRL}}{t_{ocsp}} = \frac{2930}{50.2} \approx 58.4\times$$ **Step 4 — Interpret.** > OCSP is approximately **58 times faster** than downloading a full CRL for a single certificate check, primarily because CRL size scales with the total revoked count, while OCSP returns only the targeted entry. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: CRL vs OCSP vs Stapling | Criterion | CRL (RFC 5280) | OCSP (RFC 6960) | OCSP Stapling | |---|---|---|---| | **Real-time accuracy** | Poor (stale by up to $\Delta_{crl}$) | Excellent (live responder) | Excellent (live responder) | | **Bandwidth per check** | High (entire list) | Low (~3 KB) | Low (~3 KB) | | **Server load on CA** | Low (one publish per interval) | High (one query per check) | Moderate (cached queries) | | **Client privacy** | Good (no per-site leak) | Poor (responder sees client IP per site) | Good (no client–responder contact) | | **Availability dependency** | Low (cached CRL) | High (soft-fail risk) | Moderate (cached by server) | | **Scalability** | Limited for large PKIs | Good with caching | Best | | **Latency to first check** | High (initial download) | Moderate (1 RTT) | Low (in TLS handshake) | | **Deployment complexity** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | | **Failure mode** | Stale data | Soft-fail (often insecure) | Fail-closed with must-staple | --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Delta CRL Bandwidth Savings ### Problem A base CRL has 50,000 entries (size ~3.6 MB). In a typical 6-hour period, only **200 new** certificates are revoked. Compare bandwidth with vs without delta CRLs. ### Solution **Without Delta CRLs:** The CA must republish the **full 3.6 MB** every 6 hours (since 200 new entries plus 49,800 existing ones are re-listed). $$B_{noDelta} = 3.6 \text{ MB} \cdot 4 \text{ publishes/day} = 14.4 \text{ MB/day}$$ **With Delta CRLs:** The base CRL is republished **once per day** (24 h), and delta CRLs every 6 hours. A delta with 200 entries: $$S_{delta} = 250 + 200 \cdot 75 = 15{,}250 \text{ bytes} \approx 14.9 \text{ KB}$$ Daily bandwidth: $$B_{delta} = 3.6 \text{ MB} + 4 \cdot 14.9 \text{ KB} \approx 3.66 \text{ MB/day}$$ **Bandwidth savings:** $$\text{Savings} = \frac{14.4 - 3.66}{14.4} \approx 74.6\%$$ > Delta CRLs reduce bandwidth by roughly **75%** in this scenario, illustrating their engineering value for large-scale PKIs. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Revocation Architecture — Full PKI Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Authority["CA Authority Infrastructure"] DB[("Revocation Database
Serial No + Reason + Date")] CAEngine["CA Engine
Signs Certificates"] CRLGen["CRL Generator
Periodic job every Delta_crl"] OCSPResp["OCSP Responder
Real-time lookup service"] end subgraph Subscriber["Subscriber Side"] UserCert["User Certificate
Serial 12345"] PrivKey["Private Key
(Compromised!)"] end subgraph Relying["Relying Party / Client"] App["Application
Browser / Mail Client"] Cache[("Status Cache
CRL / OCSP")] end DB --> CRLGen DB --> OCSPResp CAEngine --> UserCert PrivKey -.Compromise.-> DB UserCert -.Embedded AIA.-> App CRLGen -.CRL over HTTP/LDAP.-> Cache OCSPResp -.OCSP over HTTP.-> Cache Cache --> App App --> Decision{Is Certificate
Revoked?} Decision -->|Yes| Reject[Reject Connection] Decision -->|No| Accept[Proceed with TLS] ``` --- ## 4.2 CRL Issuance and Distribution Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CA as Certification Authority participant DB as Revocation DB participant Repo as CRL Repository
(HTTP/LDAP) participant Client as Relying Party Note over CA,DB: T = 0: CA revokes cert Serial 12345 CA->>DB: INSERT (Serial=12345, Date=now, Reason=keyCompromise) Note over CA,Repo: T = 6h: Scheduled CRL publication CA->>DB: SELECT all revoked entries DB-->>CA: List of N revoked serials CA->>CA: Build ASN.1 CRL structure CA->>CA: Sign CRL with CA private key CA->>Repo: Publish signed CRL Note over Client,Repo: T = 6h+1m: Client validates a cert Client->>Repo: GET latest CRL Repo-->>Client: Signed CRL (366 KB) Client->>Client: Verify CA signature Client->>Client: Search for target serial alt Serial found in CRL Client-->>Client: Reject certificate else Serial not in CRL Client-->>Client: Accept (if within validity) end ``` --- ## 4.3 OCSP Request-Response Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as TLS Client participant Server as Web Server participant OCSP as OCSP Responder participant CA as Certification Authority Note over CA,OCSP: Background: CA pushes revocation updates to OCSP responder DB CA->>OCSP: Sync revoked serials list Note over Client,Server: TLS Handshake begins Client->>Server: ClientHello Server->>OCSP: GET OCSP response for own cert OCSP-->>Server: Signed OCSP response (good/revoked) Server->>Client: ServerHello + Certificate + CertificateStatus (stapled) Client->>Client: Verify responder signature on stapled OCSP alt Status = good Client-->>Server: Complete TLS handshake else Status = revoked Client-->>Server: Abort handshake end ``` --- ## 4.4 Decision Tree: How a Client Validates a Certificate ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Receive Certificate] --> B{Check NotBefore
and NotAfter} B -->|Outside validity| Z1[Reject: Expired or not yet valid] B -->|Within validity| C{Certificate has
OCSP URL in AIA?} C -->|Yes| D[Send OCSP Request] C -->|No| E{Certificate has
CRL Distribution Point?} E -->|Yes| F[Download and verify CRL] E -->|No| G[Accept on trust] D --> H{OCSP Response
Status?} H -->|good| Y[Accept Certificate] H -->|revoked| Z2[Reject: Revoked] H -->|unknown| H1{Treat unknown as
soft-fail or hard-fail?} H1 -->|Soft-fail| Y H1 -->|Hard-fail| Z3[Reject: Status unknown] F --> I{Serial Number
in CRL?} I -->|Yes| Z2 I -->|No| Y G --> Y ``` --- ## 4.5 CRL Internal Data Structure (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CertificateList ASN.1] --> B[Version v2] A --> C[Signature Algorithm
sha256WithRSAEncryption] A --> D[Issuer DN
CN = KTU Root CA] A --> E[This Update
2024-09-15T10:00:00Z] A --> F[Next Update
2024-09-16T10:00:00Z] A --> G[Revoked Certificates List] G --> G1[Entry 1: Serial 12345
Date 2024-09-14T08:30:00Z
Reason keyCompromise] G --> G2[Entry 2: Serial 12346
Date 2024-09-14T09:15:00Z
Reason superseded] G --> G3[Entry N: Serial ...] A --> H[CRL Extensions
CRL Number, AKID] A --> I[CA Digital Signature
over tbsCertList] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define certificate revocation. List any four reasons for revoking a digital certificate.** **Model Answer:** > **Certificate revocation** is the process by which a Certification Authority (CA) invalidates a digital certificate **before its scheduled expiration date**, declaring it untrusted. The four main reasons are: 1. **Key Compromise** — The private key corresponding to the certificate is suspected or known to be exposed. 2. **CA Compromise** — The issuing CA's signing key is compromised. 3. **Affiliation Change** — The subject no longer represents the organization (e.g., employee resignation). 4. **Cessation of Operation** — The subject entity is permanently shut down or no longer needs the certificate. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]` **Differentiate between CRL and OCSP.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | CRL | OCSP | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | A signed list of revoked certificate serial numbers | A request-response protocol for on-demand status | | **Frequency** | Periodic (e.g., every 24 h) | Real-time | | **Data Returned** | Full list of all revoked serials | Status of one specific certificate | | **Bandwidth** | High (scales with revocation count) | Low (single response) | | **Freshness** | Stale by up to one CRL interval | Always up-to-date | | **Privacy** | Better (no per-cert leak) | Poorer (responder sees queries) | | **Standard** | RFC 5280 | RFC 6960 | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a **Certificate Revocation List (CRL)** as defined in RFC 5280. List and explain the **CRL extensions** used in X.509 v2 CRLs. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **OCSP Stapling**? Explain how it solves the privacy and performance issues of plain OCSP. Discuss the role of the **must-staple** extension. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** A **CRL** is a digitally signed, time-stamped list issued by a CA that contains the **serial numbers of all certificates revoked** by that CA before their expiration. **Step 2 — Structure (3 marks):** The CRL has the following fields (as per ASN.1 in RFC 5280): - **Version** — CRL format version (v1 or v2). - **Signature Algorithm Identifier** — Algorithm used to sign the CRL (e.g., `sha256WithRSAEncryption`). - **Issuer** — Distinguished Name (DN) of the CA that issued and signed the CRL. - **thisUpdate** — Time at which this CRL was generated. - **nextUpdate** — Time at which the next CRL will be issued. - **Revoked Certificates** — A sequence of `RevokedCertificate` entries, each containing: - `userCertificate` (serial number) - `revocationDate` - `CRLEntryExtensions` (optional) - **CRL Extensions** (v2 only) — e.g., `Authority Key Identifier`, `CRL Number`, `Issuer Alternative Name`, `Delta CRL Indicator`. - **Signature Value** — CA's digital signature over the `tbsCertList` (everything except the signature itself). **Step 3 — CRL Extensions (2 marks):** | Extension | Purpose | |---|---| | `Authority Key Identifier` | Identifies the public key corresponding to the signing key. | | `CRL Number` | Monotonically increasing sequence number for the CRL. | | `Delta CRL Indicator` | Marks this CRL as a delta (contains only incremental changes). | | `Issuing Distribution Point` | Indicates the CRL scope (e.g., user certs only, partitioned). | | `Freshest CRL` | Points to the most recent delta CRL. | **Step 4 — Per-entry extension: Reason Code (1 mark):** The `ReasonCode` CRL entry extension specifies the cause of revocation (e.g., `keyCompromise = 1`, `cACompromise = 2`, `affiliationChanged = 3`). --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — OCSP Recap (1 mark):** OCSP is a real-time protocol where a client queries the **OCSP Responder** to learn if a certificate is `good`, `revoked`, or `unknown`. **Step 2 — Problems with Plain OCSP (2 marks):** 1. **Privacy:** The responder learns which websites the user is visiting. 2. **Latency:** Every TLS handshake requires an extra round-trip to a third-party server. 3. **Soft-fail risk:** If the responder is down, many clients fail-open, defeating the security guarantee. **Step 3 — OCSP Stapling Definition (2 marks):** **OCSP Stapling** is a TLS extension (RFC 6066) where the **web server** itself periodically fetches a fresh OCSP response from the CA's responder, caches it, and **staples** (attaches) it to the TLS `CertificateStatus` message during the handshake. The client verifies the responder's signature but **does not** contact the OCSP responder directly. **Step 4 — Must-Staple Extension (2 marks):** The **`must-staple` certificate extension** (RFC 7633) is set in the certificate by the CA. It instructs the client: *"If you cannot obtain a valid stapled OCSP response, REJECT this TLS connection."* This enforces **fail-closed** behavior, eliminating the soft-fail vulnerability inherent in plain OCSP. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the **CRL freshness problem** in detail. Derive the expression for the **worst-case vulnerability window** and the **average vulnerability window**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a **Delta CRL**? A base CRL contains **40,000** entries with an average entry size of **80 bytes** and an envelope overhead of **300 bytes**. The CA republishes a base CRL every **24 hours** and a delta CRL every **6 hours**. If, on average, **300** new entries are added per delta interval, calculate the **daily bandwidth with and without delta CRLs**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark):** A CRL is published at fixed intervals. Between two consecutive publications, a certificate that was valid in the previous CRL but has been revoked in the interim is **not yet advertised** as revoked. **Step 2 — Worst-case Window (3 marks):** If a compromise occurs **immediately after** a CRL is published at time $T_0$, the CA will not republish until $T_0 + \Delta_{crl}$. The **worst-case** vulnerability window is: $$L_{window}^{worst} = \Delta_{crl} - t_{post}$$ where $t_{post}$ is the time elapsed after CRL publication when the compromise occurs. The maximum is achieved when $t_{post} \to 0$: $$\max(L_{window}) = \Delta_{crl}$$ **Step 3 — Average Window (3 marks):** If the compromise is equally likely at any moment in the interval, the average elapsed time after a CRL is $\Delta_{crl}/2$. Hence: $$\bar{L}_{window} = \Delta_{crl} - \frac{\Delta_{crl}}{2} = \frac{\Delta_{crl}}{2}$$ **Conclusion:** Halving the CRL publication interval halves the average vulnerability window but doubles the CA's publishing load and the clients' download traffic. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Delta CRL Concept (1 mark):** A **Delta CRL** contains only certificates revoked **since the last base (full) CRL**. Clients combine the base CRL with the latest delta to assemble a complete revocation list. **Step 2 — Compute Base CRL Size (2 marks):** $$S_{base} = 300 + 40{,}000 \cdot 80 = 300 + 3{,}200{,}000 = 3{,}200{,}300 \text{ bytes} \approx 3.05 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Delta CRL Size (1 mark):** $$S_{delta} = 300 + 300 \cdot 80 = 300 + 24{,}000 = 24{,}300 \text{ bytes} \approx 23.7 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Daily Bandwidth WITHOUT Delta CRLs (1 mark):** Without deltas, the CA republishes the full base CRL every 6 hours (4 times per day): $$B_{noDelta} = 4 \cdot 3.05 \text{ MB} = 12.2 \text{ MB/day}$$ **Step 5 — Compute Daily Bandwidth WITH Delta CRLs (1 mark):** With deltas, the base CRL is published once per day and the delta CRL every 6 hours: $$B_{delta} = 1 \cdot 3.05 \text{ MB} + 4 \cdot 23.7 \text{ KB} \approx 3.05 \text{ MB} + 94.8 \text{ KB} \approx 3.14 \text{ MB/day}$$ **Step 6 — Bandwidth Savings and Comment (1 mark):** $$\text{Savings} = \frac{12.2 - 3.14}{12.2} \times 100\% \approx 74.3\%$$ > **Comment:** Delta CRLs reduce daily bandwidth by approximately **74%**. This is critical for large-scale PKIs (e.g., browser-trusted CAs with millions of revocations) where bandwidth and client download time directly impact user experience. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Revocation Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing revocation with expiration.** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the student does not explicitly state that revocation is *premature* invalidation. > 2. **Forgetting CRL signature.** A CRL is **digitally signed by the CA**. If you describe it as an unsigned list, you lose 1 mark. > 3. **Omitting `thisUpdate` and `nextUpdate` in CRL structure.** Both are mandatory to describe in 7-mark structure questions. > 4. **Mixing up OCSP stapling with CRL.** Stapling is **not** the same as caching a CRL; it is the **server** delivering a fresh OCSP response during the TLS handshake. > 5. **Not stating the RFC numbers.** RFC 5280 (CRL), RFC 6960 (OCSP), and RFC 7633 (must-staple) are **expected** in any 7+ mark answer. Forgetting them = -1 mark. > 6. **Calculation errors in CRL size or window derivations.** Always show the formula **first**, then substitute. Step-skipping = -2 marks. > 7. **In Decision Matrix / Comparison answers**, do not write prose only. Examiners expect a **table** for any 7-mark comparison question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Revocation** - ☐ **Revocation** = premature invalidation of a certificate by a CA, **not** the same as expiration. - ☐ A **CRL** is a **digitally signed, time-stamped list** of serial numbers of revoked certificates (RFC 5280). - ☐ Mandatory CRL fields: `version`, `signature`, `issuer`, `thisUpdate`, `revokedCertificates`, `signatureValue`. - ☐ Optional but recommended: `nextUpdate`, `CRL Number`, `Authority Key Identifier`. - ☐ **CRL Reason Codes:** 0=unspecified, 1=keyCompromise, 2=cACompromise, 3=affiliationChanged, 4=superseded, 5=cessationOfOperation, 6=certificateHold. - ☐ **CRL Freshness Problem:** Stale data between publications; worst-case window = $\Delta_{crl}$, average = $\Delta_{crl}/2$. - ☐ **CRL Size Formula:** $S_{crl} = H + N \cdot E$ (envelope + entries). - ☐ **OCSP** is a real-time, request-response protocol (RFC 6960) returning `good`, `revoked`, or `unknown`. - ☐ OCSP **does not** guarantee the certificate was ever issued (only that it isn't currently revoked). - ☐ **OCSP Stapling** shifts the OCSP query from client to web server, preserving privacy and reducing latency. - ☐ **Must-staple** (RFC 7633) forces fail-closed behavior, eliminating soft-fail vulnerabilities. - ☐ **Delta CRLs** contain only new revocations since the last base CRL — saves ~70-75% bandwidth. - ☐ **ARL** = Authority Revocation List, used to revoke **CA certificates** (not end-entity certs). - ☐ **Indirect CRLs** are issued by a centralized authority on behalf of multiple CAs. - ☐ In **7-mark CRL structure questions**, always include the diagram and explicitly mention the CA signature. - ☐ In **numerical problems**, always write the formula first, then substitute, then compute — never skip steps. - ☐ Remember the **3 OCSP response statuses**: `good`, `revoked`, `unknown` — and that `good` ≠ "valid". - ☐ **Soft-fail vs Hard-fail:** Plain OCSP usually soft-fails (accepts on responder error); stapling + must-staple = hard-fail. - ☐ Real-world deployments: Chrome, Firefox use OCSP stapling; S/MIME uses CRL/OCSP; code signing uses both. - ☐ The **`AIA` (Authority Information Access) extension** in a certificate points to the OCSP responder URL. - ☐ The **`CRL Distribution Points (CDP)` extension** points to the CRL location. --- **END OF MODULE 1 — REVOCATION NOTES**

topics.title: Electronic Mail Security - Pretty Good Privacy (PGP) - Operational Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic Mail Security - Pretty Good Privacy (PGP) - Operational Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Pretty Good Privacy (PGP)** is a hybrid cryptographic protocol designed by **Philip Zimmermann (1991)** to provide **confidentiality, authentication, message integrity, and non-repudiation** for electronic mail and file storage applications. PGP is widely regarded as the *de-facto* open standard for securing e-mail and follows the **OpenPGP standard (RFC 4880 / RFC 9580)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:**
> PGP is an application-layer protocol that uses a combination of **symmetric key encryption (IDEA/CAST/3DES/AES)**, **asymmetric key encryption (RSA/ElGamal)**, **hash functions (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-2)**, **data compression (ZIP)**, and **Radix-64 encoding** to provide end-to-end secure messaging.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Notary Letter"

Imagine you want to send a confidential business proposal through a regular postal service (insecure channel). To protect it, you would:

1. **Sign it with your unique seal** (Authentication) → receiver knows *you* wrote it.
2. **Put the document inside a tamper-proof locked box** (Confidentiality) → only the recipient can open it.
3. **Compress bulky papers** to save space (Compression).
4. **Wrap the locked box in a standard courier envelope** (Radix-64 encoding) so the courier system can transport it.
5. **Send the recipient a separate small key parcel** so they can unlock the box (encrypted session key).

**PGP does exactly this — but mathematically and in milliseconds.**

### 1.3 Why PGP? (Problem It Solves)

| Threat | PGP Counter |
|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | Symmetric + Asymmetric encryption |
| Tampering | Hash + Digital signature |
| Forgery (Impersonation) | Digital signature with private key |
| Repudiation | Non-repudiable signature |
| MIME incompatibility | Radix-64 encoding |
| Replay attacks | Timestamp + random session key |

### 1.4 PGP Cryptographic Primitives Summary

| Function | Algorithm(s) Used |
|---|---|
| Symmetric Encryption | **CAST-128, IDEA, 3DES, AES-256** |
| Asymmetric Encryption | **RSA (≥2048-bit), ElGamal** |
| Hash / Digest | **MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2 family** |
| Signature | RSA or DSA on the hash |
| Compression | **ZIP (RFC 1951)** |
| Email encoding | **Radix-64 (Base64)** |

> [!NOTE]
> PGP uses **one-time session keys** for symmetric encryption — a fresh random key is generated for *each* message, which is then itself encrypted with the recipient's public key.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five PGP Services (KTU Board Favourite)

PGP provides the following services, listed in order of application in the canonical transmission flow:

1. **Digital Signature (Authentication + Integrity + Non-repudiation)**
2. **Message Compression (Efficiency)**
3. **Message Encryption (Confidentiality)**
4. **Radix-64 Conversion (Email Compatibility)**
5. **Segmentation (Reassembly over SMTP size limits)**

### 2.2 Authentication-Only Operation (Signature Path)

The sender proves identity and ensures integrity. **No confidentiality is provided.**

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Sender drafts plaintext message $M$.
2. Sender computes the hash digest:
$$H = \text{Hash}(M)$$
3. Sender encrypts the hash with their **private key** (this is the signature):
$$S = E_{K_{R,sender}}(H)$$
4. Sender transmits the concatenated bundle: $(M \parallel S)$ over the network.
5. Receiver decrypts $S$ using sender's **public key** to recover $H'$:
$$H' = D_{K_{U,sender}}(S)$$
6. Receiver independently hashes $M$ to get $H$:
$$H = \text{Hash}(M)$$
7. If $H' = H$, the message is **authentic and unaltered**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Authentication is performed on the **plaintext** message (before compression and encryption), because signatures on compressed ciphertext are non-verifiable by third parties later.

### 2.3 Confidentiality-Only Operation (Encryption Path)

The sender protects the message body. **No authentication.**

1. Sender generates a fresh **128-bit random session key** $K_S$.
2. Sender compresses message: $M_C = \text{ZIP}(M)$.
3. Sender encrypts compressed message with $K_S$ using symmetric cipher:
$$C = E_{K_S}(M_C)$$
4. Sender encrypts session key with recipient's **public key**:
$$K_{S,\text{enc}} = E_{K_{U,recipient}}(K_S)$$
5. Receiver reverses: $K_S = D_{K_{R,recipient}}(K_{S,\text{enc}})$, then $M_C = D_{K_S}(C)$.

### 2.4 Combined Authentication + Confidentiality (Full PGP)

This is the **exam-favourite** flow. The canonical PGP transmission follows:

**Sender side (transmission order):**
- Signature first → Compression → Encryption → Radix-64

**Mathematical flow:**
$$H = \text{Hash}(M)$$
$$S = E_{K_{R,sender}}(H)$$
$$\text{Signed\_Bundle} = (M \parallel S)$$
$$M_C = \text{ZIP}(\text{Signed\_Bundle})$$
$$C = E_{K_S}(M_C)$$
$$K_{S,\text{enc}} = E_{K_{U,recipient}}(K_S)$$
$$\text{Output} = K_{S,\text{enc}} \parallel C$$

> [!NOTE]
> An **alternative (simplified)** flow omits compression between signature and encryption, applying the order: Sign → Encrypt. This is also accepted by KTU examiners.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Plaintext message | Input |
| $H = \text{Hash}(M)$ | Message digest (e.g., 160-bit SHA-1, 256-bit SHA-256) | Integrity primitive |
| $S = E_{K_{R,sender}}(H)$ | Digital signature | Authentication primitive |
| $K_S$ | Random **128-bit** one-time symmetric session key | Symmetric key |
| $E_{K_S}(M_C)$ | Symmetric encryption (CAST-128/IDEA/AES) | Confidentiality |
| $E_{K_{U,recipient}}(K_S)$ | Asymmetric encryption of session key | Key transport |
| $C$ | Ciphertext | Output |
| $\text{ZIP}(M)$ | Lossless compression | Efficiency |
| $\text{R64}(x)$ | Radix-64 / Base64 conversion | Email transport |

### 2.6 Key Identifiers in PGP

PGP does **not** transmit the entire public key with each message. Instead, it transmits a **64-bit Key ID** derived from the public key:

$$\text{KeyID} = \text{lowest 64 bits of } K_U$$

This is critical because users may have multiple public keys, and the recipient must know *which* key to use to unwrap the session key.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **End-to-End Encrypted Email** (ProtonMail, GPG-signed Gmail)
- **Software Distribution Signing** (Linux package managers `apt`, `yum` use GPG)
- **Code Commit Signing** (Git cryptographic signing)
- **Journalist / Whistleblower Communications** (PGP was originally designed for this)
- **Blockchain Wallet Authentication** (PGP-inspired key signing ceremonies)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Operational Analysis & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 End-to-End PGP Transmission — Mathematical Walkthrough

Let us work through a **complete authentication + confidentiality** scenario with concrete (small) numerical examples for the cryptographic components, while keeping the bit-widths realistic to PGP standards.

> [!NOTE]
> In real PGP, the message can be megabytes; we illustrate the structure on a 32-byte plaintext $M$.

#### Given Parameters
- Plaintext message: $M$ of length $|M| = 32$ bytes
- Hash algorithm: **SHA-1** (digest length = 160 bits = 20 bytes)
- Signature algorithm: **RSA** with 1024-bit key (illustrative; modern PGP mandates ≥ 2048 bits)
- Symmetric cipher: **CAST-128** (block size = 64 bits = 8 bytes)
- Public-key algorithm for session key encryption: **RSA** with 1024-bit key
- Session key size: **128 bits = 16 bytes**

#### STEP 1 — Hash the plaintext

The sender computes the SHA-1 digest of the message:

$$H = \text{SHA-1}(M), \quad \vert H \vert = 160 \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

Suppose $H = \text{0x3A2F...C7D1}$ (20-byte abstract value).

#### STEP 2 — Sign the digest with the sender's private RSA key

The signature is the RSA encryption of the digest using the sender's private key $(d_S, n_S)$:

$$S = (H)^{d_S} \mod n_S, \quad \vert S \vert = 1024 \text{ bits} = 128 \text{ bytes}$$

**Interpretation:** This step is *expensive* (modular exponentiation) — but the operation is performed only on the 20-byte hash, not the full message, so it remains fast.

#### STEP 3 — Concatenate the signed bundle

The sender constructs the signed (but not yet confidential) bundle:

$$\text{SignedBundle} = M \parallel S, \quad \vert \text{SignedBundle} \vert = 32 + 128 = 160 \text{ bytes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The signature is computed on the **plaintext** $M$, *not* on the encrypted form. This preserves verifiability by third parties (e.g., notaries, courts) who do not have the session key.

#### STEP 4 — ZIP compress the signed bundle

$$\text{CompressedBundle} = \text{ZIP}(\text{SignedBundle})$$

Let the compression ratio be $\rho = 0.75$:

$$\vert \text{CompressedBundle} \vert \approx 160 \times 0.75 = 120 \text{ bytes}$$

**Why compress *after* signing?**
- Verifiers can store the plaintext for re-verification later.
- Compression is **deterministic** for the same input, so verification remains reproducible.
- It shrinks the data *before* expensive symmetric encryption.

#### STEP 5 — Generate a one-time symmetric session key

The sender uses a cryptographically secure random number generator (CSPRNG) to produce:

$$K_S = \text{Rand}(128 \text{ bits}) = \text{0x9F4A...B201}$$

**Critical property:** $K_S$ is used **only once**. This satisfies the *one-time-pad ideal* — even if compromised in transit, it decrypts only one message.

#### STEP 6 — Symmetrically encrypt the compressed bundle

CAST-128 operates in **CFB mode** (Cipher Feedback), which avoids the need for message-length alignment. The encryption:

$$C = E_{K_S}^{\text{CAST-128}}(\text{CompressedBundle})$$

In CFB mode, the ciphertext length is approximately equal to the plaintext length (no padding inflation):

$$\vert C \vert \approx 120 \text{ bytes} \ (\text{rounded to nearest 8-byte block} = 120 \text{ bytes})$$

#### STEP 7 — Encrypt the session key with recipient's RSA public key

The recipient's public key is $(e_R, n_R)$ with $|n_R| = 1024$ bits:

$$K_{S,\text{enc}} = (K_S)^{e_R} \mod n_R, \quad \vert K_{S,\text{enc}} \vert = 128 \text{ bytes}$$

#### STEP 8 — Apply Radix-64 encoding to the entire output

Radix-64 expands 3 bytes (24 bits) to 4 ASCII characters. Expansion ratio = $4/3 \approx 1.333$:

$$\text{FinalMessage} = \text{R64}(K_{S,\text{enc}} \parallel C)$$

$$\vert \text{FinalMessage} \vert = (128 + 120) \times \tfrac{4}{3} = 248 \times \tfrac{4}{3} \approx 331 \text{ ASCII chars}$$

#### STEP 9 — Receiver-side reversal (verification + decryption)

| Step | Receiver Operation | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 9.1 | Decode Radix-64 | $K_{S,\text{enc}} \parallel C = \text{R64}^{-1}(\text{FinalMessage})$ |
| 9.2 | RSA-decrypt the session key | $K_S = (K_{S,\text{enc}})^{d_R} \mod n_R$ |
| 9.3 | Symmetric-decrypt ciphertext | $\text{CompressedBundle} = D_{K_S}^{\text{CAST-128}}(C)$ |
| 9.4 | Decompress | $\text{SignedBundle} = \text{UnZIP}(\text{CompressedBundle})$ |
| 9.5 | Extract $M$ and $S$ | Split at byte 33 |
| 9.6 | Recover claimed hash | $H' = (S)^{e_S} \mod n_S$ |
| 9.7 | Compute fresh hash | $H = \text{SHA-1}(M)$ |
| 9.8 | Compare | **If $H' = H$ → ACCEPT; else REJECT** |

### 3.2 Analytical Trade-Off Matrix

| Design Decision | Reason | KTU Exam Hot Point |
|---|---|---|
| Why hash before sign? | RSA on a 20-byte hash is **~1000× faster** than RSA on a multi-MB message | ✅ Frequently asked |
| Why session key (hybrid)? | AES is **~1000× faster** than RSA for bulk data | ✅ Frequently asked |
| Why compress *after* sign? | Allows third-party verification without the session key | ✅ 7-mark question |
| Why Radix-64? | SMTP gateways only relay **7-bit ASCII**; binary 8-bit data may be corrupted | ✅ Frequently asked |
| Why one-time session key? | Limits exposure; provides *forward secrecy* of individual messages | ✅ 7-mark question |
| Why encrypt signature with private key? (not the message) | Public key can *verify* it; only the owner has the private key | ✅ 3-mark question |

### 3.3 PGP Message Packet Structure (Packet Sequence)

A real PGP message consists of a sequence of packets. The general structure:

```
PGP_Message = [Packet_1] [Packet_2] ... [Packet_n]

where each Packet is:
  [Tag | Length | Body]
```

**Example packet sequence for signed+encrypted message:**

| Packet # | Tag | Body Content |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Public-Key Encrypted Session Key | $K_{S,\text{enc}}$ + Key ID |
| 2 | Symmetrically Encrypted Data | $C$ (CAST-128 output) |
| 3 (inner) | Signature Packet | $S$ + timestamp + digest type |
| 4 (inner) | Literal Data Packet | Original $M$ with filename and timestamp |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PGP Authentication-Only Transmission Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Plaintext Message M] --> H1[SHA-1 Hash Function]
    H1 --> HDigest[160-bit Message Digest H]
    HDigest --> RSAPriv[RSA Encrypt with Senders Private Key]
    RSAPriv --> Sig[Digital Signature S]
    M --> Concat[Concatenation Block]
    Sig --> Concat
    Concat --> OutAuth[Output: M concatenated with S]
    OutAuth --> Net((Insecure Network))
    Net --> Split[Split into M and S]
    Split --> H2[SHA-1 Hash on M]
    H2 --> Hfresh[Fresh Digest H]
    Split --> RSAPub[RSA Decrypt with Senders Public Key]
    RSAPub --> Hrecv[Recovered Digest H prime]
    Hfresh --> CMP[Compare H and H prime]
    Hrecv --> CMP
    CMP --> Decision{Equal?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Accept[Message Authentic]
    Decision -->|No| Reject[Reject Tampered]
```

### 4.2 PGP Confidentiality-Only Transmission Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Plaintext Message M] --> ZIP1[ZIP Compression]
    ZIP1 --> MC[Compressed Message MC]
    MC --> SymEnc[CAST-128 Symmetric Encryption]
    KS[Random 128-bit Session Key KS] --> SymEnc
    SymEnc --> C[Ciphertext C]
    KS --> ASymEnc[RSA Encrypt KS with Recipients Public Key]
    ASymEnc --> KSEnc[Encrypted Session Key KS enc]
    KSEnc --> Prepend[Prepend to C]
    C --> Prepend
    Prepend --> OutConf[Output Packet]
    OutConf --> Net((Insecure Network))
    Net --> Sep[Separate KS enc and C]
    Sep --> ASymDec[RSA Decrypt KS enc with Recipients Private Key]
    ASymDec --> KSRec[Recovered Session Key KS]
    Sep --> SymDec[CAST-128 Decrypt C using KS]
    KSRec --> SymDec
    SymDec --> MCRec[Recovered Compressed Message MC]
    MCRec --> UnZIP[UnZIP Decompression]
    UnZIP --> MRec[Recovered Plaintext M]
```

### 4.3 PGP Full Combined Operation Sender-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Plaintext Message M] --> S1
    S1[Step 1 Hash M with SHA-1] --> HDig[Hash H 160 bits]
    HDig --> S2
    S2[Step 2 Sign H with senders RSA private key] --> SigS[Signature S]
    SigS --> S3
    M --> S3[Step 3 Form signed bundle M parallel S]
    S3 --> S4
    S4[Step 4 ZIP compress the signed bundle] --> MC[Compressed bundle MC]
    MC --> S5
    KGen[Random 128-bit session key KS generator] --> KS
    KS[Session Key KS] --> S5
    S5[Step 5 CAST-128 encrypt MC using KS] --> C[Ciphertext C]
    KS --> S6
    S6[Step 6 RSA encrypt KS with recipients public key] --> KSEnc[Encrypted Session Key]
    KSEnc --> S7
    C --> S7
    S7[Step 7 Concatenate KS enc with C] --> Raw[Raw Binary Packet]
    Raw --> S8
    S8[Step 8 Radix-64 Base64 encode] --> Final[ASCII Armored Email Body]
    Final --> SMTP((SMTP Email Transport))
```

### 4.4 PGP Key Ring Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SenderNode[Sender Host]
        PrivRing[Private Key Ring]
        PubRingOut[Public Key Ring Local]
    end
    subgraph RecipientNode[Recipient Host]
        PrivRingR[Private Key Ring]
        PubRingR[Public Key Ring Local]
    end
    SenderNode -->|Encrypted session key + ciphertext via SMTP| RecipientNode
    PubRingOut -.Lookup.-|Recipient Public Key| SenderNode
    PrivRing -.Used for signing.- SenderNode
    PrivRingR -.Used for decryption.- RecipientNode
    PubRingR -.Used for verification.- RecipientNode
    KeyServer[(PGP Key Server Public Repository)] -.Synchronizes.- PubRingOut
    KeyServer -.Synchronizes.- PubRingR
```

### 4.5 PGP Message Packet Sequence (Time-Ordered View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Hash
    participant Sign
    participant Compress
    participant SymEnc
    participant AsymEnc
    participant Radix64
    participant Email
    participant Receiver
    participant Verify
    Sender->>Hash: Provide plaintext M
    Hash-->>Sender: 160-bit digest H
    Sender->>Sign: Encrypt H with private key
    Sign-->>Sender: Signature S
    Sender->>Compress: ZIP(M parallel S)
    Compress-->>Sender: Compressed bundle MC
    Sender->>SymEnc: CAST-128 encrypt MC with KS
    SymEnc-->>Sender: Ciphertext C
    Sender->>AsymEnc: RSA encrypt KS with recipient public key
    AsymEnc-->>Sender: KS enc
    Sender->>Radix64: Base64 encode KS enc parallel C
    Radix64-->>Email: ASCII armored message
    Email->>Receiver: SMTP delivery
    Receiver->>Verify: RSA decrypt S with sender public key
    Verify-->>Receiver: Recovered H prime
    Receiver->>Hash: Recompute SHA-1 over M
    Hash-->>Verify: Fresh H
    Verify->>Receiver: H prime equals H -> ACCEPT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECAT863]**
*State the purpose of Pretty Good Privacy (PGP). List any three cryptographic services provided by PGP.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PGP is a hybrid cryptographic protocol used to secure e-mail communication and file storage by providing end-to-end security over an insecure channel such as the Internet.

**Three cryptographic services of PGP:**
1. **Confidentiality** – achieved through symmetric encryption (CAST-128, IDEA, AES) of the message using a one-time session key, with the session key itself RSA-encrypted with the recipient's public key.
2. **Authentication & Integrity** – achieved by hashing the message (SHA-1/MD5) and RSA-encrypting the digest with the sender's private key (digital signature).
3. **Non-repudiation** – the digital signature cannot be denied by the sender since only the sender possesses the private key.
*(Other services: data compression via ZIP, email compatibility via Radix-64 encoding, segmentation for long messages.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question Bank]**
*Why does PGP use a one-time session key for encryption instead of encrypting the message directly with RSA?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PGP uses a **hybrid cryptosystem** combining symmetric and asymmetric encryption for the following reasons:

1. **Speed / Efficiency:** Symmetric algorithms (CAST-128, AES) are approximately **1000× faster** than asymmetric algorithms (RSA) for bulk data encryption. RSA would be impractically slow for messages of megabyte size.
2. **Security of long messages:** Direct RSA encryption of large messages is cryptographically weaker; a one-time 128-bit session key combined with a strong symmetric cipher offers superior security.
3. **Forward secrecy of individual messages:** A fresh random session key per message ensures that compromising one message key does not compromise any other message. The recipient's RSA private key is only used to decrypt the small 128-bit session key, not the bulk data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(Internal Choice)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the operational flow of PGP providing **both authentication and confidentiality**. Explain the order of operations and justify why that specific order is chosen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain the structure of the **PGP message packet sequence**. What is the role of a **Key ID** in PGP, and why is it only 64 bits long? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The combined PGP operation provides **digital signature (authentication + integrity + non-repudiation)** and **encryption (confidentiality)**. The canonical order is:

**Sender-side operational flow:**

1. **Hash the message** $M$ using SHA-1 to obtain a 160-bit digest $H$.
2. **Sign the digest** by RSA-encrypting with sender's private key: $S = E_{K_{R,s}}(H)$.
3. **Form the signed bundle**: $(M \parallel S)$.
4. **Compress** the bundle: $M_C = \text{ZIP}(M \parallel S)$.
5. **Generate a fresh random 128-bit session key** $K_S$.
6. **Symmetric encryption** (CAST-128 in CFB mode): $C = E_{K_S}(M_C)$.
7. **Encrypt session key** with recipient's public key: $K_{S,\text{enc}} = E_{K_{U},r}(K_S)$.
8. **Radix-64 encode** the packet $(K_{S,\text{enc}} \parallel C)$ for SMTP transport.

**Justification of order:**

- **Signature before compression** → preserves verifiability for third parties who do not hold the session key.
- **Compression before encryption** → exploits redundancy; encrypted data is incompressible. Saves bandwidth and reduces symmetric-encryption workload.
- **Encryption last** → ensures only the receiver can read the message.
- **Radix-64 last** → ensures the binary packet survives 7-bit-clean SMTP gateways.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

A PGP message is a sequence of packets. The general structure:

| Packet | Tag (purpose) | Body contents |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Public-Key Encrypted Session Key (Tag 1) | Key ID of recipient + encrypted $K_S$ |
| 2 | Signature Packet (Tag 2) | Digest type + hashed signature |
| 3 | Compressed Data Packet (Tag 8) | ZIP-compressed content |
| 4 | Symmetrically Encrypted Data (Tag 9) | $C$ (CAST/AES ciphertext) |
| 5 | Literal Data Packet (Tag 11) | Original $M$, filename, timestamp |

**Role of Key ID:** PGP transmits only the lowest **64 bits** of the recipient's public key (the Key ID) along with the encrypted session key, not the entire public key. This allows the receiver to identify *which* of multiple public keys (e.g., work + personal) to use to unwrap the session key. A 64-bit value is sufficient to uniquely identify a key in practice and is much smaller than transmitting the full public key (typically 1024–4096 bits). It also acts as a quick lookup index into the recipient's local public-key ring.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]* **(Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Web of Trust** in PGP. How does it differ from the hierarchical X.509 PKI used in S/MIME? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **PGP key rings** and explain how PGP handles the **public-key trust problem** in a decentralized environment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Web of Trust (WoT)** is PGP's decentralized trust model, introduced because PGP was designed for *grassroots, peer-to-peer* key distribution without relying on a centralized Certificate Authority (CA).

In WoT:
- Each PGP user signs the public keys of other users they have personally verified (e.g., in person, by checking passport).
- The signed keys are uploaded to **public key servers** (e.g., `pgp.mit.edu`).
- Trust propagates **transitively** through chains of signatures: if Alice trusts Bob, and Bob has signed Charlie's key, then Alice can assign Charlie's key a level of **marginal trust**.
- Trust levels: *Unknown*, *Never*, *Marginal*, *Full*, *Ultimate* (for the user's own key).

**Comparison with X.509 / S/MIME:**

| Aspect | PGP (WoT) | S/MIME (X.509) |
|---|---|---|
| Trust model | Decentralized; peer-to-peer signatures | Centralized hierarchy of CAs |
| Trust anchor | Self (user's own key) | Root CA certificate |
| Revocation | Manual, via key servers | CRLs / OCSP responders |
| Cost | Free, open | Requires paid CA |
| Best for | Personal, journalistic, open-source | Corporate, government, banking |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

PGP maintains **two key rings** on each user's machine:

1. **Private Key Ring:** Stores the user's *own* private keys, indexed by Key ID, timestamp, and trust level.
2. **Public Key Ring:** Stores the public keys of all known correspondents, indexed by Key ID.

**How PGP handles trust:**

When Alice receives a message purportedly from Bob, PGP performs a **trust lookup** in Alice's public-key ring:

1. **Validity check:** Is Bob's public key on the ring? If not, fetch from a key server.
2. **Signature verification:** Is Bob's signing key signature valid?
3. **Trust assessment:** How much does Alice trust the introducers who signed Bob's key?

PGP uses two metrics:
- **Key Legitimacy** = cryptographic validity of the key (verified signatures).
- **Owner Trust** = how much *Alice* personally trusts the *owner* of the key (a subjective field).

A key is considered *trusted* for encryption if it has **at least one Marginal or Full trust signature** from introducers on Alice's local ring.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PGP Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong order of operations** — Students frequently write *Compress → Sign → Encrypt*. The correct order for full PGP is **Sign → Compress → Encrypt → Radix-64**. Reversing compress and sign invalidates third-party verifiability. (**–2 marks**)
> 2. **Confusing private/public key usage** — Signing uses the *sender's PRIVATE* key; verifying uses the *sender's PUBLIC* key. Encrypting session key uses the *recipient's PUBLIC* key; decrypting uses the *recipient's PRIVATE* key. (**–2 marks**)
> 3. **Forgetting Radix-64** — Many answers omit the final Base64 encoding step, which is essential for SMTP compatibility. (**–1 mark**)
> 4. **Skipping the "Why"** — Examiners allocate marks for justification, not just steps. A block diagram without the *reason* for ordering scores only ~60%. (**–2 to 3 marks**)
> 5. **Using outdated algorithms without context** — Stating "PGP uses MD5" without noting that modern OpenPGP **mandates SHA-2** shows outdated knowledge. (**–1 mark**)

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **PGP = hybrid cryptosystem** combining symmetric (CAST-128/AES), asymmetric (RSA/ElGamal), hashing (SHA-1/SHA-2), compression (ZIP), and Base64 (Radix-64).
- **Primary goal:** end-to-end secure e-mail with confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation.
- **Signature step:** $\text{Hash}(M) \xrightarrow{E_{K_R}} S$ — uses **sender's private key**.
- **Encryption step:** $M \xrightarrow{E_{K_S}} C$ with $K_S \xrightarrow{E_{K_U}} K_{S,\text{enc}}$ — uses **recipient's public key**.
- **Session key:** 128-bit, **one-time use per message**; provides per-message forward secrecy.
- **Canonical order:** **Sign → Compress → Encrypt → Radix-64 → Email**.
- **Why sign before compress:** preserves third-party verifiability; signatures on compressed data are not re-verifiable.
- **Why compress before encrypt:** encrypted data has no redundancy; compression *must* precede encryption.
- **Why Radix-64:** SMTP only transports **7-bit ASCII**; binary 8-bit bytes may be corrupted.
- **Key ID:** lowest 64 bits of the public key; uniquely identifies which key to use without transmitting the full key.
- **Key rings:** PGP stores both a **private key ring** and a **public key ring** locally.
- **Web of Trust vs. X.509:** PGP is *decentralized* (peer-signed); S/MIME is *hierarchical* (CA-signed).
- **Key revocation:** PGP issues a **Key Revocation Certificate** signed by the owner's private key, propagated via key servers.
- **CFB mode:** CAST-128 in PGP runs in CFB (Cipher Feedback) mode, allowing ciphertext = plaintext length (no padding overhead).
- **Filename preservation:** PGP stores the original filename inside the **Literal Data Packet** so receivers see the original attachment name.
- **Timestamp:** PGP signatures include a **creation timestamp** to mitigate replay attacks.
- **OpenPGP standard:** PGP is now standardized as **RFC 4880** (2016) and **RFC 9580** (2024 update).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the PGP block diagram, always label both the *sender's private key* (for signing) and the *recipient's public key* (for key encryption) on the arrows. KTU evaluators explicitly check the directional correctness of the keys — this single detail often decides between full and partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptographic Keys and Key Rings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cryptographic Keys and Key Rings ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **cryptographic key** is a string of bits used as a parameter by a cryptographic algorithm to transform plaintext into ciphertext (encryption) or vice-versa (decryption), to generate digital signatures, or to verify signatures. Mathematically, a key $k$ is an element drawn from a finite key space $\mathcal{K}$, and the cipher is a family of invertible functions: $$E_k : \mathcal{M} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}, \quad D_k : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{M}$$ where $\mathcal{M}$ is the message (plaintext) space, $\mathcal{C}$ is the ciphertext space, and $k \in \mathcal{K}$ is the key. A **key ring** is an organized data structure (typically a flat-file or database record) that stores and manages a collection of cryptographic keys belonging to a single user, system, or application. Each key in the ring is paired with metadata such as the key identifier (Key ID), algorithm identifier, creation timestamp, expiration date, user ID, and trust signatures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Key rings are not merely key stores — they implement *trust models* through ownertrust fields, signature trust, and validity flags. In PGP/GPG, a key ring is the *de facto* local representation of the global public key infrastructure. ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy Imagine a **physical key ring (like the one holding your house keys)**: - Each **key** on the ring unlocks a specific door (different algorithms/protocols). - The **key ring itself** is the organized carrier that holds everything together. - Some keys are **private** (your house key — never shared). - Some keys are **public** (a copy you may hand to trusted guests — freely distributed). - Each key has a **label** (Key ID) so you know which door it opens. - Older, compromised keys are **revoked** (removed and listed in a revocation certificate). Just as you would not use a bathroom key to start your car, a cryptographic key is bound to a specific algorithm (AES-256 key ≠ RSA-2048 key ≠ HMAC-SHA256 key). ### 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |-----------|---------------|--------------| | Symmetric key length | **128 / 192 / 256 bits** | AES standard (NIST FIPS 197) | | RSA modulus size | **2048 / 3072 / 4096 bits** | NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 | | ECC curve size | **256 / 384 / 521 bits** | NIST P-256, P-384, P-521 | | Key ID (PGP/GPG) | **64-bit fingerprint** | Low 64 bits of SHA-1 hash | | Subkey validity | **Default 2 years** | GPG default expiration | > [!NOTE] > A key's **security strength** is measured in **bits of security**. AES-256 ≈ RSA-15360 ≈ ECC-512 in brute-force resistance. Always match algorithm strengths when designing hybrid systems. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Cryptographic Keys Keys are classified along multiple orthogonal axes: **A. By Function (Cryptographic Role)** - **Encryption key** ($k_E$): Used with an encryption algorithm to provide confidentiality. - **Decryption key** ($k_D$): Used to recover plaintext; equals $k_E$ in symmetric systems, differs in asymmetric systems. - **Signature key** ($k_S$): Private key used to create digital signatures (authenticity + integrity). - **Verification key** ($k_V$): Public counterpart for signature verification. - **Authentication/Key-Exchange key**: Used in protocols like TLS, IKE, Kerberos. - **Master key / Key-Encryption Key (KEK)**: Used only to wrap (encrypt) other keys. - **Session key**: Short-lived symmetric key generated for one communication session. **B. By Relationship Between Encrypt/Decrypt** - **Symmetric (secret) keys**: $k_E = k_D$ (or trivially derivable). Fast (AES, ChaCha20). - **Asymmetric (public) keys**: $k_E \neq k_D$, mathematically related but computationally infeasible to derive one from the other (RSA, ECC, ElGamal). **C. By Lifecycle Stage** - **Initial key**: Bootstrap key installed at manufacture or first boot. - **Static key**: Long-lived identity key (valid years). - **Ephemeral key**: Per-session, single-use (forward secrecy). - **Derived key**: Generated from a master via a Key Derivation Function (KDF). ### 2.2 Key Properties (the "Why" Behind Good Keys) - **Unpredictability**: Must be drawn from a uniform distribution; weak RNGs break even strong algorithms. - **Sufficient Length**: Brute-force cost grows as $2^N$ for an $N$-bit key. - **Confidentiality of Private Keys**: The private key never leaves the secure boundary. - **Integrity of Public Keys**: Bound to identities via certificates (X.509) or web-of-trust (PGP). - **Forward Secrecy (FS)**: Compromise of long-term key does not compromise past session keys. - **Domain Separation**: Same key MUST NOT be used for two different algorithms (cross-protocol attacks). ### 2.3 Anatomy of a Key Ring (PGP/GPG Model) A key ring contains two logical sub-rings: 1. **Public Key Ring** (stored locally, semi-public data): - Public key bits - Key ID, User ID, algorithm - Trust signatures from other users - Validity period 2. **Private Key Ring** (highly protected): - Private key bits - Passphrase-derived wrapping key (PBKDF2) - Timestamp of creation Trust in PGP is computed by the formula: $$T_{\text{valid}}(B \rightarrow A) = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot f(T_{\text{owner}}(B), \text{sig\_count}_i)$$ where $w_i$ is the introducer's trust weight and $f$ is the signature trust function. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Notation | Notes | |---------|--------------------|-------| | Symmetric cipher | $C = E_k(P)$, $P = D_k(C)$ | $k_E = k_D$ | | Asymmetric cipher | $C = E_{k_{\text{pub}}}(P)$, $P = D_{k_{\text{priv}}}(C)$ | Computationally infeasible to invert without trapdoor | | RSA key generation | $n = p \cdot q$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | $p, q$ are large primes | | RSA encryption | $C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$ | | | RSA decryption | $M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ | | | Diffie-Hellman shared secret | $K = (g^b)^a \equiv (g^a)^b \pmod{p}$ | Forward secrecy via ephemeral keys | | HKDF | $K = \text{HKDF-Expand}(\text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt}, IKM), \text{info}, L)$ | RFC 5869 | | HMAC | $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((K' \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K' \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M))$ | $K'$ is normalized key | | Entropy of key | $H(X) = -\sum_i p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ bits | Max when uniform: $H = N$ | | Work factor (brute force) | $W = 2^N / r$ operations | $r$ = attacker's parallel rate | | PGP Key ID | $\text{KeyID} = \text{SHA-1}(\text{pub\_key\_packet}) \bmod 2^{64}$ | Last 64 bits | > **Engineering Utility:** Key rings underpin every practical deployment — TLS (server's certificate chain), SSH (`~/.ssh/authorized_keys`), PGP email, disk encryption (LUKS key slots), and IoT device identity provisioning. Understanding the data model is essential for cryptographic audits. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Worked Example: RSA Key Generation (Full Derivation) **Problem:** Generate an RSA key pair using small primes $p = 61$, $q = 53$ and $e = 17$. Compute $d$, encrypt $M = 42$, then decrypt. **Step 1 — Compute the modulus:** $$n = p \cdot q = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$ **Step 2 — Compute Euler's totient:** $$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$ **Step 3 — Verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$:** $$\gcd(17, 3120) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ Since $17$ is prime and does not divide $3120$, the inverse exists. **Step 4 — Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:** We need integers $x, y$ such that $17x + 3120y = 1$. $$ \begin{aligned} 3120 &= 17 \cdot 183 + 9 \\ 17 &= 9 \cdot 1 + 8 \\ 9 &= 8 \cdot 1 + 1 \\ 8 &= 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ Back-substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 9 - 8 \cdot 1 \\ &= 9 - (17 - 9 \cdot 1) \cdot 1 \\ &= 9 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 1 \\ &= (3120 - 17 \cdot 183) \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 1 \\ &= 3120 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 366 - 17 \cdot 1 \\ &= 3120 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 367 \end{aligned} $$ So $x = -367$, meaning $17 \cdot (-367) \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$. Therefore: $$d \equiv -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753 \pmod{3120}$$ **Public key:** $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$. **Private key:** $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$. **Step 5 — Encrypt $M = 42$:** $$ C \equiv M^e \pmod{n} \equiv 42^{17} \pmod{3233} $$ Using successive squaring (modular exponentiation): $$ \begin{aligned} 42^1 &\equiv 42 \\ 42^2 &\equiv 1764 \\ 42^4 &\equiv 1764^2 = 3{,}111{,}696 \equiv 3{,}111{,}696 \bmod 3233 = 1606 \\ 42^8 &\equiv 1606^2 = 2{,}579{,}236 \equiv 2{,}579{,}236 \bmod 3233 = 2{,}569{,}836 - 794 \cdot 3233 \\ &\text{Simplify: } 2{,}579{,}236 / 3233 \approx 797.6, \text{ so } 2{,}579{,}236 - 797 \cdot 3233 = 2{,}579{,}236 - 2{,}576{,}701 = 2535 \\ 42^8 &\equiv 2535 \\ 42^{16} &\equiv 2535^2 = 6{,}426{,}225 \equiv 6{,}426{,}225 \bmod 3233 \\ &\approx 1987.4 \rightarrow 6{,}426{,}225 - 1987 \cdot 3233 = 6{,}426{,}225 - 6{,}424{,}771 = 1454 \\ 42^{16} &\equiv 1454 \end{aligned} $$ Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so: $$42^{17} \equiv 42^{16} \cdot 42 \equiv 1454 \cdot 42 = 61{,}068$$ $$61{,}068 \bmod 3233: \quad 61{,}068 / 3233 \approx 18.89, \quad 61{,}068 - 18 \cdot 3233 = 61{,}068 - 58{,}194 = 2874$$ $$\boxed{C = 2874}$$ **Step 6 — Decrypt $C = 2874$:** $$M' \equiv C^d \pmod{n} \equiv 2874^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$ By Euler's theorem, $M' = 2874^{2753} \bmod 3233 \equiv 42 \pmod{3233}$. (Verification by CRT or direct modular exponentiation; the algebra is symmetric.) Thus the original message is recovered: $M = 42$. ✓ ### 3.2 Worked Example: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Let $p = 23$ (a safe prime for the demo), $g = 5$ (generator), and the participants choose secret exponents: - Alice: $a = 6$ - Bob: $b = 15$ **Public values sent over the wire:** $$A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23 = 15{,}625 \bmod 23 = 8$$ $$B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23$$ Compute $5^{15} \bmod 23$ by repeated squaring: $$ \begin{aligned} 5^1 &\equiv 5 \\ 5^2 &\equiv 25 \equiv 2 \\ 5^4 &\equiv 2^2 = 4 \\ 5^8 &\equiv 4^2 = 16 \\ 5^{15} &= 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \\ &\equiv 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \bmod 23 \\ &\equiv 16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 46 = 18 \\ &\equiv 18 \cdot 2 = 36 \equiv 36 - 23 = 13 \\ &\equiv 13 \cdot 5 = 65 \equiv 65 - 46 = 19 \\ \end{aligned} $$ So $B = 19$. **Shared secret computed by Alice:** $$K = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23$$ $$ \begin{aligned} 19^2 &\equiv 361 \bmod 23: \quad 361 / 23 = 15.69, \quad 361 - 15 \cdot 23 = 361 - 345 = 16 \\ 19^4 &\equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 11 \cdot 23 = 256 - 253 = 3 \\ 19^6 &= 19^4 \cdot 19^2 \equiv 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \equiv 48 - 2 \cdot 23 = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Shared secret computed by Bob:** $$K = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23$$ $$ \begin{aligned} 8^2 &\equiv 64 \equiv 64 - 46 = 18 \\ 8^4 &\equiv 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14 \cdot 23 = 324 - 322 = 2 \\ 8^8 &\equiv 2^2 = 4 \\ 8^{15} &= 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \bmod 23 \\ &\equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \\ &\equiv 8 \cdot 18 = 144 \equiv 144 - 6 \cdot 23 = 144 - 138 = 6 \\ &\equiv 6 \cdot 8 = 48 \equiv 2 \end{aligned} $$ Both Alice and Bob arrive at $K = 2$. ✓ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Key Types vs. Properties | Key Type | Confidentiality | Integrity | Authentication | Non-Repudiation | Speed | |----------|:---------------:|:---------:|:--------------:|:---------------:|:-----:| | AES Symmetric | ✓ | ✗ (need MAC) | ✗ | ✗ | **Fast** | | RSA Private | — | ✓ (sign) | ✓ (decrypt) | ✓ | Slow | | ECC Private | — | ✓ (sign) | ✓ | ✓ | **Fast** | | HMAC Key | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Fast | | Session Key | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Fast | | KEK | ✓ (for keys) | — | — | — | Medium | ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Key Ring Architecture (PGP/GPG Model) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef store fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1 classDef meta fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#bf360c classDef alg fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20 classDef user fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:1px,color:#4a148c classDef tr fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,stroke-width:1px,color:#880e4f subgraph PUBRING["PUBLIC KEY RING"] direction TB PK1["PUBLIC KEY 1"]:::store PK2["PUBLIC KEY 2"]:::store PK3["PUBLIC KEY 3"]:::store PKID1["Key ID : A1B2C3D4"]:::meta PKUID1["UserID : alice@example.com"]:::user PKALG1["Algorithm : RSA 4096"]:::alg PKVAL1["Valid Until : 2027-01-01"]:::meta PK1 --- PKID1 PK1 --- PKUID1 PK1 --- PKALG1 PK1 --- PKVAL1 subgraph TRUSTMETA["TRUST METADATA"] direction LR TS1["Signer Trust"]:::tr TS2["Owner Trust"]:::tr TS3["Validity Flag"]:::tr end PK1 --- TRUSTMETA end subgraph PRVRING["PRIVATE KEY RING"] direction TB SK1["PRIVATE KEY 1 AES WRAPPED"]:::store SK2["PRIVATE KEY 2 AES WRAPPED"]:::store SKID1["Key ID : A1B2C3D4"]:::meta SKTS1["Timestamp : 2024-06-15"]:::meta SKPB1["PBKDF2 Passphrase Hash"]:::meta SK1 --- SKID1 SK1 --- SKTS1 SK1 --- SKPB1 end PUBRING <--> MATCH["MATCH BY KEY ID"]:::meta PRVRING <--> MATCH USER["USER QUERY: SELECT KEY FOR recipient@x.com"]:::user USER --> PUBRING USER --> PRVRING ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Key Lifecycle and Trust Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef state fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1 classDef action fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20 classDef alarm fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#b71c1c classDef store fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px,color:#f57f17 GEN["KEY GENERATION
RNG or KDF"]:::action STORE["STORE IN KEY RING
with metadata"]:::store USE["ACTIVE USE
Encrypt, Sign, Auth"]:::state ROTATE["ROTATION
New subkey derived"]:::action EXP["EXPIRATION
Validity = 0"]:::alarm REV["REVOCATION
Revocation Certificate Issued"]:::alarm DEST["DESTROY
Secure Zeroise"]:::alarm GEN --> STORE STORE --> USE USE -->|"Time Elapses"| ROTATE ROTATE -->|"Old Key"| EXP USE -->|"Compromise"| REV EXP --> DEST REV --> DEST USE -.->|"Forward Secrecy"| EPH["EPHEMERAL KEY
per session"]:::store ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Public Key Infrastructure (Trust Hierarchy) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef root fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#b71c1c classDef ca fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#e65100 classDef ent fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20 classDef user fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:1px,color:#0d47a1 ROOT["ROOT CA
Self-signed master key"]:::root ICA1["INTERMEDIATE CA 1"]:::ca ICA2["INTERMEDIATE CA 2"]:::ca SRV1["Web Server Cert"]:::ent SRV2["Mail Server Cert"]:::ent CLI1["Client User Alice"]:::user CLI2["Client User Bob"]:::user ROOT ---|"Signs"| ICA1 ROOT ---|"Signs"| ICA2 ICA1 ---|"Signs"| SRV1 ICA1 ---|"Signs"| SRV2 ICA2 ---|"Issues"| CLI1 ICA2 ---|"Issues"| CLI2 SRV1 -.->|"TLS Handshake"| CLI2 CLI1 -.->|"S/MIME Sign"| SRV2 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]** **"Differentiate between a symmetric key and an asymmetric key.
