$$KE_i = g^{x'} \bmod p \quad (\text{new DH value if PFS})$$

---

### 3.2.2 Message 2 (Responder → Initiator)

Accepts the proposal and sends its own nonce + DH value.

$$HASH(2) = PRF(SKEYID\_a,\ M\text{-}ID \mid SA \mid N_r \mid KE_r)$$

$$N_r = \text{fresh nonce}$$

$$KE_r = g^{y'} \bmod p \quad (\text{if PFS})$$

---

### 3.2.3 Message 3 (Initiator → Responder)

Confirms the exchange.

$$HASH(3) = PRF(SKEYID\_a,\ 0 \mid M\text{-}ID \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

The leading `0` octet ensures the hash inputs differ from HASH(1) and HASH(2).

---

### 3.2.4 Derivation of IPSec KEYMAT

The ESP session keys are derived from:

$$KEYMAT = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ \text{ESP} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

If, for instance, AES-128 needs 128 bits (16 bytes) and SHA-1 needs 160 bits (20 bytes) of keying material, the PRF output may be expanded by iteration.

Let $L$ be the required key length in bits. The PRF is repeated:

$$K_1 = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ \text{ESP} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

$$K_2 = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ K_1 \mid \text{ESP} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

$$K_3 = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ K_2 \mid \text{ESP} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

$$KEYMAT = K_1 \mid K_2 \mid K_3 \mid \ldots \quad \text{(truncated to $L$ bits)}$$

For example, if each PRF call returns 160 bits (HMAC-SHA1 output), and we need 16+20 = 288 bits:

- Take the first 288 bits of `K1 ∥ K2` (320 bits, truncated).
- This produces the **encryption key** (16 bytes) and **authentication key** (20 bytes).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Main Mode vs Aggressive Mode vs Quick Mode

| Property | Main Mode (Phase 1) | Aggressive Mode (Phase 1) | Quick Mode (Phase 2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of messages | **6** | **3** | **3** |
| Total round trips | 3 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| Identity protection | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes (uses SKEYID_e)** |
| DH exchange | Yes | Yes | Optional (only with PFS) |
| Authentication | Yes | Yes | Inherits Phase 1 |
| SA established | ISAKMP SA | ISAKMP SA | IPSec SA |
| Encrypted | Only Msg 5,6 | None (cleartext) | All 3 messages |
| Vulnerability to MITM | Lower | Higher (no ID protection) | Lower |

---

## 3.4 Decision Flow — Choosing Between Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to use Aggressive Mode?**
> When the **Initiator's IP address is dynamic** (e.g., a remote-access VPN client). Main Mode requires the peer to look up the PSK based on the Initiator's IP address, which is impossible if the IP is unknown in advance. Aggressive Mode sends the ID in cleartext so the Responder can select the correct PSK.
> 
> **When to use Main Mode?**
> Always preferred in **site-to-site VPNs** where both peers have static IPs.

---

## 3.5 IKEv2 — The Modern Replacement

IKEv2 (RFC 7296) eliminates the Phase 1 / Phase 2 split and replaces it with four **logical exchanges** within a single IKE_SA:

### 3.5.1 The Four IKEv2 Exchanges

| Exchange | Messages | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **IKE_SA_INIT** | 2 (1 ↔ 2) | Negotiate cryptographic algorithms, exchange nonces and DH public values |
| **IKE_AUTH** | 2 (3 ↔ 4) | Authenticate identities, exchange certificates/PSK, create first Child SA |
| **CREATE_CHILD_SA** | 2 (per pair) | Create or rekey additional Child SAs |
| **INFORMATIONAL** | 1 (or 2) | Notify errors, delete SAs, check liveness |

### 3.5.2 Mapping IKEv1 to IKEv2

| IKEv1 Concept | IKEv2 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Phase 1 Main Mode | IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH |
| Phase 1 Aggressive Mode | IKE_AUTH (single round-trip) |
| Phase 2 Quick Mode | CREATE_CHILD_SA |
| ISAKMP SA | IKE_SA |
| IPSec SA | Child SA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why did IKEv2 win?** It cuts the initial handshake from 6+3 = 9 messages down to **4 messages**, supports NAT-T (RFC 4559) natively, adds reliable delivery (via sequence numbers), and detects dead peers via INFORMATIONAL exchanges.

---

## 3.6 Cookie / Anti-Clogging Mechanism (DoS Defense)

The Cookie exchange is designed to protect the **Responder** from a flooding attack where an attacker opens thousands of half-completed Phase 1 sessions.

**Mechanism:**

1. Responder receives Message 1.
2. Instead of immediately allocating state, Responder replies with a Cookie request.
3. Initiator echoes the Cookie back in Message 2.
4. Responder now allocates state.

The Cookie value is generated as:

$$CKY_r = Hash(IP_i,\ Port_i,\ \text{Secret}_r)$$

where `Secret_r` is periodically refreshed. An attacker who does not receive the Responder's reply cannot forge a valid Cookie, because the attacker does not know `Secret_r`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IKEv1 Two-Phase Architecture (High-Level Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Application Data Flow"] --> B["IPSec Layer"]
    B --> C{"ISAKMP SA Exists?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Phase 1: Main or Aggressive Mode"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E{"IPSec SA Exists?"}
    D --> F["Derive SKEYID family"]
    F --> G["ISAKMP SA Established"]
    G --> E
    E -- "No" --> H["Phase 2: Quick Mode"]
    E -- "Yes" --> I["Apply ESP or AH to User Data"]
    H --> J["Derive KEYMAT"]
    J --> K["IPSec SA Established"]
    K --> I
    I --> L["Encrypted / Authenticated Traffic"]
```

## 4.2 Phase 1 Main Mode — 6 Message Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant R as Responder
    I->>R: MSG1 HDR + SA Proposal
    R->>I: MSG2 HDR + SA Choice
    I->>R: MSG3 HDR + KEi + Ni
    R->>I: MSG4 HDR + KEr + Nr
    Note over I,R: Both compute DH Shared Secret g^xy
    I->>R: MSG5 HDR* + IDii + HASH_I (Encrypted)
    R->>I: MSG6 HDR* + IDir + HASH_R (Encrypted)
    Note over I,R: ISAKMP SA Established
```

## 4.3 Phase 1 Aggressive Mode — 3 Message Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant R as Responder
    I->>R: MSG1 HDR + SA + KEi + Ni + IDii
    R->>I: MSG2 HDR + SA + KEr + Nr + IDir + HASH_R
    I->>R: MSG3 HDR + HASH_I
    Note over I,R: ISAKMP SA Established (No Identity Protection)
```

## 4.4 Phase 2 Quick Mode — 3 Message Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant R as Responder
    I->>R: MSG1 HDR* + HASH1 + SA + Ni + KEi
    R->>I: MSG2 HDR* + HASH2 + SA + Nr + KEr
    I->>R: MSG3 HDR* + HASH3
    Note over I,R: Both derive KEYMAT
    Note over I,R: IPSec SA Established
```

## 4.5 Key Derivation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["PSK / Signature / PKEnc"] --> B["SKEYID Master Secret"]
    B --> C["SKEYID_d Derivation Key"]
    B --> D["SKEYID_a Authentication Key"]
    B --> E["SKEYID_e Encryption Key"]
    C --> F["KEYMAT for ESP AH"]
    D --> G["HMAC for IKE Messages"]
    E --> H["Encryption for IKE Messages"]
    F --> I["ESP Session Keys"]
    F --> J["AH Session Keys"]
```

## 4.6 IKEv2 Four-Message Initial Exchange

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant R as Responder
    I->>R: IKE_SA_INIT SA + KEi + Ni
    R->>I: IKE_SA_INIT SA + KEr + Nr
    Note over I,R: IKE_SA created
    I->>R: IKE_AUTH IDi + AUTH + SA + TSi + TSr
    R->>I: IKE_AUTH IDr + AUTH + SA + TSi + TSr
    Note over I,R: First Child SA created
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:**
> When asked to "draw the IKE Phase 1 exchange", always label the **first 4 messages as cleartext** and the **last 2 as encrypted (HDR\*)**. Forgetting the asterisk is a common cause of lost marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Differentiate between Phase 1 and Phase 2 of IKE. What are the two modes available in Phase 1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Phase 1 of IKE establishes a secure, authenticated channel between two peers known as the **ISAKMP SA** (or IKE SA). It authenticates the peers, negotiates cryptographic algorithms, and performs a Diffie–Hellman key exchange to derive the SKEYID family of keys.

Phase 2 of IKE uses the ISAKMP SA established in Phase 1 to negotiate the actual **IPSec SAs** that protect user data traffic through the AH or ESP protocols.

The two modes available in Phase 1 are:
- **Main Mode** — uses 6 messages and provides identity protection (identity is encrypted).
- **Aggressive Mode** — uses 3 messages, is faster, but does not provide identity protection.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is the purpose of the SKEYID family of keys in IKE? List its members.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The SKEYID family is derived from the master secret `SKEYID` after the Diffie–Hellman exchange in Phase 1. Its purpose is to produce independent sub-keys for different cryptographic roles:

1. **SKEYID_d** — Derivation key, used to generate KEYMAT for IPSec session keys.
2. **SKEYID_a** — Authentication key, used as the HMAC key to verify integrity of IKE messages.
3. **SKEYID_e** — Encryption key, used to encrypt IKE messages (including all Phase 2 traffic).

The master secret `SKEYID` itself is never used directly for any operation; only its derived children are.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Phase 1 Main Mode** exchange of IKE in detail. List all the messages exchanged and clearly identify which messages are encrypted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the derivation of the **SKEYID family** of keys in IKE. Write the formulas for `SKEYID`, `SKEYID_d`, `SKEYID_a`, and `SKEYID_e`, and state the purpose of each derived key. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution:

Phase 1 Main Mode is the standard, identity-protected exchange that creates the **ISAKMP SA**. It uses **6 messages in 3 round trips**:

| Msg # | Direction | Content | Encrypted? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initiator → Responder | HDR, SA (Proposal) | No |
| 2 | Responder → Initiator | HDR, SA (Choice) | No |
| 3 | Initiator → Responder | HDR, KEi, Ni | No |
| 4 | Responder → Initiator | HDR, KEr, Nr | No |
| 5 | Initiator → Responder | HDR*, IDii, HASH_I | **Yes** |
| 6 | Responder → Initiator | HDR*, IDir, HASH_R | **Yes** |

```
Initiator                              Responder
   |                                       |
   |------- MSG1: HDR, SA (Prop) -------->|
   |<------ MSG2: HDR, SA (Choice) --------|
   |                                       |
   |------- MSG3: HDR, KEi, Ni ---------->|
   |<------ MSG4: HDR, KEr, Nr ------------|
   |                                       |
   |  [Both peers compute g^xy mod p]      |
   |  [Both peers derive SKEYID family]    |
   |                                       |
   |------- MSG5: HDR*, IDii, HASH_I ---->|
   |<------ MSG6: HDR*, IDir, HASH_R ------|
   |                                       |
   |       ISAKMP SA Established           |
```

**Explanation of each phase within Main Mode:**

- **Messages 1–2 (SA Negotiation):** Both peers agree on the **Protection Suite** — encryption algorithm, hash algorithm, authentication method, DH group, and lifetime.
- **Messages 3–4 (Key Exchange):** The two peers exchange their DH public values `g^x mod p` and `g^y mod p` along with nonces `Ni` and `Nr`. After Message 4, each peer independently computes the **shared DH secret** $g^{xy} \bmod p$.
- **Messages 5–6 (Authenticated, Encrypted Exchange):** Each peer proves its identity using a HASH payload. These messages are encrypted with `SKEYID_e` and authenticated with `SKEYID_a`, providing **identity protection**.

Upon successful verification of Message 6, the **ISAKMP SA** is established.

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

After Messages 4 of Phase 1 Main Mode, both peers have all the necessary inputs to derive the SKEYID family. The derivation depends on the authentication method.

**For Pre-Shared Key (PSK) Authentication:**

**Step 1 — Master Secret:**

$$SKEYID = PRF(PSK,\ N_i \mid N_r)$$

The PSK is known only to the two legitimate peers; its inclusion binds the master secret to the peer identities.

**Step 2 — Derivation Key (SKEYID_d):**

$$SKEYID\_d = PRF(SKEYID,\ g^{xy} \mid CKY_i \mid CKY_r \mid 2)$$

**Step 3 — Authentication Key (SKEYID_a):**

$$SKEYID\_a = PRF(SKEYID,\ SKEYID\_d \mid g^{xy} \mid CKY_i \mid CKY_r \mid 3)$$

**Step 4 — Encryption Key (SKEYID_e):**

$$SKEYID\_e = PRF(SKEYID,\ SKEYID\_a \mid g^{xy} \mid CKY_i \mid CKY_r \mid 4)$$

**For Digital Signature Authentication:**

$$SKEYID = PRF(N_i \mid N_r,\ g^{xy})$$

**Purpose of each key:**

| Key | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **SKEYID** | Master secret — never used directly; only inputs to PRF for sub-key derivation |
| **SKEYID_d** | Derivation key for IPSec session keys (KEYMAT) |
| **SKEYID_a** | Authentication key — used as HMAC key for integrity of IKE messages |
| **SKEYID_e** | Encryption key — used to encrypt subsequent IKE messages |

The constants `2`, `3`, `4` are single-byte domain separators ensuring the three derived keys are cryptographically independent even though they originate from the same `SKEYID`.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Phase 2 Quick Mode** exchange of IKE. What role does Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) play in this phase? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Cookie exchange mechanism** used in IKE for DoS protection. Also describe how IPSec session keys (KEYMAT) are derived from the SKEYID family. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution:

Phase 2 Quick Mode is the exchange that establishes **IPSec SAs** (one or more) under the protection of the already-established ISAKMP SA. Quick Mode uses **3 messages**:

```
Initiator                              Responder
   |                                       |
   |--- MSG1: HDR*, HASH(1), SA, Ni, KEi ->|
   |                                       |
   |<-- MSG2: HDR*, HASH(2), SA, Nr, KEr --|
   |                                       |
   |--- MSG3: HDR*, HASH(3) ------------->|
   |                                       |
   |       IPSec SA Established            |
```

**Message details:**

- **MSG1:** Initiator proposes one or more IPSec SA proposals (ESP/AH, algorithm, mode, lifetime, optional PFS group), along with a fresh nonce `Ni` and (if PFS) a fresh DH value `KEi`.
- **MSG2:** Responder accepts a single proposal, returns its own nonce `Nr` and (if PFS) its fresh DH value `KEr`.
- **MSG3:** Initiator confirms the exchange.

**Hash computations:**

$$HASH(1) = PRF(SKEYID\_a,\ M\text{-}ID \mid SA \mid N_i \mid KE_i)$$

$$HASH(2) = PRF(SKEYID\_a,\ M\text{-}ID \mid SA \mid N_r \mid KE_r)$$

$$HASH(3) = PRF(SKEYID\_a,\ 0 \mid M\text{-}ID \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

All three messages are **encrypted** with `SKEYID_e` and authenticated with `SKEYID_a` (hence the `HDR*` notation).

**Role of Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS):**

By default, Quick Mode **does not** perform a new Diffie–Hellman exchange — it reuses `g^xy` from Phase 1. This means if an attacker later compromises a long-term secret, they could derive `SKEYID_d` and decrypt all past IPSec traffic.

If **PFS is enabled**, a *fresh* DH exchange is performed inside Quick Mode, producing a new shared secret `g^{x'y'}`. The KEYMAT is then derived using this *new* value mixed into `SKEYID_d`. As a result:

- Compromise of the long-term secret **does not** reveal past session keys.
- Each Phase 2 negotiation is cryptographically independent.

The trade-off: PFS increases computational cost because a fresh modular exponentiation must be performed for every Quick Mode.

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Cookie Exchange for DoS Protection:**

The Cookie mechanism protects the **Responder** from being overwhelmed by attackers who open numerous half-completed Phase 1 sessions. Each open session consumes memory and CPU at the Responder.

**Mechanism:**

1. The Initiator sends Message 1 (HDR, SA).
2. Instead of immediately allocating state and computing DH values, the Responder replies with a **Cookie Request** containing:
$$CKY_r = Hash(IP_i \mid Port_i \mid Secret_r \mid Timestamp)$$
3. The Initiator must resend Message 1 with the Cookie echoed back.
4. The Responder verifies the Cookie (by recomputing the hash) and **only then** allocates state.

Because `Secret_r` is rotated periodically, an off-path attacker cannot forge a valid Cookie. Thus, the Responder is protected from being forced into expensive cryptographic computations by random spoofed source IPs.

**Derivation of IPSec KEYMAT:**

Once Phase 2 Quick Mode completes, the IPSec session keys are derived as follows:

$$KEYMAT = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ \text{protocol} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

where:
- `protocol` = ESP or AH (octet value)
- `SPI` = Security Parameter Index of the new SA
- `Ni`, `Nr` = nonces from Quick Mode
- `SKEYID_d` = derivation key from Phase 1 (or fresh if PFS was used)

If more keying material is needed than a single PRF output provides, the derivation iterates:

$$K_1 = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ \text{protocol} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

$$K_2 = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ K_1 \mid \text{protocol} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

$$K_3 = PRF(SKEYID\_d,\ K_2 \mid \text{protocol} \mid SPI \mid N_i \mid N_r)$$

$$KEYMAT = \text{first } L \text{ bits of } K_1 \mid K_2 \mid K_3$$

For example, for **ESP with AES-128 and HMAC-SHA-1**, we need 128 bits (encryption) + 160 bits (authentication) = 288 bits of KEYMAT.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to mark HDR\* for encrypted messages.** The asterisk distinguishes plaintext from encrypted IKE headers. Diagrams without this marker lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing SKEYID with SKEYID_e.** `SKEYID` is the *master* secret. `SKEYID_e` is the *encryption* key. Examiners specifically look for correct naming.
> 3. **Writing `g^(x+y)` instead of `g^(xy)`.** The DH shared secret is `g^(xy) mod p`, NOT `g^x + g^y`. This single-character error can cost 1 mark.
> 4. **Not stating the constants `2`, `3`, `4` in SKEYID derivation.** These are required to obtain full marks on the formula derivation question.
> 5. **Mixing up Phase 1 and Phase 2 message counts.** Always state clearly: Phase 1 Main Mode = 6 messages; Phase 1 Aggressive = 3 messages; Phase 2 Quick Mode = 3 messages.
> 6. **Omitting PFS explanation.** If the question mentions Phase 2, always discuss whether PFS is being used — it is a favorite sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for IKE Phases:**

- ⭐ **IKE has TWO phases in IKEv1**: Phase 1 establishes ISAKMP SA; Phase 2 establishes IPSec SA.
- ⭐ **Phase 1 Main Mode** = 6 messages, identity-protected, preferred for site-to-site VPNs.
- ⭐ **Phase 1 Aggressive Mode** = 3 messages, no identity protection, used when Initiator IP is dynamic.
- ⭐ **Phase 2 Quick Mode** = 3 messages, always encrypted using `SKEYID_e`.
- ⭐ **SKEYID family consists of FOUR keys**: `SKEYID` (master), `SKEYID_d` (derivation), `SKEYID_a` (authentication), `SKEYID_e` (encryption).
- ⭐ **SKEYID_d** is the only key that produces KEYMAT; KEYMAT is the actual session key used by ESP/AH.
- ⭐ **DH shared secret** = $g^{xy} \bmod p$ — never written as $g^{x+y}$.
- ⭐ **PFS** = a fresh DH exchange in Quick Mode; increases security but adds computational cost.
- ⭐ **Cookie exchange** protects Responder from DoS flooding.
- ⭐ **IKEv2** reduces the entire initial handshake to **4 messages** (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH).
- ⭐ **Encryption algorithms**: DES, 3DES, AES-128, AES-256.
- ⭐ **Hash algorithms**: MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512.
- ⭐ **Authentication methods**: PSK, Digital Signatures (RSA), Public Key Encryption.
- ⭐ **DH Groups**: Group 1 (768-bit), Group 2 (1024-bit), Group 5 (1536-bit), Group 14 (2048-bit).
- ⭐ **HDR vs HDR\***: Plain header vs encrypted header (with asterisk).
- ⭐ **Lifetime**: Default ISAKMP SA lifetime = 28800s (8 hours); IPSec SA = 3600s (1 hour).
- ⭐ **ISAKMP SA is bidirectional**; **IPSec SAs are unidirectional** (one for each direction of traffic).
- ⭐ **IKEv2 sequence numbers** provide reliable delivery — IKEv1 used UDP without reliability.
- ⭐ **HASH(3) in Quick Mode** uses a leading `0` octet to differentiate its input from HASH(1) and HASH(2).
- ⭐ **NAT-Traversal (NAT-T)** is built into IKEv2 but was an add-on (RFC 3947) in IKEv1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Security Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Web Security Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Security Considerations** encompass the set of design principles, protocols, cryptographic mechanisms, and best-practice countermeasures engineered to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data exchanged across the **World Wide Web (WWW)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this domain addresses the vulnerabilities introduced when an enterprise's network is extended over public TCP/IP infrastructure — particularly the Internet — where web clients (browsers), web servers, application servers, and supporting back-end databases become attack surfaces.

The principal security concerns span four layers of the web stack:

| Layer | Component | Primary Threat Class |
|---|---|---|
| Network | TCP/IP, DNS, BGP | Eavesdropping, DNS spoofing, DDoS |
| Transport | TLS/SSL, HTTPS | Man-in-the-Middle (MITM), downgrade attacks |
| Application | HTTP, REST, SOAP | Injection, XSS, CSRF, broken authentication |
| Data | Cookies, sessions, tokens, DB queries | Session hijacking, SQL injection, IDOR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The Module 3 learning outcomes map Web Security to Course Outcomes **CO3** — *"Apply cryptographic and protocol-level mechanisms to secure web and transport-layer communication."* Expect 14-mark derivations on **SSL/TLS handshake**, **HTTPS operation**, and **threat taxonomy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Mailbox

Imagine the Web as a city filled with **glass mailboxes** (HTTP requests) that anyone walking past can read, alter, or even swap. **Web Security Considerations** are the rules you enforce to convert each glass box into a *tamper-proof, signed, encrypted vault*:

- **TLS/SSL** → the **sealed, registered envelope** — only sender and receiver have the key.
- **Digital Certificates** → the **government-issued ID card** verifying identity.
- **HTTPS** → the entire **secure courier service** combining TLS with HTTP.
- **Same-Origin Policy** → the rule that says "only residents of this building may open this mailbox."
- **CSP (Content Security Policy)** → a **guest list** controlling who is allowed to leave scripts inside your page.
- **Session Cookies (`HttpOnly`, `Secure`)** → a **tamper-proof wristband** required for re-entry.

Without these controls, every browser-server transaction is like shouting your credit card across a crowded marketplace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The standard for modern web encryption is **TLS 1.3** (RFC 8446, finalized **2018**), which has effectively deprecated TLS 1.0/1.1 and all SSL versions. KTU examiners expect you to mention **TLS 1.3** explicitly when describing current secure web deployments.

## 1.3 Why Web Security is Non-Negotiable in Modern Computing

The web is the **de facto application delivery platform** — banking, healthcare, e-governance, and IoT control planes all traverse HTTP(S). Three forces drive its criticality:

1. **Attack Surface Expansion** — Every browser tab, REST endpoint, and third-party script is a potential entry vector.
2. **High-Value Asset Transit** — Credentials, PII, payment tokens, and intellectual property traverse the wire.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — GDPR, India's **DPDP Act 2023**, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA mandate cryptographic protection of web traffic.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question asks "secure a web transaction," your answer should simultaneously address **confidentiality** (encryption), **integrity** (MAC/digest), **authentication** (certificates), and **replay protection** (nonces/timestamps). This is the **CIA+N** quartet.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Web Security Threat Taxonomy (OWASP-Aligned)

The **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP)** maintains the industry-standard catalogue of web vulnerabilities. The following taxonomy is what KTU examiners expect you to reproduce:

### 2.1.1 Injection Attacks
- **SQL Injection (SQLi)** — Malicious SQL fragments inserted into input fields to manipulate database queries.
- **Command Injection** — OS commands embedded in user input (e.g., `; rm -rf /`).
- **LDAP / XPath Injection** — Targeting directory services and XML stores.

**Countermeasures:** *Parameterized queries*, *stored procedures*, *input whitelisting*, *ORM frameworks*, *least-privilege DB accounts*.

### 2.1.2 Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
- **Reflected XSS** — Malicious script in URL reflected back in server response.
- **Stored XSS** — Payload persisted in DB (e.g., forum post) and served to all visitors.
- **DOM-based XSS** — Client-side script writes attacker-controlled data into the DOM unsafely.

**Countermeasures:** Output encoding, Content Security Policy (CSP), HttpOnly cookies, sanitization libraries.

### 2.1.3 Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
An attacker tricks an authenticated user's browser into submitting a forged request to a target application, exploiting the browser's automatic cookie attachment.

**Countermeasures:** Synchronizer Token Pattern, SameSite cookie attribute, Origin/Referer header checks, double-submit cookies.

### 2.1.4 Session Management Failures
- **Session Hijacking** — Stealing session IDs via XSS or network sniffing.
- **Session Fixation** — Attacker pre-sets session ID before user login.
- **Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)** — Predictable resource identifiers in URLs.

**Countermeasures:** Secure random session IDs (≥128 bits of entropy), regeneration upon login, idle timeouts, Secure/HttpOnly cookie flags.

## 2.2 Transport Layer Security (TLS) — The Cryptographic Backbone

TLS is the **sine qua non** of web security. Its operational model uses **asymmetric cryptography for key exchange** and **symmetric cryptography for bulk data encryption**.

### 2.2.1 The TLS 1.2 Handshake (Simplified for KTU)

1. **ClientHello** — Client sends supported cipher suites, random nonce, and TLS version.
2. **ServerHello** — Server selects cipher suite, sends its random nonce.
3. **Server Certificate** — Server transmits X.509 certificate chain.
4. **Server Key Exchange** — (If using ephemeral DH) Server sends DH parameters signed by its private key.
5. **ServerHelloDone** — Server indicates end of handshake messages.
6. **Client Key Exchange** — Client sends premaster secret encrypted with server's public RSA key OR DH public value.
7. **Change Cipher Spec** — Both parties switch to negotiated symmetric cipher.
8. **Finished** — Encrypted hash of entire handshake verifies integrity.

Both sides derive session keys via a **Key Derivation Function (KDF)**:

$$\text{Master Secret} = \text{PRF}(\text{premaster secret}, \text{client random}, \text{server random})$$

### 2.2.2 TLS 1.3 Improvements (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 |
|---|---|---|
| Handshake RTT | 2-RTT | **1-RTT** (0-RTT optional) |
| Cipher Suites | Many weak ones allowed | Only AEAD suites (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) |
| Forward Secrecy | Optional | **Mandatory** (via (EC)DHE) |
| Legacy crypto | RSA key transport, CBC, MD5, SHA-1 | All removed |
| Encrypted Extensions | No | **Yes** — ServerCert + extensions encrypted |

## 2.3 HTTPS — HTTP Over TLS

**HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure)** is HTTP layered over TLS, operating by default on **port 443** (vs. HTTP's port 80). The URL scheme `https://` instructs the browser to:

1. Resolve the host via DNS (often validated via **DNSSEC** in modern setups).
2. Establish a TCP connection (port 443).
3. Perform the TLS handshake.
4. Transmit HTTP requests inside the encrypted tunnel.
5. Verify server identity against the browser's trusted CA store.

## 2.4 Same-Origin Policy (SOP)

A foundational browser security model: **a script loaded from one origin may only access resources from the same origin**. An *origin* is the triple `(scheme, host, port)`.

- `https://example.com:443/pageA` and `https://example.com:443/pageB` → **Same Origin**.
- `http://example.com` and `https://example.com` → **Different Origin** (scheme mismatch).

Violations are explicitly permitted via **Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)**, where servers send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers to whitelist trusted origins.

## 2.5 Content Security Policy (CSP)

A declarative allow-list mechanism delivered via HTTP response header (`Content-Security-Policy`) or `<meta>` tag. It instructs the browser which sources of scripts, styles, images, and connections are permissible.

**Example header:**

> `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://cdn.trusted.com; object-src 'none'`

CSP effectively neutralizes many XSS vectors by preventing inline scripts and unauthorized script sources from executing.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all high-yield formulas and metrics for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Master Secret Derivation | $\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{premaster}, \text{client\_random}, \text{server\_random})$ | Generates symmetric session keys in TLS |
| 2 | Key Entropy Requirement | $H \geq 128\ \text{bits}$ for session IDs | Prevents brute-force session guessing |
| 3 | RSA Key Exchange | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | Client encrypts premaster with server's public key |
| 4 | RSA Decryption | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ | Server recovers premaster using private key |
| 5 | DH Shared Secret | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Both peers derive same key without transmitting it |
| 6 | AES Block Size | $128\ \text{bits}$ | Standard block cipher for TLS bulk encryption |
| 7 | HMAC Integrity | $\text{HMAC} = H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | Authenticates TLS records |
| 8 | Digital Signature | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{\text{priv}}(H(M))$ | Server authenticates certificate chain |
| 9 | Hash Length (SHA-256) | $256\ \text{bits}$ | Modern TLS minimum digest size |
| 10 | TLS 1.3 Handshake Latency | $1\ \text{RTT}$ | Compared to TLS 1.2's 2-RTT |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, these considerations manifest in concrete deployments:

- **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Full TLS 1.3 enforcement, HSTS headers, PCI-DSS compliant tokenization.
- **Banking (UPI, NEFT web portals):** Mutual TLS (mTLS) for API-to-API communication, certificate pinning in mobile apps.
- **Healthcare (Aarogya Setu):** CSP, SameSite cookies, certificate transparency monitoring.
- **Government (DigiLocker):** DNSSEC, OCSP stapling, hardware security module (HSM) backed private keys.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 RSA Public-Key Encryption (Used in TLS RSA Key Exchange)

> **Goal:** Show how the client encrypts the 48-byte premaster secret using the server's RSA public key, and the server recovers it.

**Step 1 — Key Generation**

The server generates two large primes $p$ and $q$ (each ≥ 1024 bits in classical RSA; ≥ 2048 bits post-2024).

$$n = p \times q$$

**Step 2 — Compute Euler's Totient**

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

This value counts integers in $[1, n]$ that are coprime to $n$.

**Step 3 — Choose Public Exponent**

Select $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. The de facto standard is $e = 65537 = 2^{16} + 1$.

**Step 4 — Compute Private Exponent**

Find $d$ satisfying:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

This is solved using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.

**Step 5 — Client Encryption**

The client converts the premaster secret $\text{PS}$ into an integer $M$ where $0 \leq M < n$, then computes:

$$C = M^{e} \bmod n$$

**Step 6 — Server Decryption**

The server uses its private key:

$$M = C^{d} \bmod n$$

**Step 7 — Recovery of Premaster Secret**

The server interprets $M$ as the 48-byte premaster secret and proceeds to compute the master secret using the PRF (typically HMAC-SHA-256 in TLS 1.2/1.3).

**Why this works (textual explanation):** By Euler's theorem, for any $M$ coprime to $n$:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

Therefore:

$$C^{d} = (M^{e})^{d} = M^{ed} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1 \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

### 3.1.2 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (Foundation of TLS 1.3 Forward Secrecy)

> **Goal:** Demonstrate how two parties derive a shared secret without ever transmitting it.

**Step 1 — Public Parameters**

Agree on a large prime $p$ and generator $g$ (where $g$ generates a subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$).

**Step 2 — Client Private Value**

Client picks random $a$ where $1 \leq a \leq p - 2$ and computes:

$$A = g^{a} \bmod p$$

**Step 3 — Server Private Value**

Server picks random $b$ where $1 \leq b \leq p - 2$ and computes:

$$B = g^{b} \bmod p$$

**Step 4 — Exchange Public Values**

Client sends $A$ to server; server sends $B$ to client. Both values traverse the wire (visible to attackers).

**Step 5 — Shared Secret Computation**

Client computes:

$$K_{\text{client}} = B^{a} \bmod p = (g^{b})^{a} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$$

Server computes:

$$K_{\text{server}} = A^{b} \bmod p = (g^{a})^{b} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$$

Therefore:

$$K_{\text{client}} = K_{\text{server}} = K = g^{ab} \bmod p$$

**Step 6 — Computational Diffie-Hellman (CDH) Hardness**

An eavesdropper observing $g$, $p$, $A$, $B$ must solve the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** to recover $a$ or $b$, which is computationally infeasible for properly chosen parameters (≥ 2048-bit $p$).

**Step 7 — Forward Secrecy Property**

Because $a$ and $b$ are ephemeral and discarded after each session, compromising long-term keys does **not** retroactively decrypt past sessions. This is the cornerstone of TLS 1.3's mandatory forward secrecy.

### 3.1.3 HMAC Construction (Used in TLS Record Authentication)

> **Goal:** Show how TLS guarantees message integrity of every record.

**Step 1 — Key Padding**

Pad $K$ to block length $B$ (64 bytes for SHA-1/SHA-256) to obtain $K^{+}$.

**Step 2 — Inner Pad**

$$\text{ipad} = 0x36 \text{ repeated } B \text{ times}$$

Compute:

$$\text{inner} = K^{+} \oplus \text{ipad}$$

**Step 3 — Hash Inner Concatenation**

$$H_{1} = H((\text{inner}) \parallel M)$$

**Step 4 — Outer Pad**

$$\text{opad} = 0x5C \text{ repeated } B \text{ times}$$

Compute:

$$\text{outer} = K^{+} \oplus \text{opad}$$

**Step 5 — Final HMAC**

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((\text{outer}) \parallel H_{1})$$

**Unified equation:**

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\left((K^{+} \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H\left((K^{+} \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M\right)\right)$$

### 3.1.4 Digital Signature Verification (Certificate Chain Authentication)

> **Goal:** Validate that the server's certificate was issued by a trusted CA.

**Step 1 — Receive Certificate**

Browser obtains server's X.509 certificate $C_{\text{server}}$.

**Step 2 — Hash the Certificate**

$$h = \text{SHA-256}(C_{\text{server}})$$

**Step 3 — Verify CA Signature**

Using the CA's public key (from CA's certificate in trust store):

$$\text{Valid} \iff \text{Verify}_{\text{CA\_pub}}(\sigma, h) = \text{true}$$

Internally, verification checks:

$$h' = \sigma^{e_{\text{CA}}} \bmod n_{\text{CA}}$$

If $h' = h$, the signature is valid.

**Step 4 — Chain Validation**

Repeat up to the **root CA** in the trust store. Browsers ship with ~150 root CA certificates pre-installed (Mozilla, Microsoft, Apple, and Linux trust stores).

**Step 5 — Revocation Check**

Consult **CRL (Certificate Revocation List)** or **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)** responder to ensure the certificate has not been revoked.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. Control Mapping

| Threat | Confidentiality | Integrity | Authentication | Replay Protection | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | TLS encryption | — | — | — | High |
| MITM | TLS encryption | HMAC | Certificates | Nonces | High |
| SQL Injection | — | Parameterized queries | — | — | High |
| XSS | — | Output encoding | CSP | — | High |
| CSRF | — | Tokens | SameSite cookies | Nonces | Medium |
| Session Hijacking | TLS | Signed cookies | Session binding | Rotation | Medium |
| DNS Spoofing | DNSSEC | DNSSEC chains | Chain of trust | Short TTLs | Medium |
| DDoS | Rate limiting | — | CAPTCHA | — | Low (Module 3) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — RSA Modulus Computation

Let $p = 61$, $q = 53$ (small primes for illustration).

**Step 1:** Compute modulus:

$$n = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$

**Step 2:** Compute totient:

$$\phi(n) = (61 - 1)(53 - 1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$

**Step 3:** Choose $e = 17$ (verify $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ ✓).

**Step 4:** Compute $d$ such that $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, $d = 2753$ (verify: $17 \times 2753 = 46801 = 15 \times 3120 + 1$ ✓).

**Step 5:** Encrypt plaintext $M = 65$:

$$C = 65^{17} \bmod 3233 = 2790$$

**Step 6:** Decrypt:

$$M = 2790^{2753} \bmod 3233 = 65\ \checkmark$$

This illustrates the same mathematical framework TLS 1.2 used for premaster secret transport — although, again, TLS 1.3 has moved to **mandatory (EC)DHE** for forward secrecy.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — TLS 1.2 Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant clientA as Client Browser
    participant serverB as Web Server
    participant caStoreC as CA Trust Store

    clientA->>serverB: ClientHello (cipher suites, nonce, TLS version)
    serverB->>clientA: ServerHello (selected cipher, nonce)
    serverB->>clientA: Server Certificate (X.509 chain)
    serverB->>clientA: ServerHelloDone
    clientA->>caStoreC: Validate Certificate Chain
    caStoreC-->>clientA: Trusted or Untrusted
    clientA->>serverB: ClientKeyExchange (encrypted premaster)
    clientA->>serverB: ChangeCipherSpec
    clientA->>serverB: Finished (encrypted verify)
    serverB->>clientA: ChangeCipherSpec
    serverB->>clientA: Finished (encrypted verify)
    Note over clientA,serverB: Application Data (HTTP) flows over encrypted channel
```
