A default-allow posture leaves the system open to zero-day exploits and misconfigurations. **The "How"**: - The administrator defines a **rule base** evaluated top-to-bottom. - The first matching rule decides the action. - If no rule matches, the implicit final rule is **DENY ALL** (often called the "cleanup rule" or "catch-all"). **Boolean Logic Formulation**: For a given packet $p$ with attributes (source IP, dest IP, source port, dest port, protocol), the decision function $D(p)$ is: $$ D(p) = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } \exists \, r_i \in \text{Ruleset} : \text{match}(p, r_i) \land r_i.\text{action} = \text{ALLOW} \\ \text{DROP} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### Characteristic 3: Imperviousness to Bypass (Self-Protection) **Definition**: The firewall itself must be **hardened** against direct attack. It should be running a stripped-down, secure operating system with no superfluous services. **The "Why"**: An attacker who compromises the firewall effectively owns the entire network it protects. The firewall becomes the single point of total compromise. **The "How"**: - Run a **locked-down OS** (e.g., OpenBSD/pf, Cisco IOS with no IP services enabled). - **Disable all unnecessary services** (no Telnet, no HTTP management, no DNS server). - Apply **flood guards** to prevent resource exhaustion. - Maintain a **dedicated management interface** on a separate VLAN. ### Characteristic 4: Minimal Performance Impact (Efficiency) **Definition**: The firewall should introduce **minimal latency and throughput degradation** for legitimate traffic. **The "Why"**: A firewall that adds 500 ms of latency to every packet is operationally useless for VoIP, video conferencing, and real-time trading. **The "How"**: - Hardware-accelerated packet processing (ASICs, NPUs). - **Stateful lookup tables** (hash-based) for connection tracking. - **Connection table capacity**: A typical enterprise firewall handles $10^6$ concurrent connections. **Throughput Metric**: The maximum sustainable throughput $R$ of a firewall is governed by: $$ R = \frac{N_{pkts} \cdot S_{avg}}{t_{proc}} $$ Where: - $N_{pkts}$ = number of packets processed - $S_{avg}$ = average packet size (bytes) - $t_{proc}$ = total processing time (seconds) ### Characteristic 5: Defense in Depth Compatibility (Layered Security) **Definition**: A firewall must be able to integrate with other security controls (IDS/IPS, anti-virus, VPN, NAC) to form a **defense-in-depth** architecture. **The "Why"**: No single control is sufficient. Firewalls are necessary but not sufficient. **The "How"**: - Layered deployment: **Perimeter firewall → Internal segmentation firewall → Host-based firewall**. - Integration with SIEM for centralized logging. - Coordination with IDS for **inline IPS mode**. ### Characteristic 6: Transparency to Legitimate Users **Definition**: Authorized users should be able to access permitted services **without noticing the firewall's presence**. No special configuration should be required on end-user devices. **The "Why"**: A firewall that requires every user to install a client or manually configure proxies will be bypassed by users, defeating its purpose. **The "How"**: - **Transparent proxying** for HTTP/HTTPS. - **NAT-based operation** that hides internal addressing. - **DHCP/BOOTP passthrough** for automatic addressing. ### Characteristic 7: Comprehensive Logging Capability **Definition**: The firewall must log all security-relevant events: denied connections, accepted connections (auditing), configuration changes, system events, and potential attacks. **The "Why"**: Forensics, compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR), and incident response all depend on detailed logs. **The "How"**: - **Local logging** to a circular buffer. - **Remote logging** to a SIEM via syslog (UDP/514 or TCP/514). - **Timestamping** with NTP-synchronized clocks. - **Log format**: Common Event Format (CEF) or syslog RFC 5424. ### Characteristic 8: Fail-Safe Stance **Definition**: In the event of a hardware failure, software crash, or power loss, the firewall must **fail closed** (block all traffic) rather than fail open (allow all). **The "Why"**: Fail-open creates a temporary security gap that attackers actively probe for. **The "How"**: - Redundant power supplies. - **Active-passive or active-active high-availability (HA) pairs**. - **Bypass switches** for emergency maintenance that fail closed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed — KTU Exam Favorite Question**: > - A **fail-closed** firewall drops all traffic when it crashes — **security is preserved**. > - A **fail-open** firewall allows all traffic when it crashes — **availability is preserved at the cost of security**. > - In KTU 2024 module expectations, you must justify: *"Most enterprise firewalls are configured fail-closed by default for security-critical deployments."* ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | |---|---------|----------------|-------------------| | 1 | Choke Point Identity | $T_{f} = T$ | $T$ = Total boundary packets, $T_{f}$ = Firewall-processed packets | | 2 | Default-Deny Decision | $D(p) = \text{ACCEPT} \iff \exists \, r_i \in R : \text{match}(p, r_i)$ | $p$ = packet, $R$ = ruleset | | 3 | Throughput | $R = \dfrac{N_{pkts} \cdot S_{avg}}{t_{proc}}$ | $R$ in **bps**, $S_{avg}$ in **bytes**, $t_{proc}$ in **seconds** | | 4 | Latency | $L = t_{out} - t_{in}$ | $L$ in **ms**, $t$ in **ms** | | 5 | Concurrent Connections | $C_{max} = \dfrac{M_{conn} \cdot T_{idle}}{T_{avg}}$ | $M$ = memory, $T$ = timeout | | 6 | Packet Loss Probability | $P_{loss} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot S}$ (M/M/1 model) | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $S$ = service time | | 7 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $A$ dimensionless, ratio | | 8 | Rule Match Order | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ linear scan for $n$ rules | Time complexity | | 9 | ACL Bitmask Match | $\text{match} = (\text{IP} \oplus \text{prefix}) \land \text{mask} = 0$ | Bitwise operation | | 10 | Stateful Table Lookup | $\text{hash} = H(\text{5-tuple}) \mod N_{buckets}$ | $H$ = hash function, $N$ = bucket count | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip**: The decision function $D(p)$ and the choke-point identity are the two highest-yield items for a 3-mark short answer. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Firewall Characteristics | |--------|------------------------------------------| | **Banking & Finance** | Choke-point + default-deny + fail-closed protects SWIFT gateways and trading systems. | | **Healthcare (HIPAA)** | Comprehensive logging + access control for PHI (Protected Health Information) compliance. | | **E-Commerce** | Defense-in-depth + WAF integration protects against SQLi, XSS, and DDoS. | | **Industrial Control (SCADA/ICS)** | Self-protection + segmentation isolates OT networks from IT networks. | | **Government / Defense** | Multi-layer firewalls with cross-domain guards enforcing classification boundaries. | | **Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP)** | Security Groups + NACLs + WAF implementing the same eight characteristics. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Choke-Point Bypass Probability A common KTU question asks: *"Why is the choke-point characteristic critical? What happens if it is violated?"* We will derive the **effective attack surface** with and without a proper choke point. **Step 1**: Define the network model. Let there be $N$ physical/logical entry/exit points between the trusted network $\mathcal{T}$ and the untrusted network $\mathcal{U}$ in a misconfigured scenario. **Step 2**: Define the security exposure $E$. For each path $p_i$, there exists a probability $q_i$ that an attacker finds and exploits a vulnerability on that path. **Step 3**: The total exposure (probability of at least one compromise) assuming independent paths follows the **union bound** for small $q_i$: $$ E_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - q_i) $$ **Step 4**: For a properly architected choke point, $N = 1$ (only the firewall path), so: $$ E_{choke} = 1 - (1 - q_{fw}) = q_{fw} $$ **Step 5**: For $N$ independent bypass paths with uniform vulnerability probability $q$: $$ E_{multi} = 1 - (1 - q)^{N} $$ **Step 6**: Taking the ratio to quantify the degradation: $$ \frac{E_{multi}}{E_{choke}} = \frac{1 - (1 - q)^{N}}{q} $$ **Step 7**: For $N = 5$ paths and $q = 0.05$ (5% chance of compromise per path): $$ \begin{aligned} E_{multi} &= 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{5} \\ &= 1 - (0.95)^{5} \\ &= 1 - 0.7738 \\ &= 0.2262 \quad \text{(22.62% exposure)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8**: Compared to the choke-point case $E_{choke} = 0.05$ (5%), the exposure has **increased 4.52×**. This numerically demonstrates why the choke-point characteristic is non-negotiable. $$ \boxed{\text{Risk Multiplier} = \frac{1 - (1-q)^{N}}{q} = \frac{0.2262}{0.05} \approx 4.52} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Rule-Match Search Complexity A KTU 14-mark question often asks you to compare the efficiency of firewall rule processing. Let us derive the time complexity. **Step 1**: A ruleset $R = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n\}$ is ordered, typically by **administrative distance** or **specificity**. **Step 2**: For an incoming packet $p$, the firewall scans sequentially: $$ \forall \, r_i \in R, \quad i = 1 \to n $$ **Step 3**: The first matching rule's action is executed: $$ D(p) = \text{action}(r_{i^*}), \quad \text{where } i^* = \min \{ i : \text{match}(p, r_i) \} $$ **Step 4**: In the **worst case** (no match or match at the last rule): $$ T_{worst} = \mathcal{O}(n) $$ **Step 5**: In the **best case** (match at the first rule): $$ T_{best} = \mathcal{O}(1) $$ **Step 6**: The **average case** assuming uniform distribution of matching positions: $$ T_{avg} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{n+1}{2} = \mathcal{O}(n) $$ **Step 7**: To optimize, modern firewalls use **decision-tree-based classifiers** (e.g., HiCuts, HyperCuts) that achieve: $$ T_{optimized} = \mathcal{O}(\log n) \quad \text{to} \quad \mathcal{O}(d \cdot \log n) $$ where $d$ is the number of packet header fields (typically 5 in a classic 5-tuple lookup). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Characteristic vs. Attack Mitigation This matrix is **exam-ready** for 14-mark comparison questions: | Characteristic | Attack Mitigated | Attack NOT Mitigated | Failure Consequence | |----------------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------| | **Choke Point** | Eavesdropping, unauthorized routing | Insider attacks, lateral movement | Total perimeter bypass | | **Default-Deny** | Zero-day exploits, novel worms | Insider abuse of allowed services | Accidental DoS, productivity loss | | **Self-Protection** | Direct firewall compromise | DoS/DDoS resource exhaustion | Total network compromise | | **Performance** | Latency-based evasion | — | Operational degradation | | **Defense in Depth** | Single-point failures | Sophisticated APTs | Layered attack success | | **Transparency** | User circumvention | Malware using allowed ports | Bypass by end users | | **Logging** | Lack of forensic evidence | Privacy violation, log injection | Compliance failure | | **Fail-Safe** | Hardware failure exploitation | — | Network outage | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Firewall Throughput Calculation A stateful firewall must process **$1.2 \times 10^6$ packets per second** with an **average packet size of 800 bytes**. The packet processing time is **$0.6 \, \mu\text{s}$** per packet. Determine the maximum throughput and verify it meets the design goal of **8 Gbps**. **Step 1**: Calculate the maximum number of packets processable per second: $$ P_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{proc}} = \frac{1}{0.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.667 \times 10^{6} \text{ packets/sec} $$ **Step 2**: Compare with the offered load $P_{load} = 1.2 \times 10^{6}$ pps: $$ P_{load} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} < 1.667 \times 10^{6} = P_{max} $$ Since $P_{load} < P_{max}$, the firewall can handle the load without dropping packets. **Step 3**: Calculate the throughput in bits per second: $$ \begin{aligned} R &= P_{load} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8 \\ &= 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 800 \times 8 \\ &= 7.68 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps} \\ &= 7.68 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4**: Compare with the design goal of 8 Gbps: $$ 7.68 \text{ Gbps} < 8 \text{ Gbps} \Rightarrow \text{FAIL by } 0.32 \text{ Gbps} $$ **Step 5**: The firewall is **under-provisioned**. To meet 8 Gbps, either: - Upgrade to a faster processor (reduce $t_{proc}$). - Use hardware offload. - Increase average packet size (jumbo frames). **Required reduction in $t_{proc}$**: $$ t_{proc}^{required} = \frac{800 \times 8}{8 \times 10^{9}} = 8 \times 10^{-7} = 0.8 \, \mu\text{s/packet} \cdot \frac{1.2 \times 10^{6}}{10^{9}} \Rightarrow t_{proc}^{new} = 0.6 \times \frac{7.68}{8} = 0.576 \, \mu\text{s} $$ This is a 4% improvement needed, achievable with a hardware upgrade. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU**: When two flows collide (e.g., bursty traffic), use the **M/M/1 queueing model** to compute queuing delay and packet loss. The utilization $\rho = \lambda \cdot S$ must satisfy $\rho < 1$ for stability. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Firewall Placement and Choke Point Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the choke-point principle and the eight characteristics at a glance: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[External Untrusted Network
Internet] --> B[Firewall Device
Choke Point] B --> C[Internal Trusted Network
Corporate LAN] B --> D[DMZ Zone
Public Servers] B --> E[VPN Gateway
Remote Access] B --> F[SIEM Server
Centralized Logging] F -.->|Syslog UDP 514| B C -->|Default Gateway| B D -->|Reverse Proxy| B E -->|Tunnel Termination| B style A fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000 style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Firewall Characteristics ```mermaid graph TD node1[Packet Arrival] --> node2{Is this packet authorized
by security policy} node2 -- Yes ALLOW --> node3[Forward to destination
Log ACCEPT event] node2 -- No DENY --> node4[Drop or Reject packet
Log DENY event] node2 -- No Match --> node5[Apply default-deny rule
Log IMPLICIT DENY] node3 --> node6[Update state table
for stateful inspection] node4 --> node7[Update threat statistics
Trigger IDS alert] node5 --> node7 node6 --> node8[Connection tracked in
state table] node8 --> node9[Return packet match
to existing state] node7 --> node10[Log sent to SIEM
via syslog] node9 --> node10 style node1 fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366 style node2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 style node3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style node4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style node5 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000 style node6 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B style node7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style node8 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style node9 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 style node10 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00 ``` ## 4.3 The Eight Characteristics — Conceptual Map ```mermaid mindmap root((Firewall
Characteristics)) C1 Traffic Containment Choke Point Single entry exit C2 Authorized Only Default deny Whitelist policy C3 Self Protection Hardened OS No extra services C4 Performance Low latency High throughput C5 Defense in Depth Layered security IDS IPS integration C6 Transparency No client config NAT hiding C7 Logging Syslog SIEM Compliance audit C8 Fail Safe Fail closed HA redundancy ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flow for Incoming Packet ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Packet In] --> nodeB[Check 5 Tuple Hash] nodeB --> nodeC{Match in State Table} nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Return ACK from Established State] nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Apply Rule Base Scan] nodeE --> nodeF{First Matching Rule} nodeF -- ALLOW --> nodeG[Create State Entry
Forward Packet] nodeF -- DENY --> nodeH[Drop Packet
Log Violation] nodeF -- REJECT --> nodeI[Send ICMP Unreachable
Log Violation] nodeF -- No Match --> nodeJ[Apply Implicit DENY ALL
Log as Anomaly] nodeG --> nodeK[Update Counters] nodeH --> nodeK nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeK nodeD --> nodeK style nodeA fill:#E6F3FF style nodeB fill:#FFFACD style nodeC fill:#FFE4B5 style nodeD fill:#90EE90 style nodeE fill:#FFE4B5 style nodeF fill:#FFA07A style nodeG fill:#98FB98 style nodeH fill:#FFB6C1 style nodeI fill:#FFB6C1 style nodeJ fill:#FF6347 style nodeK fill:#E0FFFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation**: The above flow represents the **stateful inspection** decision process which directly embodies Characteristics C2 (default-deny) and C7 (logging). The state table lookup embodies C4 (performance optimization through hashing). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. Define a firewall. List any FOUR characteristics of a firewall.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: **Definition (1 mark)**: A firewall is a hardware/software system positioned at the network boundary that enforces a security policy by controlling the flow of traffic between two networks based on predefined rules. **Four Characteristics (0.5 marks each × 4 = 2 marks)**: 1. **Traffic Containment**: All traffic must pass through the firewall (choke point). 2. **Authorized Traffic Only**: Only explicitly permitted traffic is allowed; everything else is denied. 3. **Self-Protection**: The firewall itself is hardened against direct attack. 4. **Comprehensive Logging**: All security-relevant events are recorded for audit and forensics. > [!TIP] > Any four from the eight characteristics earn full marks. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between fail-open and fail-closed firewall stances. Which is preferred for security-critical deployments?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: | Aspect | Fail-Open | Fail-Closed | |--------|-----------|-------------| | **On Failure** | Allows all traffic | Blocks all traffic | | **Priority** | Availability | Security | | **Risk** | Temporary security gap | Temporary DoS | | **Used In** | Safety systems, medical devices | Banking, defense, government | **Preferred Stance (1 mark)**: For security-critical deployments (e.g., banking, military, classified networks), **fail-closed** is preferred because it preserves confidentiality and integrity at the cost of temporary unavailability. The principle of "secure by default" overrides the "always-on" availability. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **choke point principle** of a firewall. Why is it considered the most fundamental characteristic? Derive the **risk multiplier** formula to show how bypassing the choke point multiplies the attack surface. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **default-deny policy** with a suitable example. Show the **decision function** $D(p)$ for a sample ruleset and explain how **stateful inspection** enhances security over simple packet filtering. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) — Choke Point Principle (7 Marks) **Definition (1 mark)**: The choke point principle states that a firewall must be the **only** path for traffic to cross between the trusted and untrusted networks. There must be no alternate route — physical or logical — that bypasses the firewall. **Why It Is the Most Fundamental Characteristic (2 marks)**: - It is a **prerequisite** for all other characteristics. If traffic can bypass the firewall, the rule base, logging, and policy are all rendered ineffective. - It embodies the **"defense at the perimeter"** philosophy. A single, controlled, auditable boundary is far more secure than multiple distributed enforcement points. - It enables **centralized security management**. **Derivation of the Risk Multiplier (4 marks)**: Let $N$ be the number of independent paths between networks, each with vulnerability probability $q$. **Step 1**: The probability that **none** of the $N$ paths are exploited is: $$ P_{safe} = (1 - q)^{N} $$ **Step 2**: The probability that **at least one** path is compromised is: $$ P_{breach} = 1 - (1 - q)^{N} $$ **Step 3**: The choke point enforces $N = 1$: $$ P_{breach}^{choke} = 1 - (1 - q)^{1} = q $$ **Step 4**: Define the **Risk Multiplier** $\mathcal{R}$ as the ratio of breach probabilities: $$ \mathcal{R} = \frac{1 - (1 - q)^{N}}{q} $$ **Step 5**: Numerical example — for $N = 4$ bypass paths and $q = 0.10$: $$ \mathcal{R} = \frac{1 - (0.9)^{4}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.6561}{0.10} = \frac{0.3439}{0.10} = 3.439 $$ This means the attack surface has increased **3.44 times** by allowing four bypass paths. The choke point eliminates this multiplication. **Conclusion (1 mark)**: The choke point principle is mathematically and operationally the foundation of firewall security. Violating it creates multiplicative risk that grows exponentially with the number of paths. --- #### Part (b) — Default-Deny Policy & Stateful Inspection (7 Marks) **Default-Deny Definition (1 mark)**: The default-deny (also called default-deny-all) policy is a security stance where any packet not explicitly matched by an **ALLOW** rule in the rule base is automatically **denied** (dropped or rejected). This is the **"deny by default, allow by exception"** approach. **Example Ruleset (2 marks)**: | Rule # | Source | Destination | Protocol | Port | Action | |--------|--------|-------------|----------|------|--------| | R1 | 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.1.10 | TCP | 443 | ALLOW | | R2 | 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.1.20 | TCP | 80 | ALLOW | | R3 | 10.0.0.0/8 | any | ICMP | — | ALLOW | | R4 | 192.168.1.0/24 | any | TCP | 23 | DENY (Telnet block) | | R5 | **any** | **any** | **any** | **any** | **DENY** (cleanup) | **Decision Function** (1 mark): $$ D(p) = \text{action}(r_{i^*}), \quad \text{where } i^* = \min \{ i \in [1,5] : \text{match}(p, r_i) \} $$ A packet from `10.0.0.5` to `8.8.8.8` on TCP/443 matches **R1** and is **ALLOWED**. A packet from `10.0.0.5` to `8.8.8.8` on TCP/23 (Telnet) matches **R5** and is **DENIED**. **Stateful Inspection Enhancement (3 marks)**: | Aspect | Stateless Packet Filter | Stateful Inspection | |--------|--------------------------|---------------------| | **Inspection Depth** | Individual packet only | Packet + connection state | | **Tracking** | None | Connection table (5-tuple) | | **Return Traffic** | Must be explicitly allowed | Automatically allowed for established connections | | **Attack Mitigation** | Limited (SYN floods, ACK scans) | Stronger (out-of-state packets dropped) | | **Performance** | Higher throughput | Slightly lower (state table lookup) | **Stateful Example**: A user initiates an HTTP connection to `www.example.com`. The firewall: 1. Sees the **SYN** packet → creates a state entry `SYN_SENT`. 2. Sees the **SYN-ACK** reply → transitions state to `ESTABLISHED`. 3. Sees the **ACK** from the user → confirms `ESTABLISHED`. 4. All subsequent packets in this connection are matched against the state table and forwarded **without re-scanning the rule base**, dramatically improving performance. **Conclusion (1 mark)**: Default-deny provides the secure baseline, and stateful inspection adds both **security** (rejecting out-of-state packets, mitigating spoofing) and **performance** (connection state caching) on top of it. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** List and explain the **eight characteristics of a firewall**. For each characteristic, give one **real-world example** of how it is implemented in an enterprise environment. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A stateless packet filter firewall is deployed at an enterprise boundary. Compute the **maximum throughput** in **Gbps** given: $N_{pkts} = 5 \times 10^{5}$ packets/sec, $S_{avg} = 1200$ bytes, $t_{proc} = 1.2 \, \mu\text{s}$. Is this firewall suitable for a **10 Gbps** link? Justify your answer and suggest **two improvements**. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — Eight Characteristics with Examples (7 Marks) For each characteristic, provide the definition (0.5 marks) and a real-world example (0.5 marks). Here is a compact tabulated answer: | # | Characteristic | Definition | Real-World Example | |---|----------------|------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Traffic Containment** | All traffic passes through firewall. | Cisco ASA deployed at the only edge router; all VLANs route through it. | | 2 | **Authorized Traffic Only** | Default-deny, explicit allow. | E-commerce site only opens TCP 443 (HTTPS) on the Web server. | | 3 | **Self-Protection** | Hardened OS, no extra services. | Palo Alto PAN-OS running on hardened Linux with SSH only on mgmt port. | | 4 | **Performance** | Minimal latency, high throughput. | Fortinet FortiGate 6000F with NP7 ASICs delivering 1 Tbps throughput. | | 5 | **Defense in Depth** | Layers with IDS/IPS. | DMZ has WAF + IPS; internal has Cisco Firepower NGIPS for east-west traffic. | | 6 | **Transparency** | No client configuration. | Transparent bridge-mode firewall inserted in-line without IP renumbering. | | 7 | **Logging** | Comprehensive event records. | All deny events forwarded to Splunk SIEM for SOC 24/7 monitoring. | | 8 | **Fail-Safe** | Fails closed on crash. | Active-passive HA pair with stateful failover; primary failure → secondary takes over without dropping established connections. | **Conclusion (0.5 marks)**: All eight characteristics are interrelated; an enterprise-grade firewall must satisfy all of them to be considered robust. --- #### Part (b) — Throughput Calculation (7 Marks) **Given**: - $N_{pkts} = 5 \times 10^{5}$ packets/sec - $S_{avg} = 1200$ bytes - $t_{proc} = 1.2 \, \mu\text{s} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ s **Step 1: Compute the maximum packet processing rate** (1 mark): $$ P_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{proc}} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.33 \times 10^{5} \text{ packets/sec} $$ **Step 2: Compare with the offered load** (1 mark): $$ P_{load} = 5 \times 10^{5} < 8.33 \times 10^{5} = P_{max} \quad \checkmark $$ The firewall can keep up with the offered load. **Step 3: Compute the actual throughput** (2 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} R &= N_{pkts} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8 \\ &= 5 \times 10^{5} \times 1200 \times 8 \\ &= 4.8 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps} \\ &= 4.8 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compare with the 10 Gbps link requirement** (1 mark): $$ 4.8 \text{ Gbps} < 10 \text{ Gbps} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Not Suitable} $$ **Shortfall**: $$ \Delta R = 10 - 4.8 = 5.2 \text{ Gbps (52\% short)} $$ **Step 5: Two Improvements** (2 marks): 1. **Upgrade the processing engine** (e.g., move from CPU-based iptables to an ASIC/NPU-accelerated platform like Intel QuickAssist or Cavium OCTEON). This can reduce $t_{proc}$ by 3–5×, raising throughput to 14–24 Gbps. 2. **Use flow offload / stateful fast-path**: Cache the rule decision for the first packet of a flow and apply it to subsequent packets in the same flow without re-evaluation. This effectively raises $P_{max}$ to match the bus speed (40/100 Gbps on modern platforms). **Final Justification (1 mark)**: The current firewall is **not suitable** for a 10 Gbps link because it can only sustain 4.8 Gbps, which is 52% below the requirement. A hardware upgrade and software optimization are necessary to meet the SLA. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams on Firewall Characteristics**: > > 1. **Confusing "firewall" with "anti-virus"**: A firewall is a *policy enforcement* device, not a malware scanner. Many students incorrectly list "virus detection" as a firewall characteristic. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for this confusion. > > 2. **Forgetting the "fail-safe" characteristic**: This is a high-yield, easy-to-forget item. Examiners expect you to mention it explicitly and distinguish **fail-open** vs **fail-closed** in 14-mark questions. > > 3. **Conflating "default-deny" with "default-allow"**: Always state the secure default explicitly. Saying "the firewall blocks malicious traffic" is vague — say "**default-deny, allow-by-exception**". > > 4. **Skipping units in numerical questions**: In throughput/latency problems, **always** include units (bps, Gbps, ms, pps). KTU valuation keys deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units. > > 5. **Writing paragraphs instead of tables/comparisons**: For 7-mark sub-parts, structured answers (tables, bullet points) earn higher marks than running prose. Use headings and sub-points. > > 6. **Not justifying fail-closed choice**: When asked "which is preferred?", do not just say "fail-closed". **Justify** by mentioning security-critical context, default-deny philosophy, and the trade-off with availability. > > 7. **Confusing the choke-point identity**: The choke point is about **network topology**, not about packet filtering speed. Many students mis-describe it as a performance feature. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - **Definition**: A firewall is a policy-enforcement barrier at a network boundary. - **Eight Characteristics** (MUST memorize): (1) Traffic Containment / Choke Point, (2) Authorized Traffic Only / Default-Deny, (3) Self-Protection, (4) Performance, (5) Defense in Depth, (6) Transparency, (7) Logging, (8) Fail-Safe. - **Choke Point Identity**: $T_{f} = T$ (mathematical formulation). - **Decision Function**: $D(p) = \text{action}(r_{i^*})$ where $i^* = \min\{i : \text{match}(p, r_i)\}$. - **Risk Multiplier**: $\mathcal{R} = \frac{1 - (1 - q)^{N}}{q}$ — grows with $N$. - **Rule Match Complexity**: $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for linear scan, $\mathcal{O}(d \log n)$ for decision-tree classifiers. - **Throughput Formula**: $R = N_{pkts} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8$ (in bps). - **Fail-Closed vs Fail-Open**: Fail-closed = security priority; Fail-open = availability priority. - **Stateful vs Stateless**: Stateful tracks connection state in a hash table; stateless inspects each packet in isolation. - **5-Tuple**: (Source IP, Dest IP, Source Port, Dest Port, Protocol) — used for state table lookup. - **Default-Deny is the only secure default**; default-allow is rejected in KTU 2024 curriculum. - **Logging is mandatory for compliance** (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR) — no exceptions. - **Defense in Depth**: Firewalls are **necessary but not sufficient** — they must be combined with IDS/IPS, anti-virus, and host controls. - **The firewall is itself a target**: Self-protection is non-negotiable; compromise of the firewall = compromise of the entire protected network. - **Exam weightage**: This topic typically carries **7–14 marks** in KTU 2024 ESE Module 4. - **Common exam forms**: Define + list characteristics (3 marks), Compare fail-open/fail-closed (3 marks), Explain all 8 with examples (7 marks), Throughput calculation (7 marks). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Tip**: Before writing your KTU 2024 answer, always state the **eight characteristics** as a numbered list at the start, then expand each. Examiners reward **structure and completeness** over verbose prose.

topics.title: Types of Firewalls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Firewalls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Firewall

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firewall (RFC 2979 / Stallings Definition):** A firewall is a hardware and/or software device that enforces a security policy by mediating all traffic between a trusted internal network and an untrusted external network (typically the Internet). It operates as a **perimeter defense mechanism**, inspecting packets, sessions, or application payloads to allow or deny traffic based on a predefined rule set.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Outcome mapping typically to **CO4 – Apply security mechanisms in networked systems**), firewalls are positioned as the **first line of defense** in the defense-in-depth strategy. They are categorized based on the **OSI Layer** at which they operate and the **depth of inspection** they perform on traffic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Security Checkpoint

Imagine your internal corporate network as a **high-security office building**, and the Internet as the **public street outside**.

| Real-World Element | Network Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Building entrance gate | Firewall perimeter device |
| ID card / Visitor pass | Packet header fields (Source IP, Destination IP, Port) |
| Metal detector / X-ray | Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) |
| Visitor logbook | Stateful connection table |
| Reception desk that opens the door | Application Proxy / Gateway |
| Smart camera with AI threat detection | Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a security guard can only check IDs (stateless filtering) or track who entered and exited (stateful inspection) or actually escort visitors to the meeting room (proxy), firewalls come in different "intelligence" levels corresponding to the depth of inspection.

---

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

The KTU syllabus explicitly categorizes firewalls based on **three primary dimensions**:

1. **Operational OSI Layer** — Network (L3), Transport (L4), Application (L7)
2. **State Awareness** — Stateless vs. Stateful
3. **Processing Capability** — Simple filter vs. Deep Inspection vs. Application Mediation

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "classify firewalls" in the KTU board exam, always quote the OSI layer first, then stateful/stateless nature, and finally the proxy behavior. Examiners reward this triple-axis answer structure with full 7-mark credit.

---

## 1.4 Why Firewalls Are Essential — The Threat Landscape

A firewall addresses three fundamental security threats:

- **Unauthorized Access** — Prevents external attackers from reaching internal hosts.
- **Data Exfiltration** — Blocks sensitive data from leaving the organization.
- **Malware Ingress** — Filters traffic to stop command-and-control (C2) callbacks.

The industry-standard metric is **throughput measured in Gbps** and **concurrent session capacity** (typically 1M – 100M sessions for enterprise NGFWs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Firewall Types (KTU Board Gold-Standard Classification)

### **Type 1: Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless)**

- Operates at **OSI Layer 3 (Network)** and **Layer 4 (Transport)**.
- Examines each packet **in isolation** — no memory of prior packets.
- Filters based on a static **Access Control List (ACL)** containing tuples:
  $\langle \text{Source IP}, \text{Dest IP}, \text{Source Port}, \text{Dest Port}, \text{Protocol} \rangle$
- **Decision logic:** First-match rule in a top-down ordered ACL.
- **Strength:** Extremely fast, low latency, hardware-implementable.
- **Weakness:** Vulnerable to **IP spoofing**, cannot detect fragmented packet attacks, no application-layer awareness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Stateless firewalls cannot enforce the "SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK" handshake sequence. An attacker can craft a single SYN packet with a spoofed source and the firewall will allow it.

---

### **Type 2: Stateful Inspection Firewall**

- Operates at **OSI Layers 3, 4, and 5 (Session)**.
- Maintains a **State Table** of all active TCP connections and UDP pseudo-connections.
- Tracks TCP flags (SYN, ACK, FIN, RST) and sequence numbers.
- **Decision logic:** A packet is allowed only if it belongs to an established session OR explicitly permitted by ACL.
- **Strength:** Defeats SYN flood attacks, prevents ACK scanning, allows dynamic return traffic.
- **Weakness:** Still cannot inspect application payload (e.g., cannot block a malicious HTTP GET inside an allowed TCP stream).

The state table entry is formally represented as:

$$S_i = \langle \text{IP}_{src}, \text{Port}_{src}, \text{IP}_{dst}, \text{Port}_{dst}, \text{Proto}, \text{State}, \text{Seq}_{src}, \text{Seq}_{dst} \rangle$$

---

### **Type 3: Application-Level Gateway (ALG / Proxy Firewall)**

- Operates at **OSI Layer 7 (Application)**.
- Acts as a **man-in-the-middle** — terminates the client connection and originates a fresh connection to the server.
- Inspects full application protocol semantics (HTTP headers, FTP commands, DNS queries).
- **Strength:** Total protocol compliance enforcement, hides internal IP topology (NAT-like), can enforce content rules (block URLs, strip ActiveX).
- **Weakness:** High latency, requires one proxy per protocol, single point of failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** Application-Level Gateways are also called **"Bastion Hosts"** when hardened and dedicated solely to proxy duties. Remember this synonym — it appears frequently in past papers.

---

### **Type 4: Circuit-Level Gateway**

- Operates at **OSI Layer 5 (Session)**.
- Does **NOT inspect payload** — only verifies that the TCP handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) is legitimate.
- Once the circuit is established, packets flow without further inspection.
- **Strength:** Faster than ALG, hides internal network structure.
- **Weakness:** No content filtering — can carry malicious data inside an "approved" circuit.
- **Example:** SOCKS v5 proxy.

---

### **Type 5: Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)**

- Industry term (Gartner, 2009) for firewalls that combine:
  1. Stateful inspection
  2. Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)
  3. Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)
  4. Application awareness (Layer 7 visibility)
  5. Threat intelligence feeds
  6. SSL/TLS decryption
- Operates across **all OSI layers (2 – 7)**.
- Uses **signature-based + anomaly-based** detection.
- **Strength:** Unified threat management, context-aware policy (user, app, location, time).
- **Weakness:** Expensive, requires skilled operators, can become a bottleneck if under-provisioned.

---

### **Type 6: Web Application Firewall (WAF)**

- Specialized firewall for **HTTP/HTTPS traffic**, operating at Layer 7.
- Protects against **OWASP Top 10** threats: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF.
- Uses **positive security model** (allowlist of expected behavior) or **negative security model** (blocklist of known attacks).
- Deployed as reverse proxy in front of web servers.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Comparison Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following tables are essential for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the comparison columns.**

### Table 2.A: OSI Layer Mapping

| Firewall Type | OSI Layer | Stateful? | Payload Inspection |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packet Filtering | 3, 4 | No | None |
| Stateful Inspection | 3, 4, 5 | Yes | None |
| Circuit-Level Gateway | 5 | Partial | None |
| Application-Level Gateway | 7 | Yes | Full |
| NGFW | 2 – 7 | Yes | Deep |
| WAF | 7 | Yes | HTTP-only |

### Table 2.B: Performance vs. Security Trade-off

| Metric | Packet Filter | Stateful | ALG | NGFW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput | Highest | High | Low | Medium |
| Latency | $\leq 1$ ms | 1–5 ms | 10–50 ms | 5–20 ms |
| Security Depth | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Cost | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | High |

### Table 2.C: Key Decision Rules (LaTeX-safe notation)

| Rule | Mathematical / Logical Form |
|---|---|
| Packet Filter Allow | $\text{Allow if } \text{ACL}_i(\text{pkt}) = \text{MATCH}$ |
| Stateful Allow | $\text{Allow if } \text{pkt} \in \text{StateTable} \lor \text{ACL}(\text{pkt}) = \text{MATCH}$ |
| Proxy Decision | $\text{Forward if } \text{Inspect}_{L7}(\text{payload}) \rightarrow \text{ALLOW}$ |
| Session Establishment | $\text{SYN} \rightarrow \text{SYN-ACK} \rightarrow \text{ACK} \Rightarrow \text{State} = \text{ESTABLISHED}$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Firewall Choice | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| ISP Backbone | Packet Filter (ACL on routers) | Need line-rate throughput |
| Enterprise Perimeter | Stateful + NGFW | Balance security and speed |
| DMZ Web Servers | ALG + WAF | Protect public-facing apps |
| Internal Segmentation | NGFW (user-identity aware) | East-west traffic control |
| Cloud Workloads | Virtual NGFW / WAF | Scalable, API-driven |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Packet Filter Decision Algorithm

A stateless packet filter evaluates each packet against an ordered Access Control List. The formal decision function is:

$$D(\text{pkt}) = \begin{cases} \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } \exists i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\} : R_i(\text{pkt}) = \text{MATCH}_{\text{allow}} \\ \text{DENY} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step-by-step explanation of the rule evaluation logic:**

1. **Step 1 — Header Extraction:** The firewall parses the incoming packet's L3/L4 headers to obtain the 5-tuple:
   $\langle IP_{src}, IP_{dst}, Port_{src}, Port_{dst}, Proto \rangle$

2. **Step 2 — Sequential Rule Scan:** The firewall iterates through rules in **top-down order** (first-match semantics).

3. **Step 3 — Pattern Matching:** For each rule $R_i$, the tuple is compared against the rule's fields. Wildcards (e.g., `any`) match all values.

4. **Step 4 — Action Selection:** The first matching rule determines the action (ACCEPT, DROP, REJECT).

5. **Step 5 — Default Deny:** If no rule matches, the packet is **implicitly denied** (principle of least privilege).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stateful Inspection State Machine

The TCP state machine tracked by a stateful firewall is a **finite state automaton (FSA)**. The transition function $\delta$ is defined as:

$$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$$

where $Q$ is the set of connection states and $\Sigma$ is the alphabet of TCP flags observed.

For a standard TCP session:

$$\delta(\text{LISTEN}, \text{SYN}) = \text{SYN\_RECEIVED}$$
$$\delta(\text{SYN\_RECEIVED}, \text{SYN-ACK}) = \text{ESTABLISHED}$$
$$\delta(\text{ESTABLISHED}, \text{FIN}) = \text{CLOSE\_WAIT}$$
$$\delta(\text{ESTABLISHED}, \text{RST}) = \text{CLOSED}$$

**Interpretation:** A packet with the ACK flag set, arriving from outside, will be allowed **only if** the firewall's state table already contains an entry in the $\text{ESTABLISHED}$ state for that exact 5-tuple. Otherwise, the packet is dropped as a **"stray ACK"** — a classic port-scanning signature.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attack Coverage by Firewall Type

The following matrix maps common network attacks to the firewall type capable of defeating them. This is a **high-yield KTU table** — questions on "Which firewall stops X attack?" appear every semester.

| Attack Vector | Packet Filter | Stateful | ALG | NGFW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| IP Spoofing | Partial | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| SYN Flood | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Port Scanning | No | Partial | Yes | Yes |
| ACK Scan | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Application Exploit (SQLi) | No | No | Partial | Yes |
| Malware Download | No | No | URL only | Yes |
| DNS Tunneling | No | No | Partial | Yes |
| SSL-Encrypted Malware | No | No | No | Yes (with decryption) |
| Insider Threat (East-West) | No | Limited | No | Yes (with segmentation) |
| Zero-Day Exploit | No | No | No | Partial (anomaly-based) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: ACL Evaluation Order Problem

**Problem (KTU-Style 7-mark):** Consider an ACL with the following rules on a corporate firewall:

| Order | Source IP | Dest IP | Dest Port | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.0.0.0/8 | any | 80 | ALLOW |
| 2 | 10.1.1.5 | any | 80 | DENY |
| 3 | any | any | any | DENY |

A packet arrives with $\text{Source} = 10.1.1.5$, $\text{Dest Port} = 80$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Evaluate Rule 1. Source 10.1.1.5 falls within 10.0.0.0/8 → MATCH (ALLOW).

**Step 2 (The Trap):** Because Rule 1 matched first, **Rule 2 is never evaluated**. The packet is allowed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often wrongly conclude that the specific DENY rule (10.1.1.5) overrides the broad ALLOW. ACLs are **strictly first-match**. The correct fix is to reorder: put specific DENY rules **before** broad ALLOW rules.

**Step 3 (Corrected Order):**

| Order | Source IP | Dest IP | Dest Port | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.1.1.5 | any | 80 | DENY |
| 2 | 10.0.0.0/8 | any | 80 | ALLOW |
| 3 | any | any | any | DENY |

Now Rule 1 matches first, and the packet is correctly denied.

---

## 3.5 Quantitative Throughput Derivation

For an NGFW, the effective throughput $T_{eff}$ is degraded by inspection overhead:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{T_{raw}}{1 + \alpha \cdot D_{pkt} \cdot L_{payload}}$$

Where:

- $T_{raw}$ = wire-speed throughput (e.g., 10 Gbps)
- $\alpha$ = per-byte DPI processing coefficient (typically $10^{-9}$ s/byte)
- $D_{pkt}$ = packet rate (packets/second)
- $L_{payload}$ = average payload size (bytes)

**Interpretation:** Higher DPI depth ($L_{payload}$) reduces throughput non-linearly. This is why NGFWs are sized carefully — a 10 Gbps link may only deliver 4–5 Gbps of inspected throughput.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Firewall Placement in a Network Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    internet[INTERNET UNTRUSTED ZONE]
    ngenv[NGFW PERIMETER FIREWALL]
    ddmz[DMZ WEB SERVERS]
    waf[WEB APPLICATION FIREWALL WAF]
    intfw[INTERNAL STATEFUL FIREWALL]
    db[INTERNAL DATABASE SERVERS]
    users[EMPLOYEE WORKSTATIONS]

    internet -->|HTTPS 443| ngenv
    ngenv -->|HTTP HTTPS Filtered| ddmz
    ddmz -->|WAF Inspects HTTP| waf
    waf -->|Clean HTTP| ddmz
    ddmz -->|Allowed DB Traffic 3306| intfw
    intfw -->|User Authenticated Traffic| users
    users -->|East West NGFW| db
```

**Explanation of the flow:**

- The **NGFW** sits at the perimeter, performing stateful + DPI on all inbound/outbound traffic.
- The **DMZ** hosts public-facing servers, protected by a **WAF** in front.
- A separate **internal stateful firewall** segments the corporate LAN from the DMZ.
- East-west (internal) traffic is also subjected to micro-segmentation NGFW policies.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: OSI Layer Mapping of Firewall Types

```mermaid
graph LR
    l2[OSI L2 DATA LINK]
    l3[OSI L3 NETWORK]
    l4[OSI L4 TRANSPORT]
    l5[OSI L5 SESSION]
    l7[OSI L7 APPLICATION]

    pf[PACKET FILTER FIREWALL]
    sf[STATEFUL INSPECTION]
    cg[CIRCUIT LEVEL GATEWAY]
    ag[APPLICATION LEVEL GATEWAY]
    ng[NEXT GEN FIREWALL]
    wf[WEB APPLICATION FIREWALL]

    l2 --- ng
    l3 --- pf
    l3 --- sf
    l3 --- ng
    l4 --- pf
    l4 --- sf
    l4 --- ng
    l5 --- sf
    l5 --- cg
    l5 --- ng
    l7 --- ag
    l7 --- ng
    l7 --- wf
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Stateful Firewall Connection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LISTEN
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD : SYN Received
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : SYN ACK Received
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT : FIN Received
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSED : RST Received
    FIN_WAIT --> TIME_WAIT : ACK of FIN
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : 2MSL Timeout
    CLOSED --> [*]
```

**Operational Interpretation for KTU Exam:**

- A packet is allowed into the network only if the connection is in the **ESTABLISHED**, **FIN_WAIT**, or **TIME_WAIT** state.
- An unsolicited SYN from outside creates a NEW entry — but it must complete the handshake within the timeout, otherwise it is evicted (SYN flood defense).

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Application-Level Gateway (Proxy) Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    client[INTERNAL CLIENT HOST A]
    proxy[PROXY BASTION HOST FIREWALL]
    server[EXTERNAL WEB SERVER HOST B]

    client -->|HTTP Request to proxy 8080| proxy
    proxy -->|Inspect HTTP Headers URL Method| proxy
    proxy -->|Originate NEW HTTP Request| server
    server -->|HTTP Response| proxy
    proxy -->|Inspect Response Strip Headers| proxy
    proxy -->|Forward Clean Response| client
```

**Note:** The internal client **never talks directly** to the external server. All L7 communication is mediated, which is why ALGs are considered the most secure classical firewall type.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between a stateless packet filter and a stateful inspection firewall. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Packet Filter (Stateless) | Stateful Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | L3, L4 | L3, L4, L5 |
| State Tracking | None — each packet independent | Maintains connection state table |
| Attack Defense | Cannot prevent SYN flood, IP spoofing | Defeats SYN flood, ACK scans |
| Speed | Faster (single ACL lookup) | Slightly slower (table lookup) |

A stateful firewall maintains a state table containing entries for every active TCP connection, while a stateless firewall treats each packet as an independent entity with no memory of prior packets. (3 Marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is an Application-Level Gateway? Mention one advantage and one limitation. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An **Application-Level Gateway (ALG)**, also called a proxy firewall, operates at **OSI Layer 7** and acts as a mediator between internal clients and external servers. It terminates the client connection, inspects the application protocol (HTTP, FTP, DNS), and then initiates a new connection to the destination.

- **Advantage:** Provides deep content inspection and protocol compliance; hides internal IP addresses.
- **Limitation:** High latency and requires a dedicated proxy per application protocol.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 4 carries 14 marks. The student is given a choice between two full 14-mark questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Stateful Inspection Firewall** with a neat diagram. Compare it with a **Packet Filtering Firewall** on at least four parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the architecture and features of a **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)**. How does it differ from a traditional stateful firewall? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Working of Stateful Inspection Firewall (7 Marks)**

A stateful inspection firewall operates at **OSI Layers 3, 4, and 5** and maintains a **dynamic state table** to track all active network connections.

**Working Steps:**

1. **Packet Arrival:** When the first packet of a new session arrives (e.g., SYN from internal client), the firewall creates a new entry in the state table containing:
   $\langle \text{IP}_{src}, \text{Port}_{src}, \text{IP}_{dst}, \text{Port}_{dst}, \text{Proto}, \text{State} \rangle$

2. **Rule Check:** The firewall consults the ACL. If permitted, the packet is forwarded and the state table entry transitions to $\text{SYN\_RECEIVED}$.

3. **Handshake Completion:** Upon receiving the SYN-ACK and final ACK, the entry transitions to $\text{ESTABLISHED}$.

4. **Return Traffic:** All subsequent packets in that session are allowed **without ACL lookup** — they are matched against the state table (fast-path).

5. **Session Teardown:** On FIN or RST, the entry is removed after a timeout (typically 30 – 120 seconds).

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 for state machine)**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Packet Filter | Stateful |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | L3, L4 | L3, L4, L5 |
| Connection State | Not maintained | Tracked in state table |
| Return Traffic | Requires explicit ACL rule | Auto-allowed via state |
| Performance | Very high | Slightly lower |
| Attack Defense | Weak against spoofing | Strong against SYN flood |
| Memory Usage | Minimal | High (for state table) |
| Configuration Complexity | Simple ACLs | Stateful rules + TCP flags |

---

**(b) NGFW Architecture and Features (7 Marks)**

A **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)** is defined by Gartner (2009) as a deep-packet-inspection firewall that moves beyond port/protocol inspection to include:

**Core Architectural Components:**

1. **Stateful Inspection Engine** — L3/L4 baseline filtering.
2. **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** — Examines payload bytes up to Layer 7.
3. **Application Awareness** — Identifies applications regardless of port (e.g., Skype over port 80).
4. **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** — Signature + anomaly detection integrated inline.
5. **User Identity Awareness** — Integrates with AD/LDAP for per-user policies.
6. **SSL/TLS Decryption** — Intercepts and inspects encrypted traffic via MITM.
7. **Threat Intelligence Feeds** — Real-time IP/domain reputation databases.
8. **Sandboxing** — Detonates suspicious files in isolated VMs.

**Difference from Traditional Stateful Firewall:**

| Feature | Stateful | NGFW |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Up to L5 | Up to L7 |
| Application Visibility | None | Full |
| User Identity | IP-based | User/Group-based |
| Threat Intelligence | None | Real-time feeds |
| Encrypted Traffic | Blind | Decrypts and inspects |
| Inline IPS | Separate appliance | Integrated |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly write "NGFW = Stateful + Proxy" and lose 2 marks. The correct answer requires specifying **at least three differentiating capabilities**: application awareness, user-identity awareness, and integrated threat intelligence. Mere addition of DPI is insufficient.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the working of an **Application-Level Gateway (Proxy Firewall)**. List any two advantages and two disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)**? Explain how it protects against SQL Injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Application-Level Gateway (7 Marks)**

An **Application-Level Gateway (ALG)** operates at **OSI Layer 7** and serves as a relay of application-level traffic between internal and external hosts.

**Working Principle (refer to Section 4.4 for diagram):**

1. The internal client sends a request (e.g., HTTP GET) addressed to the **proxy server**, not directly to the external server.
2. The proxy receives the request, performs **full Layer 7 inspection** of the HTTP headers, URL, cookies, and body.
3. If the request complies with the security policy, the proxy **originates a brand-new TCP connection** to the destination server.
4. The server's response is also inspected by the proxy before being forwarded to the client.
5. The internal client's IP is hidden (Network Address Translation effect).

**Advantages:**
- Full application-layer visibility and protocol enforcement.
- Hides internal network topology from external attackers.

**Disadvantages:**
- High latency due to per-packet proxying.
- Requires one proxy instance per protocol (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, etc.).

---

**(b) Web Application Firewall (WAF) (7 Marks)**

A **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** is a specialized Layer 7 firewall that filters, monitors, and blocks HTTP/HTTPS traffic to and from a web application.

**Protection against SQL Injection:**
A WAF inspects HTTP request parameters for known SQL meta-characters (e.g., `'`, `OR 1=1`, `UNION SELECT`, `--`). It uses:
- **Signature-based rules** matching OWASP CRS (Core Rule Set) patterns.
- **Anomaly detection** identifying unusual input lengths or character distributions.

When a malicious payload is detected, the WAF blocks the request and returns HTTP 403 Forbidden.

**Protection against XSS (Cross-Site Scripting):**
The WAF scans request payloads and response bodies for HTML/JavaScript injection patterns such as `<script>`, `javascript:`, `onerror=`, and event handlers. It can:
- **Sanitize input** by stripping dangerous tags.
- **Block requests** matching XSS signatures.
- **Encode output** to neutralize embedded scripts.

**WAF Deployment Modes:**
- **Reverse Proxy** (most common, sits in front of the web server).
- **Transparent Bridge** (inline, no IP changes).
- **Cloud-Hosted** (e.g., AWS WAF, Cloudflare WAF).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students confuse WAF with NGFW. The correct distinction: **WAF = Layer 7 HTTP-only specialist**, while **NGFW = Layer 2–7 generalist with application awareness**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this distinction is not made explicit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 4: Types of Firewalls**

- **Six firewall types:** Packet Filter, Stateful, Circuit-Level, ALG, NGFW, WAF.
- **Packet Filter = Stateless, Layer 3/4, fast but spoofable.**
- **Stateful = Layer 3/4/5, tracks TCP state machine, defeats SYN flood.**
- **Circuit-Level Gateway = Layer 5, verifies TCP handshake only, no payload inspection.**
- **Application-Level Gateway (ALG) = Layer 7 proxy, full payload inspection, also called Bastion Host.**
- **NGFW = Stateful + DPI + IPS + User Identity + Threat Intel + SSL Decryption (Gartner 2009 definition).**
- **WAF = HTTP/HTTPS specialist, protects against OWASP Top 10 (SQLi, XSS, CSRF).**
- **Default ACL behavior = First-match, then implicit DENY (least privilege).**
- **OSI layer memory trick:** "**P**acket filter at **P**ort level (L4), **S**tateful at **S**ession level (L5), **A**pp at **A**pplication (L7)."
- **Throughput trade-off:** More inspection depth → lower throughput (formula in Section 3.5).
- **State table entry tuple:** $\langle IP_{src}, Port_{src}, IP_{dst}, Port_{dst}, Proto, State, Seq \rangle$
- **NGFW ≠ Stateful + Proxy.** Examiners expect the three pillars: **Application Awareness, User Identity, Threat Intelligence.**
- **WAF deployment modes:** Reverse Proxy, Transparent Bridge, Cloud-Hosted.
- **Circuit-Level vs. ALG:** Both act as relays, but only ALG inspects payload.
- **Common exam trap:** Rule ordering in ACLs — **specific DENY rules must precede broad ALLOW rules.**
- **High-Yield Synonyms:** ALG = Proxy Firewall = Bastion Host; NGFW = Unified Threat Management (UTM) in older literature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firewall Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Firewall Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Firewall Configuration** refers to the strategic architectural deployment of one or more firewall components (packet filters, proxy gateways, screening routers) within a network topology to enforce a defined **Security Policy**. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, firewall configuration is the process of defining the *placement*, *interconnection*, and *operational rule-set* of firewall elements that mediate traffic between two or more network security domains — typically a **Trusted Internal Network**, an **Untrusted External Network** (Internet), and an optional **Semi-Trusted Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> A firewall configuration is a *policy-driven, topology-aware arrangement* of filtering devices that collectively implement the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** across a network boundary by inspecting packet headers, connection states, or application payloads before permitting or denying transit.

## 1.2 Three Generations of Firewall Architectures

The KTU 2024 module explicitly classifies firewalls into three evolutionary generations. Each generation corresponds to a different *layer of the OSI model* at which the filtering decision is made.

| Generation | OSI Layer | Mechanism | Typical Device |
|------------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1st Gen | L3 – Network | Static Packet Filtering | Screening Router |
| 2nd Gen | L4 – Transport | Stateful Inspection | Stateful Firewall |
| 3rd Gen | L7 – Application | Application-Level Gateway | Proxy / Bastion Host |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Board Exams:** The KTU examiner will *always* ask you to identify which **generation** a given configuration belongs to. Memorize the OSI layer mapping above — it is the single most tested fact in Module 4.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bouncer at a Club

Imagine a high-end nightclub. A **firewall** is the bouncer standing at the door.

- **The Bouncer's Rulebook** → Firewall Policy / Access Control List (ACL)
- **Checking the Guest List (name only)** → Packet Filtering (1st Gen): Looks at the *header* (IP, port) — does not know the *content*
- **Tracking who is inside the club and re-validating their return** → Stateful Inspection (2nd Gen): Maintains a *connection state table*
- **The VIP host who tastes the drink and verifies the invitation card** → Application-Level Gateway (3rd Gen): Inspects the *payload* (HTTP commands, SMTP headers)

A **Firewall Configuration** is essentially the *layout of the club entrances* — where the bouncer stands, how many doors there are, and which doors lead to the VIP lounge (DMZ) versus the staff-only area (internal network).

## 1.4 Why Firewall Configurations Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU emphasizes that *no single firewall is sufficient for enterprise networks*. A single point of failure at the perimeter exposes the entire internal LAN. Configurations such as **Screened Subnet (DMZ)** exist to *eliminate single points of failure* and to *segment trust zones*.

The three trust zones in a typical configuration are:
1. **Internet (Untrusted)** — No implicit trust; every packet is hostile until verified.
2. **DMZ (Semi-Trusted)** — Hosts public-facing services (Web, Mail, DNS).
3. **Internal Network (Trusted)** — Corporate LAN, databases, employee workstations.
</br>

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Students often confuse *firewall types* (packet filter, proxy) with *firewall configurations* (screened host, DMZ). Remember: **Type = what it does**, **Configuration = where and how it is placed in the topology**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Firewall Configurations

KTU 2024 Module 4 mandates study of the following four topologies. Each is a *configuration*, meaning it is a *placement strategy* of one or more firewall elements.

### 2.1.1 Configuration 1: Single-Homed Bastion Host (Basic Firewall)

- **Topology:** Internal Network ↔ **Bastion Host** (with firewall software) ↔ **Screening Router** ↔ Internet
- **Components:** One packet-filtering router + one bastion host.
- **Rule of Operation:** The router filters obviously bad traffic; the bastion host runs *proxy services* for all allowed applications.
- **Weakness:** **Single point of failure**. If the bastion host is compromised, the entire internal network is exposed.

### 2.1.2 Configuration 2: Dual-Homed Bastion Host

- **Topology:** Internet ↔ Router ↔ **Bastion Host (with TWO NICs)** ↔ Internal Network
- **Components:** One screening router + one bastion host with two network interfaces.
- **Key Property:** IP forwarding is **DISABLED** at the OS level. The bastion acts as a *forced proxy* — packets cannot pass through it directly.
- **Security Gain:** Higher than single-homed because the OS-level disablement of routing makes it impossible to bypass the proxy.

### 2.1.3 Configuration 3: Screened Host (with Single Firewall)

- **Topology:** Internet ↔ **Screening Router** ↔ **Bastion Host** ↔ Internal Network
- **Distinction from Dual-Homed:** The bastion host here is *internal* to the screening router but sits on the *internal network*. It may or may not be dual-homed.
- **Rule of Operation:** Router permits traffic **only to and from the bastion host**. All other internal hosts are invisible to the Internet.

### 2.1.4 Configuration 4: Screened Subnet (DMZ) — The Gold Standard

- **Topology:**
  - Internet ↔ **External Router (Screening Router 1)** ↔ **DMZ** ↔ **Internal Router (Screening Router 2)** ↔ Internal Network
  - The **Bastion Host** sits in the **DMZ**.
- **Two Firewalls, Three Zones:** Untrusted / DMZ / Trusted.
- **Key Property:** An attacker would have to *compromise BOTH routers* to reach the internal LAN. This eliminates the single point of failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Favoritism:** Screened Subnet / DMZ is the most frequently asked configuration. Be prepared to draw it and justify why it is superior to the screened host.

## 2.2 The Bastion Host — In-Depth

A **Bastion Host** is a *highly hardened* computer that serves as the *only entry point* into the trusted network from the outside. It is explicitly *exposed* to the Internet and therefore runs the *minimum necessary services*.

### Hardening Checklist (High-Yield for KTU)
- Stripped-down OS (minimum services)
- Patched and audited regularly
- Runs proxy / application-level gateway software
- All unnecessary services disabled
- Extensive logging and auditing enabled
- User account discipline (no general user accounts)

## 2.3 Packet Filtering — The Rule Logic

A packet filter inspects each packet against an **Access Control List (ACL)** — an ordered list of rules. Rules are processed **top-down**, and the **first match wins**.

### ACL Rule Format
```
ACTION  PROTOCOL  SOURCE_IP  DEST_IP  SOURCE_PORT  DEST_PORT  FLAGS
```

Actions: **PERMIT** (allow) or **DENY** (drop silently) or **REJECT** (drop + send ICMP unreachable).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although firewall configuration is largely architectural, the following quantitative metrics and rule-evaluation concepts are testable.

| Concept | Expression / Definition | Unit / Domain |
|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| **Filter Throughput** | $\vert T_{filter} \vert = \frac{\vert P_{total} \vert}{\Delta t}$ | packets per second (pps) |
| **Rule Order Cost** | $C_{lookup} = O(n)$ for linear ACL scan | n = number of rules |
| **Optimized ACL Cost** | $C_{lookup} = O(\log n)$ via tree indexing | n = number of rules |
| **Attack Surface Reduction** | $\Delta S = S_{no-firewall} - S_{with-firewall}$ | dimensionless score |
| **DMZ Hop Count** | $H_{DMZ} = 2$ routers in series | hop count |
| **Bastion Trust Level** | $T_{bastion} < T_{internal} < T_{DMZ-protected}$ | ordinal trust |
| **Default Policy** | **Default-Deny** (recommended by KTU/NIST) | policy paradigm |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Context:** Every enterprise network, cloud VPC, and 5G core uses a variant of the **Screened Subnet** configuration. AWS **Public Subnets** ≈ DMZ, AWS **Private Subnets** ≈ Internal Network, and **Internet Gateways + Security Groups + NACLs** ≈ screening routers + ACLs. The KTU 2024 syllabus is explicitly designed to prepare students for such cloud-native designs.
</br>

## 2.6 Comparison Matrix — All Four Configurations

| Property | Single-Homed | Dual-Homed | Screened Host | Screened Subnet (DMZ) |
|----------|--------------|------------|---------------|------------------------|
| # of Routers | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| # of Bastions | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 (in DMZ) |
| Single Point of Failure | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Public Servers Placement | Internal LAN | Bastion | Bastion | **DMZ** |
| IP Forwarding on Bastion | Enabled | **Disabled** | May vary | N/A (routers handle) |
| Security Level | Low | Medium | Medium-High | **Highest** |
| Cost | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Low | Medium | High | **Highest** |
</br>

> [!NOTE]
> The screening router in *Screened Host* configuration is sometimes called a **Choke Router** because it "chokes" traffic before it reaches the bastion. Be ready to use this synonym.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Rule Evaluation Walk-Through

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests ACL evaluation. The examiner gives a *packet* and an *ordered rule set*; you must determine the action. The methodology below is a derivation-style explanation of the *why* behind the answer.

### Problem Setup
Given an ACL with 3 ordered rules and an incoming packet, derive the decision.

**Step 1 — Parse the Packet Header.**
Extract the 5-tuple:
$$ \vec{H}_{packet} = \langle SrcIP, DstIP, SrcPort, DstPort, Protocol \rangle $$

For a typical TCP packet from `10.0.0.5` → `203.0.113.10:80`:
$$ \vec{H}_{packet} = \langle 10.0.0.5,\ 203.0.113.10,\ 49152,\ 80,\ TCP \rangle $$

**Step 2 — Initialize the Decision Variable.**
$$ D = \emptyset \quad \text{(No decision yet)} $$

**Step 3 — Iterate Through the ACL in Order.**
For each rule $R_i$ where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:
$$ \text{Match}(R_i, \vec{H}_{packet}) = \bigwedge_{k} \big( R_i.field_k \equiv \vec{H}_{packet}.field_k \big) $$

**Step 4 — Apply the First-Match Logic.**
$$ D = \begin{cases} \text{PERMIT}, & \text{if } \exists i \in [1,n] : \text{Match}(R_i, \vec{H}_{packet}) \land R_i.action = \text{PERMIT} \land \forall j < i : \lnot\text{Match}(R_j) \\ \text{DENY}, & \text{otherwise (default-deny fallback)} \end{cases} $$

**Step 5 — Execute the Action.**
If $D = \text{PERMIT}$, the packet is forwarded. If $D = \text{DENY}$, the packet is dropped (or rejected).

### Why Default-Deny is Mathematically Superior

The probability of a *correct decision* under default-deny is:
$$ P_{correct} = P_{ACL\ covers\ legitimate\ traffic} + (1 - P_{ACL\ covers\ legitimate\ traffic}) \cdot P_{default} $$

Under **default-permit**, *any unlisted rule* allows the packet, so:
$$ P_{breach} = 1 - P_{ACL\ comprehensive\ coverage} \xrightarrow{\text{large } n} 1 $$

This is why **NIST SP 800-41** and KTU 2024 both mandate **default-deny** as the only acceptable baseline.

## 3.2 Derivation — Bastion Compromise Probability in Screened Subnet vs Screened Host

This is a derivation-style question KTU 2024 may ask in the 14-mark section.

**Assumption:** Compromise of each component is an *independent event*.

### Screened Host Configuration
The internal network is compromised if **either** the router OR the bastion is compromised:
$$ P_{breach}^{SH} = 1 - (1 - P_{router})(1 - P_{bastion}) $$

$$ P_{breach}^{SH} = P_{router} + P_{bastion} - P_{router} \cdot P_{bastion} $$

For small probabilities (typical):
$$ P_{breach}^{SH} \approx P_{router} + P_{bastion} $$

### Screened Subnet (DMZ) Configuration
The internal network is compromised only if **both** the external router AND the internal router are bypassed (or the bastion is compromised AND one router is bypassed):
$$ P_{breach}^{SS} = 1 - (1 - P_{R1} \cdot P_{R2}) \cdot (1 - P_{bastion}) $$

For small probabilities:
$$ P_{breach}^{SS} \approx P_{R1} \cdot P_{R2} + P_{bastion} $$

### Comparative Result

Taking $P_{router} = P_{bastion} = 0.05$ (5% compromise chance):
$$ P_{breach}^{SH} \approx 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.10 \quad (10\%) $$
$$ P_{breach}^{SS} \approx 0.05 \times 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.0525 \quad (5.25\%) $$

**Conclusion:** The Screened Subnet configuration nearly **halves the breach probability**, mathematically justifying its adoption in high-security environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** When you write this derivation, explicitly state the *independence assumption*. Examiners award 1-2 marks for valid assumptions in KTU 2024 scheme.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — Designing a Screened Subnet for a Web Hosting Company

A KTU 14-mark question may ask: *"Design a firewall configuration for a company that hosts a public website and an internal employee database."*

### Step 1 — Identify Trust Zones
- **External:** Internet (Untrusted)
- **DMZ:** Public Web Server, Public Mail Server, Public DNS
- **Internal:** Employee Database, File Server, Workstations (Trusted)

### Step 2 — Place Components
- **External Router (R1):** Filters inbound traffic from the Internet. Allows HTTP/HTTPS/SMTP/DNS to DMZ hosts only.
- **Bastion Host:** Placed in DMZ. Acts as proxy for internal users accessing the Internet (outbound filtering).
- **Internal Router (R2):** Filters traffic between DMZ and Internal LAN. Blocks all *unsolicited* inbound from DMZ.

### Step 3 — Define ACLs (Sample)

**R1 ACL (External → DMZ):**
```
PERMIT  TCP  any  203.0.113.10  any  80    (HTTP to Web)
PERMIT  TCP  any  203.0.113.10  any  443   (HTTPS to Web)
PERMIT  TCP  any  203.0.113.11  any  25    (SMTP to Mail)
PERMIT  UDP  any  203.0.113.12  any  53    (DNS)
DENY    ANY  any  any          any  any    (default-deny)
```

**R2 ACL (DMZ → Internal):**
```
PERMIT  TCP  203.0.113.10  10.0.0.5  any  3306   (Web → DB only on MySQL port)
DENY    ANY  any          any       any  any
```

### Step 4 — Justify the Design
- A compromise of the Web Server does **not** directly expose the Database.
- An attacker would need to *pivot through R2*, which has a strict ACL.
- This satisfies the KTU-mandated **defense in depth** principle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Screened Subnet (DMZ) — The Gold Standard Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    internet[Internet Untrusted Zone]
    R1[R1 External Screening Router]
    DMZ[DMZ Bastion Host Web Server Mail Server DNS]
    R2[R2 Internal Screening Router]
    internal[Internal Network DB Server Workstations]

    internet -->|HTTP HTTPS SMTP DNS filtered| R1
    R1 -->|only DMZ-bound traffic| DMZ
    DMZ -->|proxied outbound via bastion| R1
    R1 -.->|default-deny all else| internet

    DMZ -->|strict ACL TCP 3306 to DB only| R2
    R2 -->|filtered to internal| internal
    internal -->|outbound via R2 only| R2

    classDef untrusted fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef dmz fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef trusted fill:#d4edda,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef router fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000

    class internet untrusted
    class DMZ dmz
    class internal,DB trusted
    class R1,R2 router
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Dual-Homed Bastion Host Configuration

```mermaid
graph TB
    inet[Internet]
    routerA[Screening Router]
    bastionA[Dual Homed Bastion Host NIC1 and NIC2]
    internalA[Internal Network]

    inet -->|inbound filtered| routerA
    routerA -->|only to bastion NIC1| bastionA
    bastionA -->|proxied only IP forwarding DISABLED| internalA
    internalA -.->|forced through proxy| bastionA
    bastionA -.->|egress filtered| routerA
    routerA -.->|outbound| inet

    classDef ext fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef bastion fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef int fill:#d4edda,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef rtr fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000

    class inet ext
    class bastionA bastion
    class internalA int
    class routerA rtr
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stateful Firewall

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Incoming Packet Arrives at NIC]
    P2{Packet Filter Layer 1st Gen}
    P3{State Table Lookup 2nd Gen}
    P4{Application Proxy Layer 3rd Gen}
    P5[PERMIT Forward to LAN]
    P6[DENY Drop and Log]
    P7[Default Deny Fallback]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 -->|header fails| P6
    P2 -->|header ok| P3
    P3 -->|no matching session| P6
    P3 -->|matching session| P4
    P4 -->|payload malicious| P6
    P4 -->|payload clean| P5
    P2 -->|no rule matches| P7
    P3 -->|no rule matches| P7
    P4 -->|no rule matches| P7
    P7 --> P6

    classDef permit fill:#d4edda,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef deny fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000

    class P5 permit
    class P6,P7 deny
    class P2,P3,P4 decision
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Flow for ACL Evaluation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Receive Packet]
    B[Parse 5 Tuple Header]
    C{Compare with Rule 1}
    D{Match}
    E[Apply Rule 1 Action]
    F{Compare with Rule 2}
    G{End of Rule List}
    H[Apply Default Deny]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -->|Match| D
    D -->|Yes| E
    C -->|No Match| F
    F -->|Match| E
    F -->|No Match| G
    G -->|Rules Exhausted| H
    E --> I[Forward or Drop]
    H --> I

    classDef action fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#006600,color:#000

    class A,B,I terminal
    class C,D,F,G logic
    class E,H action
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When the KTU examiner asks to "draw the DMZ configuration," the **most important visual feature** is the **physical separation of the three zones** (Internet / DMZ / Internal) by **two routers in series**. Drawing a single box is worth 0-1 marks; drawing the three zones correctly is worth 4-5 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a packet-filtering firewall and an application-level gateway firewall.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Packet Filter (1st Gen) | Application-Level Gateway (3rd Gen) |
|--------|-------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **OSI Layer** | Network Layer (L3) / Transport Layer (L4) | Application Layer (L7) |
| **Inspection** | Examines packet **header** only | Examines **payload** (e.g., HTTP commands, URLs) |
| **State Awareness** | Stateless (unless stateful variant) | Aware of application session |
| **Speed** | Very fast (header-only) | Slower (deep content inspection) |
| **Example** | iptables `INPUT` chain, Cisco ACL | Squid HTTP proxy, SMTP proxy |

**(1 mark for each distinct valid point; 3 marks total.)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Bastion Host? List any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Bastion Host** is a computer system that is *explicitly exposed* to an untrusted network (typically the Internet) and serves as the *primary gateway* between the untrusted network and the trusted internal network. **(2 marks)**

**Two characteristics:** **(1 mark for both)**
1. It runs only the *minimum required services* (e.g., proxy, DNS, SMTP relay) — all unnecessary services are stripped or disabled.
2. It is *highly hardened* — regular patching, extensive logging/auditing, and strict user-account discipline are mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Screened Subnet (DMZ) firewall configuration** with a neat diagram. Why is it considered the most secure configuration? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain **stateful inspection firewalls**. Compare them with stateless packet filters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
A Screened Subnet configuration uses **two screening routers** and a **Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)** to create three distinct security zones: the external untrusted network (Internet), the DMZ (semi-trusted), and the internal trusted network.

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4.1 — Internet → R1 → DMZ → R2 → Internal LAN, with bastion host in DMZ.)*

**Working (2 marks):**
- The **external router (R1)** filters all inbound traffic from the Internet. It permits only traffic destined to DMZ servers (HTTP, HTTPS, SMTP, DNS).
- The **bastion host** sits in the DMZ and provides proxy services for outbound requests from internal users.
