- The **internal router (R2)** filters traffic between the DMZ and the internal network. It permits *only specific* connections (e.g., Web Server to Database on port 3306) and blocks all other traffic by default.

**Why it is the most secure (2 marks):**
1. **Eliminates single point of failure** — an attacker must compromise *both* R1 and R2 to reach the internal network.
2. **Defense in depth** — three layers of filtering (R1, Bastion, R2).
3. **Public servers are isolated** in the DMZ; a compromised Web Server does not directly expose the internal LAN.
4. **Privacy of internal network topology** — internal addresses are hidden from the Internet by R1.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Stateful Inspection — Definition (2 marks):**
A **stateful inspection firewall** (also called a *stateful packet filter* or *circuit-level gateway*) operates at the **Transport Layer (L4)** and tracks the **state of network connections** using a state table. It makes filtering decisions not only on individual packet headers but also on the *context* of the connection (e.g., SYN, SYN-ACK, ESTABLISHED, FIN).

**Working with example (3 marks):**
*Example:* Consider an internal user at `10.0.0.5` initiating an HTTP connection to `203.0.113.10:80`.
1. The user's machine sends a **SYN** packet. The firewall creates a new entry in its state table:
   $$ \text{State} = \langle 10.0.0.5, 49152 \rightleftharpoons 203.0.113.10, 80, \text{TCP}, \text{SYN\_SENT} \rangle $$
2. The server replies with **SYN-ACK**. The firewall matches the return packet against the state table and updates the state to `ESTABLISHED`.
3. All subsequent packets in the session are allowed *only* if they match this state entry.
4. When the session ends (**FIN**), the entry is removed.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Stateless Packet Filter | Stateful Inspection |
|---------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| OSI Layer | L3/L4 | L4 |
| Connection Context | **No** | **Yes** |
| Speed | Faster | Slightly slower |
| Protection vs. Spoofing | Weak | Strong |
| Resource Usage | Low | Moderate (state table) |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the **three generations of firewalls** with their OSI layer mapping and operational differences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company has a public Web Server, a public Mail Server, an internal database, and employee workstations. **Design a suitable firewall configuration** with proper placement of components and justify your design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Firewalls have evolved through three generations, each adding deeper inspection capabilities.

**Generation 1 — Packet Filters (2 marks):**
- Operate at the **Network Layer (L3)** and **Transport Layer (L4)**.
- Examine only the **packet header** (source IP, destination IP, protocol, port numbers).
- Maintain **no state** — each packet is judged in isolation.
- *Limitation:* Vulnerable to IP spoofing, cannot detect application-level attacks.

**Generation 2 — Stateful Inspection (2 marks):**
- Operate at the **Transport Layer (L4)**.
- Maintain a **state table** that tracks every active connection (SYN, SYN-ACK, ESTABLISHED, FIN).
- Decisions consider both the packet header *and* the connection state.
- *Limitation:* Still cannot inspect application payload content.

**Generation 3 — Application-Level Gateways (2 marks):**
- Operate at the **Application Layer (L7)**.
- Act as a **proxy** for specific applications (HTTP, FTP, SMTP).
- Inspect the *full payload* — URLs, commands, attachments.
- *Limitation:* Slower; one proxy per application protocol.

**Conclusion (optional 0.5 mark):**
Modern enterprise firewalls (NGFW — Next-Generation Firewalls) combine all three, with optional deep-packet-inspection (DPI) modules.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Design Choice: Screened Subnet (DMZ) Configuration.** **(1 mark for naming)**

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4.1.)*

**Component Placement (2 marks):**
- **External Router (R1):** Placed at the Internet boundary. ACL permits HTTP/HTTPS/SMTP/DNS to DMZ servers only.
- **DMZ:** Hosts the public Web Server (`203.0.113.10`), Mail Server (`203.0.113.11`), DNS (`203.0.113.12`), and the **Bastion Host** for outbound proxying.
- **Internal Router (R2):** Placed between DMZ and Internal LAN. ACL permits only the Web Server to talk to the Database on port 3306.
- **Internal Network:** Database Server, employee workstations.

**Justification (2 marks):**
1. **Public services are isolated** in the DMZ — a compromise of the Web Server does not expose the internal database.
2. **Defense in depth** — two routers must be breached to reach the internal LAN.
3. **Default-deny on R2** ensures that even if DMZ is compromised, lateral movement is severely restricted.
4. The bastion host provides **outbound content filtering** and **single egress point** for internal users, simplifying audit trails.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing *types* with *configurations*** — "Packet filter is a configuration" is wrong; it is a *type*. The configuration is *where you place it* (screened host, DMZ, etc.). **Deduction: 1-2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to disable IP forwarding** on the bastion host in dual-homed configuration. **Deduction: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Drawing the DMZ as a single line / single router** — this is a *screened host*, not a DMZ. **Deduction: 2-3 marks on the diagram.**
> 4. **Not stating "default-deny"** explicitly. The KTU 2024 rubric awards marks for the policy statement, not just the rules.
> 5. **Missing the bastion host's role as a proxy** — many students describe the bastion as just "a server." The bastion is *the* application-level gateway host. **Deduction: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- **Three Generations of Firewalls:** Packet Filter (L3/L4) → Stateful Inspection (L4) → Application-Level Gateway (L7).
- **Four Canonical Configurations:** Single-Homed Bastion, Dual-Homed Bastion, Screened Host, Screened Subnet (DMZ).
- **Bastion Host = hardened gateway**; runs minimum services; primary attack surface.
- **Screening Router = Choke Router**; filters at L3/L4 based on ACL.
- **DMZ = Semi-trusted zone** for public-facing servers (Web, Mail, DNS).
- **Two routers in DMZ configuration** = no single point of failure.
- **Dual-Homed Bastion:** IP forwarding **MUST** be disabled at the OS level.
- **Default-Deny policy** is mandatory; default-permit is unacceptable.
- **ACL evaluation is first-match, top-down**, with default-deny at the end.
- **DMZ Breach Probability ≈ $P_{R1} \cdot P_{R2} + P_{bastion}$** (vs. $P_{router} + P_{bastion}$ in screened host).
- **Defense in Depth = multiple filtering layers** (R1 + Bastion + R2 in DMZ).
- **KTU 2024 exam-favorite configuration:** Screened Subnet (DMZ) — draw it with three zones and two routers.
- **NIST SP 800-41** mandates default-deny; mention this in 14-mark answers for bonus marks.
- **Real-world analog:** AWS Public Subnet ≈ DMZ, AWS Private Subnet ≈ Internal LAN, Security Groups ≈ ACLs.
- **Common synonym to recognize:** Choke Router ≡ Screening Router; Circuit-Level Gateway ≡ Stateful Inspection.
- **Always end design questions with a justification** — KTU 2024 awards marks for *reasoning*, not just the diagram.
</br>

> **End of Module 4 — Firewall Configurations Notes**
> *Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme Standards | Aligned with PECAT863 Network Security Protocols*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encrypted Tunnels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Encrypted Tunnels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encrypted Tunnel (Cryptographic Tunnel):** A logically isolated, cryptographically protected communication channel established between two endpoints over an untrusted public network (typically the Internet), where the original payload (header + data) is encapsulated, encrypted, and integrity-protected before transmission, ensuring **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and anti-replay** of all traffic flowing through the tunnel.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PECAT863 – Network Security Protocols, Module 4), **Encrypted Tunnels** primarily refer to:

- **IPsec-based VPN tunnels** (most prominent)
- **SSL/TLS-based tunnels** (e.g., HTTPS, OpenVPN)
- **SSH tunnels** (port forwarding)
- **L2TP/IPsec and PPTP tunnels**

The two dominant architectural models are:

| Tunnel Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Site-to-Site VPN Tunnel** | Permanent tunnel between two gateways (e.g., branch office ↔ HQ). |
| **Remote-Access VPN Tunnel** | On-demand tunnel between a mobile client and a corporate gateway. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Armored Subway Through a Hostile City:**
> Imagine two corporate buildings located in different cities, separated by a crime-ridden, public road (the Internet). You cannot risk your sensitive documents travelling openly. So, you build a **private underground armored subway tunnel** connecting the two buildings:
> - The **tunnel walls** = the encryption (confidentiality).
> - The **sealed locks on every carriage** = authentication + integrity (HMAC).
> - The **serial-numbered carriages** = anti-replay protection (sequence numbers).
> - The **construction blueprint** of the tunnel = Security Association (SA) negotiated by IKE.
> - A person entering the building on either side is unaware of the road above — they just see a **logical pipe** appear between the two endpoints.

This "subway" is the **Encrypted Tunnel** — neither the IP packets nor their original IP headers are visible to anyone on the public road.

## 1.3 Why Encrypted Tunnels Are Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Problem:** Public networks (Internet, Wi-Fi hotspots, ISP backbones) are **untrusted shared media**. Without a tunnel, traffic is exposed to:
> - **Eavesdropping** (sniffing by Wireshark, tcpdump)
> - **Tampering** (man-in-the-middle modification)
> - **Identity spoofing** (IP/MAC spoofing)
> - **Replay attacks** (re-injecting captured packets)
> - **Geolocation / traffic analysis**

A properly constructed encrypted tunnel mitigates all of these via the **CIA + Non-Repudiation** security model.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **VPN** | Virtual Private Network — the logical construct that *uses* tunnels. |
| **IPsec** | Suite of protocols (AH, ESP, IKE) defined in **RFC 4301–4309**. |
| **SA (Security Association)** | A simplex logical connection state — one per direction. |
| **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** | A **32-bit identifier** that uniquely labels an SA. |
| **IKE (Internet Key Exchange)** | Protocol that negotiates SAs and keys automatically. |
| **AH (Authentication Header)** | IPsec protocol 51 — provides integrity + auth (no encryption). |
| **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)** | IPsec protocol 50 — provides encryption + integrity + auth. |
| **Tunnel Mode** | Encrypts the **entire original IP packet** (new outer IP header added). |
| **Transport Mode** | Encrypts **only the payload** (original IP header preserved). |
| **ISAKMP** | Framework defining SA negotiation, key exchange, and payload formats. |

## 1.5 IPsec Protocol Stack Overview

> [!NOTE]
> IPsec operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model — making it transparent to applications. This is its biggest advantage over SSL/TLS (Layer 4–5).

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Application Layer (HTTP, SMTP…)    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Transport Layer (TCP / UDP)        │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Network Layer  ← IPsec lives here  │
│     • AH (Protocol 51)              │
│     • ESP (Protocol 50)             │
│     • IKE (UDP 500 / 4500)          │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Data Link / Physical Layer         │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of IPsec (RFC 4301 Architecture)

The IPsec framework is built from three independent components:

### 2.1.1 Architecture (RFC 4301)
- Defines the **conceptual model**, processing rules, and traffic selectors.
- Specifies that IPsec is **mandatory for IPv6** and **optional for IPv4**.

### 2.1.2 Authentication Header — AH (RFC 4302)
- IP Protocol Number: **51**
- Provides: **Data-origin authentication, Integrity, Anti-replay**
- Does **NOT** provide confidentiality (no encryption).
- Covers: payload + most IP header fields (mutable fields excluded: TTL, Header Checksum, etc.).

### 2.1.3 Encapsulating Security Payload — ESP (RFC 4303)
- IP Protocol Number: **50**
- Provides: **Confidentiality (encryption) + Integrity + Authentication + Anti-replay**.
- This is the **most widely deployed** IPsec protocol.
- Uses symmetric ciphers: **AES-CBC (128/192/256)**, **AES-GCM (AEAD)**, **3DES (deprecated)**, **ChaCha20-Poly1305**.

## 2.2 Security Association (SA) — The Heart of IPsec

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **An SA is a one-way (simplex) logical contract** between two IPsec peers. It contains *everything* both sides need to communicate securely in *one direction*.

### 2.2.1 SA Parameters
Each SA is uniquely identified by a **triple**:

$$
\text{SA Identifier} = \{\text{Dest IP}, \text{IPsec Protocol (AH/ESP)}, \text{SPI}\}
$$

Where **SPI** is a **32-bit arbitrary value** chosen by the receiver.

### 2.2.2 SA Bundles
Since a single SA cannot carry both AH and ESP protection, **bundles** are used:

| Scenario | SA Bundle |
|---|---|
| End-to-end (host-to-host) | **One ESP SA** or **one AH SA** in transport mode |
| Host-to-gateway | One SA per direction; gateway only processes outer header |
| Gateway-to-gateway | One ESP SA + one AH SA = **2 SAs per direction = 4 total** |

### 2.2.3 SA Lifecycle
1. **Initiation** — by IKE Phase 1.
2. **Negotiation** — IKE Phase 2 (Quick Mode) creates Child SAs.
3. **Established** — traffic flows.
4. **Re-keying** — before lifetime expiry (typically **8 hours / 100 MB**).
5. **Termination** — explicit delete (informational exchange) or timeout.

## 2.3 Databases Maintained by Every IPsec Node

> [!NOTE]
> Three databases govern IPsec processing. The KTU examiner loves these.

### 2.3.1 Security Policy Database (SPD)
Defines **what to do with each packet**:
- **PROTECT** → apply IPsec
- **BYPASS** → forward unchanged
- **DISCARD** → drop the packet

### 2.3.2 Security Association Database (SAD)
Holds **active SAs** with parameters: SPI, destination, encryption algo, key, IV, lifetime, anti-replay window.

### 2.3.3 Peer Authentication Database (PAD)
Stores authentication credentials for trusted peers (PSK, certificates, etc.).

## 2.4 IPsec Modes — Transport vs Tunnel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **highest-weightage concept in KTU Module 4**. Memorize the packet structure.

### 2.4.1 Transport Mode
- Used for **host-to-host** communication.
- **Original IP header is preserved**.
- ESP encrypts only the **TCP/UDP payload + ESP trailer**, places ESP header in between.

**Original:** `[IP | TCP | Data]`
**Transport ESP:** `[IP | ESP | TCP | Data | ESP-Trailer | ESP-Auth]`

### 2.4.2 Tunnel Mode
- Used for **gateway-to-gateway** or **host-to-gateway (roadwarrior)**.
- **Entire original IP packet is encrypted** and a **new outer IP header** is prepended.
- **Default mode for VPNs**.

**Original:** `[IP_inner | TCP | Data]`
**Tunnel ESP:** `[IP_outer | ESP | IP_inner | TCP | Data | ESP-Trailer | ESP-Auth]`

## 2.5 ESP Packet Format (Tunnel Mode) — Field-by-Field

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **SPI** | 32 bits | Identifies the SA |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 bits | Anti-replay counter |
| **IV (Initialization Vector)** | 8–16 bytes | For CBC/GCM ciphers (often counted in ciphertext start) |
| **Payload Data (variable)** | n bytes | Encrypted: original IP packet + ESP Trailer padding |
| **Padding** | 0–255 bytes | Aligns to cipher block boundary |
| **Pad Length** | 8 bits | Number of padding bytes |
| **Next Header** | 8 bits | Type of payload inside (e.g., 4 = IPv4, 41 = IPv6) |
| **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** | variable | HMAC-SHA1/96, HMAC-SHA-256, AES-GCM tag |

## 2.6 IKE — Internet Key Exchange (RFC 7296 — IKEv2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IKEv1 has two phases; **IKEv2 (the modern default) uses only two exchanges**.

### 2.6.1 IKEv1 Phase 1 (Main Mode) — 6 Messages
Goal: Establish **ISAKMP SA** (also called IKE SA) — a secure channel to negotiate Phase 2.

1. **MM1:** Initiator → Responder (proposals + transforms)
2. **MM2:** Responder → Initiator (chosen proposal)
3. **MM3:** Initiator → Responder (Key Exchange — Diffie–Hellman public value)
4. **MM4:** Responder → Initiator (DH public value)
5. **MM5:** Initiator → Responder (Identity + Auth — encrypted with derived key)
6. **MM6:** Responder → Initiator (Identity + Auth — encrypted)

*Result:* 4 SAs (2 for ISAKMP, 2 for IPsec) but only **1 ISAKMP SA pair** logically.

### 2.6.2 IKEv1 Phase 2 (Quick Mode) — 3 Messages
- Negotiates **IPsec SAs** (ESP or AH).
- Performs **perfect forward secrecy (PFS)** via optional new DH exchange.
- Refreshes keys.

### 2.6.3 IKEv2 — 4 Messages Total
- **IKE_SA_INIT** (2 messages) — negotiates crypto, exchanges DH, produces SK_* keys.
- **IKE_AUTH** (2 messages) — authenticates identities, sets up first Child SA.
- Optional **CREATE_CHILD_SA** for re-keying or additional SAs.
- **INFORMATIONAL** exchange for SA deletion, error notification, liveness check.

## 2.7 IKE Key Derivation (SKEYID Family)

For IKEv1 Main Mode, after the DH exchange, both peers derive:

$$
\text{SKEYID} = \text{prf}(\text{Pre-shared Key}, \text{Ni} \mid \text{Nr})
$$

$$
\text{SKEYID\_e} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, \text{SKEYID\_a} \mid \text{g}^{xy} \mid \text{CKY-I} \mid \text{CKY-R} \mid 2)
$$

$$
\text{SKEYID\_a} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, \text{SKEYID\_d} \mid \text{g}^{xy} \mid \text{CKY-I} \mid \text{CKY-R} \mid 3)
$$

$$
\text{SKEYID\_d} = \text{prf}(\text{SKEYID}, \text{g}^{xy} \mid \text{CKY-I} \mid \text{CKY-R} \mid 0)
$$

| Key | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **SKEYID_e** | Encryption key for IKE messages |
| **SKEYID_a** | Authentication key (HMAC) for IKE messages |
| **SKEYID_d** | Source material to derive IPsec ESP/AH keys |
| **g^xy** | The shared Diffie–Hellman secret (PFS) |

> [!NOTE]
> Where `prf` is a pseudo-random function (typically HMAC-SHA-1 / HMAC-SHA-256), `Ni` / `Nr` are nonces, and `CKY-I` / `CKY-R` are ISAKMP cookies.

## 2.8 Anti-Replay Window

Each ESP packet carries a **monotonically increasing 32-bit sequence number**. The receiver maintains a **sliding window** of size $W$ (default $W = 32$ in IKEv1, $W = 64$ in IKEv2, $W = 128$ in AH draft extensions).

- Packets with $\text{SeqNum} < W_{left}$ → **dropped** (replay).
- Packets with $W_{left} \le \text{SeqNum} \le W_{right}$ → accepted if not seen before; otherwise dropped.
- Packets with $\text{SeqNum} > W_{right}$ → accepted, window slides right.

## 2.9 Encrypted Tunneling Beyond IPsec

### 2.9.1 SSL/TLS Tunnels
- Operate at **Layer 5–6** (between TCP and Application).
- Used in **OpenVPN, AnyConnect, SSL VPN portals**.
- **TLS 1.3** (RFC 8446) provides a single 1-RTT handshake.

### 2.9.2 SSH Tunnels (Port Forwarding)
- `ssh -L localPort:remoteHost:remotePort user@server` → **Local Forwarding**.
- `ssh -R` → **Remote Forwarding**.
- `ssh -D` → **Dynamic SOCKS proxy**.
- Built-in encryption: AES-CTR, ChaCha20-Poly1305.

### 2.9.3 L2TP/IPsec
- **L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol, RFC 2661)** provides no security — it is a tunneling shell.
- Combined with **IPsec ESP** for confidentiality.
- Default UDP port: **1701** for L2TP, **500 / 4500** for IPsec/IKE.

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Encrypted Tunnels**

| Concept | Formula / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **IPsec Protocol Numbers** | AH = 51, ESP = 50 | Assigned by IANA |
| **IKE Ports** | UDP 500 (initial), UDP 4500 (NAT-T) | Well-known ports |
| **SA Identifier** | (Dest IP, Protocol, SPI) | Uniquely identifies an SA |
| **ESP ICV (HMAC-SHA-1)** | 96 bits (12 bytes) | Output truncated |
| **ESP ICV (HMAC-SHA-256)** | 128 bits (16 bytes) | Per RFC 4868 |
| **AES Block Size** | 128 bits (16 bytes) | Determines ESP padding |
| **DH Shared Secret** | $K = g^{ab} \mod p$ | Where $a, b$ are private; $g^a, g^b$ are public |
| **Anti-Replay Window (IKEv2)** | $W = 64$ (default) | 64-packet sliding window |
| **AH Coverage** | Whole packet minus mutable fields | TTL, Header Checksum excluded |
| **ESP Tunnel Mode Overhead** | $20$ (new IP) $+8$ (ESP hdr) $+2$ (ESP trailer) $+12$ (ICV) | ≈ 42 bytes minimum |
| **ISAKMP Message Size** | Max 4 KB | Up to 32 KB for vendors |
| **HMAC Output** | $H = H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | Standard construction |
| **Key Refresh Trigger** | $\min(\text{Time Lifetime}, \text{Byte Lifetime})$ | Whichever expires first |

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Enterprise Networking** | Branch-office connectivity (Cisco IPsec, Palo Alto, Fortinet) |
| **Cloud Computing** | AWS Site-to-Site VPN, Azure VPN Gateway, GCP HA VPN |
| **Telecommunications** | 5G core (N2 interface between gNB and AMF uses IPsec) |
| **Industrial IoT** | Modbus/TCP tunneling over IPsec for SCADA |
| **Remote Workforce** | SSL VPN portals (Cisco AnyConnect, Zscaler) |
| **Defense / Government** | Suite-B / CNSA-compliant IPsec profiles |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: ESP Encrypted Payload Construction (Tunnel Mode)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the on-the-wire byte structure of an ESP-tunnel-mode packet step-by-step.

### Step 1: Original Packet (Before IPsec)

$$
P_{orig} = [\text{IP}_i \mid \text{TCP/UDP} \mid \text{Data}]
$$

where $\text{IP}_i$ is the original (inner) IP header. Let $L(P_{orig}) = N$ bytes.

### Step 2: Append ESP Trailer

ESP trailer contains: **Padding (0–255 bytes), Pad Length (1 byte), Next Header (1 byte)**.

$$
T_{ESP} = [\text{Padding} \mid \text{PadLen} \mid \text{NextHdr}]
$$

Choose padding length $p$ such that the payload is aligned to the cipher block size $B$ (for AES-CBC, $B = 16$ bytes):

$$
p = B - (N + 2 \mod B)
$$

If $(N + 2) \mod B = 0$, then $p = B$ (always add at least one byte of padding per RFC 4303).

### Step 3: Encrypt the Concatenation

$$
C = E_K(P_{orig} \mid T_{ESP})
$$

where $E_K(\cdot)$ is the block cipher (e.g., AES-CBC) and $K$ is the session key.

### Step 4: Prepend ESP Header

ESP header = **SPI (4 bytes) + Sequence Number (4 bytes)**. For CBC mode, also prepend the IV (8 or 16 bytes).

$$
P_{ESP} = [\text{SPI} \mid \text{Seq\#} \mid \text{IV}] \mid C
$$

### Step 5: Compute the ICV (Integrity Check Value)

ICV is computed over **SPI + Sequence Number + IV + Ciphertext** (excluding the ICV itself):

$$
\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC-SHA-}N(K_a, \text{SPI} \mid \text{Seq\#} \mid \text{IV} \mid C)
$$

where $K_a$ is the authentication key (may be the same as $K$ for AEAD ciphers like AES-GCM).

### Step 6: Append the ICV

$$
P_{wire} = P_{ESP} \mid \text{ICV}
$$

### Step 7: Prepend the New Outer IP Header (Tunnel Mode Only)

For **Tunnel mode**, prepend a new IP header $\text{IP}_o$ with source = tunnel endpoint A, destination = tunnel endpoint B:

$$
P_{final} = [\text{IP}_o \mid \text{SPI} \mid \text{Seq\#} \mid \text{IV} \mid C \mid \text{ICV}]
$$

### Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{
P_{final} = \text{IP}_o \,\|\, \text{ESP}_{\text{hdr}} \,\|\, E_K(\text{IP}_i \mid \text{Data} \mid \text{Pad} \mid \text{PadLen} \mid \text{NextHdr}) \,\|\, \text{ICV}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total On-the-Wire Overhead (Tunnel ESP + AES-CBC + HMAC-SHA-1):**
> $\text{Overhead} = 20_{\text{new IP}} + 4_{\text{SPI}} + 4_{\text{Seq}} + 8\text{--}16_{\text{IV}} + 2_{\text{trailer}} + 12_{\text{ICV}} \approx 50\text{–}58 \text{ bytes}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Diffie–Hellman Shared Secret for IKE

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show how both IPsec peers derive the same 1024-bit (or higher) shared secret without transmitting it.

### Step 1: Public Parameters (Agreed Before IKE)

Both peers agree on:
- A large prime $p$ (e.g., 2048-bit for IKEv2 Group 14).
- A generator $g$ (typically $g = 2$).

### Step 2: Initiator Side

- Picks a random private integer $a$ with $1 \le a \le p-2$.
- Computes public value: $A = g^a \mod p$.
- Sends $A$ to Responder in IKE_SA_INIT (IKEv2) or MM3 (IKEv1).

### Step 3: Responder Side

- Picks a random private integer $b$ with $1 \le b \le p-2$.
- Computes public value: $B = g^b \mod p$.
- Sends $B$ to Initiator.

### Step 4: Independent Computation of Shared Secret

Initiator computes:

$$
K_{initiator} = B^a \mod p = (g^b)^a \mod p = g^{ab} \mod p
$$

Responder computes:

$$
K_{responder} = A^b \mod p = (g^a)^b \mod p = g^{ab} \mod p
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{K = g^{ab} \mod p}
$$

### Step 5: Security Properties
- **Forward secrecy:** Even if long-term private keys are later compromised, the session key $K$ cannot be recovered.
- **Discrete-log hardness:** Computing $a$ from $A = g^a \mod p$ is infeasible for large $p$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Eve's Perspective:** Eve sees $A$, $B$, $g$, $p$ — all public. To find $K$, she would need to solve the **Computational Diffie–Hellman (CDH) problem**, which is believed to be hard.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Anti-Replay Sliding Window Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Determine whether an incoming ESP packet with sequence number $N$ is accepted or rejected.

### Definitions

- $W_{right}$ = highest sequence number seen so far.
- $W_{left} = W_{right} - W + 1$, where $W$ is the window size (e.g., 64).
- $S$ = set of sequence numbers already received within the window.

### Decision Algorithm

$$
\text{Decision}(N) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{DROP (Replay)} & \text{if } N < W_{left} \\
\text{check } S; \text{ accept if } N \notin S; \text{ drop if } N \in S & \text{if } W_{left} \le N \le W_{right} \\
\text{ACCEPT; slide window} & \text{if } N > W_{right}
\end{cases}
$$

### Example Walkthrough
Let $W = 4$, current $W_{right} = 100$, $W_{left} = 97$, $S = \{97, 98, 100\}$.

| Incoming $N$ | Decision |
|---|---|
| 95 | DROP ($N < 97$) — replay |
| 97 | DROP — already in $S$ |
| 99 | ACCEPT — new in window |
| 101 | ACCEPT — slide: $W_{right} \leftarrow 101$, $W_{left} \leftarrow 98$ |
| 93 | DROP — far replay |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favorite)

| Feature | IPsec (Tunnel) | SSL/TLS Tunnel (OpenVPN) | SSH Tunnel | L2TP/IPsec |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | 3 (Network) | 5–6 (Session) | 7 (App) | 2 + IPsec |
| **Default Port** | IP proto 50/51, UDP 500 | TCP 443 | TCP 22 | UDP 1701 + UDP 500 |
| **Encryption** | AES-CBC / AES-GCM | AES-GCM (TLS 1.3) | AES-CTR / ChaCha20 | Inherits IPsec |
| **Authentication** | PSK / Cert / RSA | Cert / PSK / Token | Password / Key | PPP + IPsec |
| **Granularity** | Per-packet (subnet-level) | Per-app / Per-port | Per-port forward | Tunnel-wide |
| **Application Transparency** | Full (any IP traffic) | Full (OpenVPN) | Partial | Full |
| **NAT Traversal** | Needs NAT-T (UDP 4500) | Native | Native | Needs NAT-T |
| **Firewall Friendliness** | Often blocked | Excellent (looks like HTTPS) | Allowed by default | Blocked |
| **Setup Complexity** | High (IKE, certificates) | Medium | Low (single command) | High (stacked) |
| **Speed** | Fastest (kernel-mode) | Fast | Moderate | Slowest |

## 3.5 Sample Numerical — ESP Overhead Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical (KTU-style):** A 1,500-byte Ethernet payload carries a TCP segment with **1,200 bytes of application data**. This packet is to be encapsulated in **ESP Tunnel mode** using:
> - AES-CBC-128 (block = 16 B, IV = 16 B)
> - HMAC-SHA-1-96 (ICV = 12 B)
> - No extra IPsec options

**Step 1: Original packet length**

$$
L_{orig} = 1{,}500 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: ESP trailer length**

PadLength (1) + NextHeader (1) = 2 bytes. Add padding $p$ to align to 16-byte boundary:

$$
L_{after} = 1{,}500 + 2 = 1{,}502
$$

$$
1{,}502 \mod 16 = 14
$$

$$
p = 16 - 14 = 2 \text{ bytes of padding}
$$

Total encrypted plaintext:

$$
L_{enc} = 1{,}500 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 1{,}504 \text{ bytes}
$$

Since $1{,}504 \mod 16 = 0$ and RFC 4303 requires at least 1 padding byte, the new padded length with $p = 2$ satisfies alignment.

**Step 3: ESP header + IV**

$$
L_{hdr} = 4_{\text{SPI}} + 4_{\text{Seq}} + 16_{\text{IV}} = 24 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: ICV**

$$
L_{ICV} = 12 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5: Total ESP-protected portion (before new IP header)**

$$
L_{ESP} = 24 + 1{,}504 + 12 = 1{,}540 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6: Add new outer IP header (Tunnel mode)**

$$
L_{final} = 20 + 1{,}540 = 1{,}560 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 7: Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{1{,}200}{1{,}560} \times 100\% = 76.92\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** IPsec ESP Tunnel mode adds **60 bytes of overhead** per packet, reducing effective throughput by ~23% for this packet size.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between IPsec Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode with suitable packet structure diagrams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Header Preservation** | Original IP header kept | New outer IP header added |
| **Encryption Coverage** | Only payload (TCP/UDP + data) | Entire original IP packet |
| **Use Case** | Host-to-host communication | Gateway-to-gateway / VPN |
| **Overhead** | ~20–30 bytes | ~50–60 bytes |
| **End-to-End Visibility** | Hosts know they use IPsec | Hosts are unaware; gateways handle it |

**Diagram (1 mark each):**
- Transport ESP: `[IP orig | ESP | TCP | Data | ESP-Trail | ICV]`
- Tunnel ESP: `[IP new | ESP | IP orig | TCP | Data | ESP-Trail | ICV]`

> [!NOTE]
> Award **1 mark for the tabular comparison**, **1 mark for the transport-mode structure**, and **1 mark for the tunnel-mode structure**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Security Association (SA) in IPsec? Explain the role of the Security Parameters Index (SPI).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Security Association is a **simplex (one-way) logical connection** that defines the security parameters (encryption algorithm, keys, lifetime, mode) used by two IPsec peers to communicate. An SA is required for **each direction** of communication — a full duplex IPsec session uses **two SAs**.
- **SPI Role (1.5 marks):** The SPI is a **32-bit identifier** chosen by the receiving IPsec endpoint that, together with the destination IP address and the IPsec protocol (AH=51 or ESP=50), **uniquely identifies an SA** in the receiver's SAD. When the receiver gets an IPsec packet, it uses the SPI to look up the correct SA and applies the right key and algorithm to verify/decrypt the packet.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): IPsec Architecture, ESP, and IKE**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **a)** Explain the IPsec architecture with reference to **AH, ESP, and the three databases (SPD, SAD, PAD)**. State clearly which protocol provides confidentiality. **(7 marks)**
>
> **b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **ESP packet format in Tunnel mode**. Explain the role of each field and show how anti-replay protection is achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. IPsec Architecture (2 marks):**
- IPsec is a **Layer 3 security framework** (RFC 4301) that operates transparently above IP and below transport-layer protocols.
- It is **mandatory in IPv6**, optional in IPv4.
- Provides CIA + anti-replay at the IP layer for all upper-layer traffic.

**2. AH – Authentication Header (1.5 marks):**
- Protocol number **51**.
- Provides **data-origin authentication, integrity, anti-replay**.
- Does **not** encrypt — payload is in cleartext.
- Computes ICV over the **entire IP packet except mutable fields** (TTL, Header Checksum).

**3. ESP – Encapsulating Security Payload (1.5 marks):**
- Protocol number **50**.
- Provides **confidentiality (encryption) + integrity + authentication + anti-replay**.
- **This is the protocol that provides confidentiality in IPsec.**
- Can be used in **Transport or Tunnel mode**.

**4. Three Databases (2 marks):**
- **SPD (Security Policy Database):** Holds policies — PROTECT / BYPASS / DISCARD — keyed by selectors (src IP, dst IP, protocol, port).
- **SAD (Security Association Database):** Holds *active* SAs with keys, SPIs, lifetimes, anti-replay windows, and algorithms.
- **PAD (Peer Authentication Database):** Stores credentials (PSK, certificates) for authenticating each peer before SA establishment.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. ESP Tunnel-Mode Packet Diagram (3 marks):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  New Outer IP Header  (Src=VPN-GW-A, Dst=VPN-GW-B)  [20 B]  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SPI  [4 B]                  │  Sequence Number  [4 B]       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  IV  [16 B]  (for AES-CBC)                                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│   │ Original IP Header  [20 B]                           │  │
│   │ TCP Header  [20 B]                                   │  │
│   │ Application Data                                     │  │
│   │ Padding  [p B]                                       │  │
│   │ Pad Length  [1 B]  │  Next Header  [1 B]              │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│            (All of the above is ENCRYPTED)                   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ICV (HMAC-SHA-1-96)  [12 B]                                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. Field Roles (2 marks):**
- **SPI** → Identifies the SA at the receiver.
- **Sequence Number** → Anti-replay counter (monotonically incremented).
- **IV** → Randomness for the cipher (e.g., AES-CBC requires 16 B IV).
- **Encrypted Payload** → Original IP packet + padding + PadLength + NextHeader, all encrypted with the session key.
- **Padding** → Aligns the payload to the cipher block size (16 B for AES).
- **PadLength** → Tells the receiver how much padding to strip.
- **NextHeader** → Identifies the protocol of the *decrypted* payload (e.g., 4 = IPv4, 6 = TCP).
- **ICV** → HMAC-SHA-1-96 over SPI + Seq# + IV + Ciphertext — provides integrity and authentication.

**3. Anti-Replay Mechanism (2 marks):**
- The sender **monotonically increments** the 32-bit Sequence Number for every ESP packet.
- The receiver maintains a **sliding window** (default 64 packets) of the highest sequence number seen and a bitmask of received packets within the window.
- If a packet's Seq# is **below the left edge** of the window → **replay**, dropped.
- If a packet's Seq# is **inside the window but already seen** → **replay**, dropped.
- If a packet's Seq# is **inside the window and new** → accepted; mark in bitmask.
- If a packet's Seq# is **beyond the right edge** → accepted; **slide window right**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: IKE & VPN Deployment**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **a)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the **IKEv1 Phase 1 Main Mode** exchange. What is the purpose of the Diffie–Hellman exchange within it? **(7 marks)**
>
> **b)** Compare and contrast **IPsec, SSL/TLS, and SSH tunnels** in terms of OSI layer, encryption, authentication, and typical use case. Which one would you recommend for a **roadwarrior remote-access VPN** and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. IKEv1 Phase 1 Main Mode — Sequence Diagram (4 marks):**

```
Initiator (Host A)                       Responder (Host B)
        |                                          |
        |  (1) HDR, SAo (proposals)                |
        |------------------------------------------>
        |  (2) HDR, SAr (chosen proposal)           |
        |<------------------------------------------
        |  (3) HDR, KE, Ni                          |
        |------------------------------------------>
        |  (4) HDR, KE, Nr                          |
        |<------------------------------------------
        |  (5) HDR*, IDii, AUTH   (encrypted)       |
        |------------------------------------------>
        |  (6) HDR*, IDir, AUTH   (encrypted)       |
        |<------------------------------------------
        |                                          |
     [ISAKMP SA established - proceed to Phase 2]
```

- **Message 1 & 2:** Negotiation of transforms (encryption, hash, DH group, auth method, lifetime).
- **Message 3 & 4:** Diffie–Hellman public values exchanged + nonces.
- **Message 5 & 6:** Encrypted identity & authentication — peer identities revealed *only after* encryption is established (identity protection).

**2. Purpose of Diffie–Hellman (3 marks):**
- Establishes a **shared secret ($g^{ab} \mod p$)** between two parties over an **insecure channel** without ever transmitting the secret.
- The derived key provides **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** — even if long-term keys (PSK / private key) are later compromised, past session keys remain secure.
- This shared secret feeds into the **SKEYID_e (encryption), SKEYID_a (authentication), SKEYID_d (key derivation material)** key hierarchy that protects all subsequent IKE and IPsec traffic.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | IPsec | SSL/TLS | SSH |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | 3 (Network) | 5–6 (Session) | 7 (Application) |
| **Encryption** | AES-CBC / AES-GCM | AES-GCM (TLS 1.3) | AES-CTR / ChaCha20 |
| **Authentication** | PSK / X.509 / RSA-Sig | X.509 / PSK / Token | Password / Public Key |
| **Granularity** | Subnet / Host | Per-application | Per-port / SOCKS |
| **NAT Traversal** | NAT-T (UDP 4500) | Native (TCP 443) | Native (TCP 22) |
| **App Awareness** | Transparent | Often app-aware | App-specific |
| **Setup Complexity** | High (IKE, certs, SAs) | Medium | Low (one command) |
| **Typical Use** | Site-to-site VPN, 5G, cloud peering | HTTPS, OpenVPN, SSL-VPN | Port forwarding, admin access |
| **Firewall Friendliness** | Often blocked | Excellent | Good (port 22 usually open) |
| **Performance** | Fastest (kernel) | Fast | Moderate |

**2. Recommendation for Roadwarrior Remote-Access VPN (2 marks):**

> **Recommended: SSL/TLS-based VPN (e.g., OpenVPN in TCP 443 mode or a commercial SSL-VPN portal like Cisco AnyConnect).**

**Justification:**
- Roadwarriors connect from **uncontrolled networks** (cafés, hotels, airports) that often **block UDP 500/4500** and ESP/AH protocols — IPsec fails to traverse such firewalls and NATs.
- SSL/TLS runs over **TCP 443**, which is **almost never blocked** and traverses any NAT/proxy.
- The **client is lightweight** (often a browser or a small agent), requires no kernel-mode driver, no administrator privileges.
- Provides **per-application granularity** and easy integration with **MFA tokens**.
- For example, OpenVPN in TCP 443 + AES-256-GCM is a battle-tested choice for a roadwarrior deployment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Encrypted Tunnels Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AH with ESP:** Writing "AH encrypts the data" — **WRONG**. AH provides **integrity only, no encryption**. Only ESP provides confidentiality. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the "new outer IP header"** in tunnel-mode diagrams — the new IP header is the *defining feature* of tunnel mode. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Treating SA as a "session" or "channel":** A SA is **simplex** — two-way traffic needs **two SAs** (one inbound, one outbound). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up IKE ports:** IKE is **UDP 500**, not TCP. NAT-T is **UDP 4500**, not 500. ESP is **IP protocol 50**, not a port. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Stating "AH = protocol 50":** Wrong — AH is **51**, ESP is **50**. This single-digit error fails the question outright. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Skipping the anti-replay window in ESP explanation:** Examiners specifically check for the **sliding window** mechanism. (–2 marks)
> 7. **Drawing IKE Phase 1 as a 3-message exchange:** Phase 1 Main Mode is **6 messages**; Aggressive Mode is 3 (and is *insecure*). (–1 mark)
> 8. **Forgetting SKEYID_d:** Many students derive only SKEYID_e and SKEYID_a and forget the **derivation key** that actually seeds the IPsec SA keys. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Encrypted Tunnels**

- ⭐ An **encrypted tunnel** = a cryptographically protected logical channel over an untrusted network.
- ⭐ **IPsec** is a **Layer 3** protocol suite defined in **RFC 4301–4309**.
- ⭐ **AH (Protocol 51)** = Authentication + Integrity, **no encryption**.
- ⭐ **ESP (Protocol 50)** = Encryption + Integrity + Auth + Anti-replay.
- ⭐ **ESP provides confidentiality** — AH does not.
- ⭐ **Transport mode** preserves the original IP header; **Tunnel mode** adds a new outer IP header.
- ⭐ **Tunnel mode** is the default for VPNs.
- ⭐ A **Security Association (SA)** is **simplex (one-way)**; full duplex needs 2 SAs.
- ⭐ SA is uniquely identified by the **triple (Dest IP, Protocol, SPI)**.
- ⭐ **SPI** = 32-bit value chosen by the **receiver**.
- ⭐ Three databases: **SPD** (policy), **SAD** (active SAs), **PAD** (peer credentials).
- ⭐ **IKE** uses **UDP 500** (initial) and **UDP 4500** (NAT-T).
- ⭐ **IKEv1 Phase 1 Main Mode** = 6 messages; establishes ISAKMP SA.
- ⭐ **IKEv1 Phase 2 Quick Mode** = 3 messages; creates IPsec SAs.
- ⭐ **IKEv2** = 4 messages (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH) → much faster.
- ⭐ **Diffie–Hellman** gives **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)**.
- ⭐ **SKEYID_e** = encryption; **SKEYID_a** = authentication; **SKEYID_d** = key derivation.
- ⭐ **Anti-replay** = monotonically increasing 32-bit sequence number + sliding window (default 64 in IKEv2).
- ⭐ **AES block size = 16 bytes** — drives ESP padding calculation.
- ⭐ **HMAC-SHA-1-96** = 12-byte ICV; **HMAC-SHA-256** = 16-byte ICV.
- ⭐ **SSL/TLS tunnels** (Layer 5–6) are best for roadwarriors — TCP 443 traversal.
- ⭐ **SSH tunnels** (`-L`, `-R`, `-D`) are lightweight but per-port.
- ⭐ **L2TP/IPsec** = L2TP (no security) + IPsec ESP for confidentiality.
- ⭐ **NAT-Traversal (NAT-T)** encapsulates ESP inside **UDP 4500** to traverse NATs.
- ⭐ ESP **Tunnel-mode overhead** ≈ 50–60 bytes per packet (≈ 23% bandwidth loss on a 1500 B packet).
- ⭐ The **5 security services** of IPsec = **Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication + Anti-replay + Limited Traffic-flow Confidentiality** (TFC padding).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trusted Systems - Data Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT863 - NETWORK SECURITY PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trusted Systems - Data Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Trusted System?

A **Trusted System** is a computer system that has been engineered to enforce a specific security policy and is capable of enforcing a defined set of security principles (confidentiality, integrity, availability) such that the system can be relied upon to protect sensitive information. According to the **Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria (TCSEC / Orange Book)**, a system is "trusted" when it has sufficient evidence (formal verification, design review, testing) to assure that its security mechanisms will behave predictably under attack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *trusted system* is not the same as a *trustworthy system*. "Trusted" in this context means the user/owner has *placed their trust* in the system's security mechanisms, supported by rigorous verification, not merely that the system works correctly.

## 1.2 What is Data Access Control?

**Data Access Control** is the selective restriction of access to data, where only authorized users, processes, or systems are granted permission to read, write, modify, delete, or otherwise manipulate data resources. It forms the **operational core** of any trusted system by mediating every interaction between a *subject* (active entity: user, process) and an *object* (passive entity: file, record, hardware device).

The fundamental tuple of access control can be represented as:

$$
\text{Access} = f(\text{Subject}, \text{Object}, \text{Action}, \text{Context})
$$

where:
- **Subject** = active entity requesting access
- **Object** = passive resource being accessed
- **Action** = operation being attempted (read/write/execute)
- **Context** = environmental conditions (time, location, IP address)

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Corporate Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Tower:**
> Imagine a multi-story corporate office as your computer system. The **building** is the trusted system, the **employees** are subjects, and the **offices/filing cabinets** are objects containing sensitive data.
>
> - The **Reception Desk** = *Authentication System* (proves who you are via ID card)
> - The **Lift Access Card** = *Authorization Layer* (defines which floors you can reach)
> - The **Floor Supervisor** = *Reference Monitor* (mediates every entry/exit request)
> - The **Building's Safety Code** = *Security Policy* (rules like "HR files only after 9 AM")
> - The **Surveillance Cameras & Alarms** = *Audit & Intrusion Detection* (logs every access)
>
> A *trusted system* is one where the building architect, the fire marshal, and the security firm have all certified (verified) that these controls cannot be bypassed — even by a clever intruder.

## 1.4 The Trusted Computing Base (TCB) — The Heart of Trust

The **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** is the totality of protection mechanisms within a computer system responsible for enforcing security policy. It is the *only* part of the system that is trusted to enforce policy — any flaw in the TCB compromises the entire security posture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCB Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *"The totality of protection mechanisms within a computer system — including hardware, firmware, and software — responsible for enforcing security policy."* — Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria (DoD 5200.28-STD)

### Core TCB Components:

| Component | Role | Example |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **Hardware** | Root-of-trust, memory protection | TPM chip, MMU, CPU rings |
| **Kernel / OS Core** | Process isolation, system calls | Linux kernel, NT Executive |
| **Reference Monitor** | Mediates all subject–object access | SELinux, AppArmor |
| **Authentication Subsystem** | Verifies identity | PAM, Windows LSA |
| **Audit Subsystem** | Records security-relevant events | syslog, Event Viewer |
| **Security Policy Database** | Stores rules | SELinux policy file |

### The Three Trusted Computing Base Requirements (Anderson's Report, 1972):

> [!NOTE]
> For a system to be considered *truly trusted*, its TCB must satisfy:
>
> 1. **Complete Mediation** — Every access to every object must be checked against the security policy. No shortcut, no cached bypass.
> 2. **Tamper-Proof** — The TCB itself must be protected from unauthorized modification (self-protection).
> 3. **Verifiable** — The TCB must be small enough and well-structured enough to be subjected to analysis and testing (smallness, simplicity).
>
> These are the foundational pillars of any trusted system's design and are favorite KTU theory questions.

## 1.5 The Reference Monitor Concept

A **Reference Monitor** is the abstract machine concept that mediates all access of subjects to objects. It is the *conceptual* enforcement point of the security policy. The concrete, physical realization of a reference monitor is called the **Security Kernel**.

$$
\text{Reference Monitor} \xrightarrow{\text{enforces}} \text{Security Policy}
$$

Visualizing the relationship:

$$
\text{Subject} \rightarrow \text{[Reference Monitor]} \rightarrow \text{Object}
$$

The reference monitor is conceptually placed *between* every subject and every object in the system, ensuring **complete mediation**.

## 1.6 The Access Control Matrix — Foundational Structure

The **Access Control Matrix** (introduced by Butler Lampson in 1971) is a two-dimensional tabular model where rows represent subjects, columns represent objects, and each cell contains the set of access rights the subject holds for that object.

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix}
m_{11} & m_{12} & \cdots & m_{1n} \\
m_{21} & m_{22} & \cdots & m_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
m_{m1} & m_{m2} & \cdots & m_{mn}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where each cell $m_{ij}$ contains the access rights that subject $S_i$ has on object $O_j$, e.g., $\{r, w, x\}$ for read, write, execute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A system with three subjects $\{S_1, S_2, S_3\}$ and three objects $\{F_1, F_2, F_3\}$ might have the matrix:
>
> |   | $F_1$ (Payroll) | $F_2$ (Public) | $F_3$ (System) |
> |---|------|------|------|
> | $S_1$ (Alice) | $\text{rw}$ | $\text{r}$ | — |
> | $S_2$ (Bob) | $\text{r}$ | $\text{rw}$ | $\text{x}$ |
> | $S_3$ (Admin) | $\text{rwx}$ | $\text{rwx}$ | $\text{rwx}$ |

## 1.7 Three Generations of Access Control Models

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three primary access control paradigms:

### A. Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
The owner of an object decides *who* can access it. The user has *discretion* to grant or revoke access. Implemented via **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** or **capabilities**.

**Real-World Analogy:** Your personal Google Drive — *you* decide who can view/edit your document.

### B. Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
Access decisions are made by a central authority based on classification labels and clearance levels. Users *cannot* override the policy. The system enforces it.

**Real-World Analogy:** Military classified documents — even a General cannot grant an Ensign access to Top Secret material without proper clearance.

### C. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
Access is granted based on the *role* a user plays within an organization (e.g., "Manager", "Doctor", "Auditor"), not on individual identity.

**Real-World Analogy:** Hospital — anyone with the "Doctor" badge can enter the ward; it doesn't matter if it's Dr. Smith or Dr. Patel.

---

**Summary of Section 1:**
A trusted system enforces a verified security policy through a small, protected, and verifiable Trusted Computing Base. Data access control is the enforcement arm of the TCB, mediated by the reference monitor and expressed through models like DAC, MAC, and RBAC operating on an access control matrix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of a Trusted System

A trusted system's architecture follows a layered defense-in-depth model. The TCB sits at the innermost, most-protected layer, and security is enforced outward.

### Layered Architecture (Innermost to Outermost):

1. **Hardware Layer** — CPU rings (Ring 0 to Ring 3), MMU, TPM
2. **Security Kernel / TCB Core** — Reference Monitor, security policy database
3. **Operating System Services** — Process management, file system
4. **User Processes / Applications** — Subject programs
5. **External Entities** — Network, peripherals, remote users

## 2.2 Access Control Models — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

DAC is governed by a **policy** $P_{DAC}$ defined as a set of access rights managed by object owners.

**Policy Formalization:**

$$
P_{DAC} = \{ (s, o, a) \mid s \in S, \, o \in O, \, a \in A, \, \text{owner}(o) \text{ has granted } a \text{ to } s \}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of all subjects
- $O$ = set of all objects
- $A$ = access right set $\{r, w, x, \text{append}, \text{delete}, \text{own}\}$

**Implementation Structures:**

| Structure | Where Stored | Strength | Weakness |
|-----------|--------------|----------|----------|
| **Access Control List (ACL)** | Per object | Easy to see who has access | Hard to revoke from a user |
| **Capability List** | Per subject | Easy to revoke user access | Hard to see all users of an object |
| **Access Control Matrix** | Both views | Complete | Storage explosion in large systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The **"owner decides"** paradigm is the defining feature of DAC. This makes DAC flexible but vulnerable to **Trojan Horse attacks**, where a malicious program uses the legitimate user's authority to leak data — a classic KTU 14-mark question topic.

### 2.2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

MAC uses a **labeling system** combining classification (sensitivity) and category (compartment).

**Label Structure:**

$$
L = (c, k)
$$

Where:
- $c \in C$ = classification level (e.g., Unclassified $<$ Confidential $<$ Secret $<$ Top Secret)
- $k \subseteq K$ = set of categories (e.g., {Nuclear, Crypto, Personnel})

**Total Order on Classifications:**

$$
L_1 \ge L_2 \iff c_1 \ge c_2 \text{ and } k_2 \subseteq k_1
$$

**The Two Cardinal Rules of MAC (Bell-LaPadula):**

1. **No Read Up (Simple Security Property):** A subject $S$ can read object $O$ only if:
$$
L(S) \ge L(O)
$$
2. **No Write Down (Star Property, $\star$):** A subject $S$ can write to object $O$ only if:
$$
L(O) \ge L(S)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\star$-property prevents information leakage to lower-classified objects but creates the *covert channel* problem (a hidden signaling path that bypasses the policy). KTU frequently tests this with scenarios like "Alice is cleared Secret and Bob is cleared Top Secret — can Alice send a file to Bob?"

### 2.2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

Introduced by **Sandhu et al. (1996)**, RBAC has four conceptual levels standardized as **NIST INCITS 359**.

**Core RBAC Components:**

$$
\text{RBAC} = (U, R, P, S, PA, UA, RH)
$$

Where:
- $U$ = Users
- $R$ = Roles
- $P$ = Permissions
- $S$ = Sessions
- $PA \subseteq P \times R$ = Permission-Role assignment
- $UA \subseteq U \times R$ = User-Role assignment
- $RH \subseteq R \times R$ = Role Hierarchy (partial order)

**Role Hierarchy Property:**

$$
r_1 \succ r_2 \iff r_1 \text{ inherits all permissions of } r_2
$$

Example:

$$
\text{Chief Surgeon} \succ \text{Surgeon} \succ \text{Doctor} \succ \text{Employee}
$$

**RBAC Constraint — Separation of Duties (SoD):**

$$
\forall u \in U : \neg (r_1 \in \text{roles}(u) \land r_2 \in \text{roles}(u)) \text{ if } (r_1, r_2) \in SSD
$$

where $SSD$ is the Static Separation of Duties relation. This is critical in finance (the person who *authorizes* a payment cannot also *execute* it).

### 2.2.4 Other Notable Models (KTU 2024 Mentions)

| Model | Primary Goal | Key Rule |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| **Biba Model** | Integrity (MAC analog) | No Read Down, No Write Up |
| **Clark-Wilson** | Commercial integrity | Well-formed transactions, Separation of duties |
| **Chinese Wall (Brewer-Nash)** | Conflict of interest | Access decision depends on history |
| **Bell-LaPadula** | Confidentiality | No Read Up, No Write Down |
| **Lattice-Based** | Information flow | Labels form a lattice; flow respects $\le$ |

## 2.3 The Information Flow Model

The information flow model controls how information propagates through the system, independent of explicit access operations.

**Denning's Lattice Model (1976):**

$$
\text{Flow}(L) = (SC, \le, \oplus, \otimes)
$$

Where:
- $SC$ = set of security classes
- $\le$ = partial order (can-flow relation)
- $\oplus$ = join (least upper bound)
- $\otimes$ = meet (greatest lower bound)

**Flow Rule:**

$$
\text{Information may flow from } a \text{ to } b \iff a \le b
$$

This prevents "downward" leaks of sensitive information, even through indirect channels (e.g., timing, error messages).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Notation Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Memorize for KTU Exam:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Used In |
|--------|---------|---------|
| $S$ | Set of subjects | All models |
| $O$ | Set of objects | All models |
| $A$ | Access right set $\{r, w, x, a, d\}$ | DAC, MAC |
| $L(s)$ | Security label of subject $s$ | MAC |
| $L(o)$ | Security label of object $o$ | MAC |
| $M[i,j]$ | Rights of $S_i$ on $O_j$ | Matrix model |
| $r_i \succ r_j$ | $r_i$ inherits $r_j$ | RBAC |
| $PA$ | Permission-Assignment relation | RBAC |
| $UA$ | User-Assignment relation | RBAC |
| $SSD$ | Static Separation of Duties | RBAC |
| $SC, \le, \oplus, \otimes$ | Security class lattice | Information flow |

| Property / Rule | Formula | Model |
|------------------|---------|-------|
| Simple Security (No Read Up) | $L(s) \ge L(o) \implies \text{allow read}$ | Bell-LaPadula |
| Star Property (No Write Down) | $L(o) \ge L(s) \implies \text{allow write}$ | Bell-LaPadula |
| Biba Low-Water-Mark | $L'(s) = L(s) \otimes L(o)$ after read | Biba |
| Clark-Wilson CDI Access | $TP : CDI_i \to CDI_j$ via certified procedure | Clark-Wilson |
| Chinese Wall Sanitization | Once sanitized, conflict resolved | Brewer-Nash |
| Information Flow | $a \le b \implies$ flow $a \to b$ legal | Lattice |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these models matter in practice:**
> - **Banking:** RBAC + Clark-Wilson enforce "maker-checker" workflow in SWIFT systems.
> - **Defense:** MAC + Bell-LaPadula protect classified networks (e.g., the U.S. NIPRNet/SIPRNet).
> - **Healthcare:** RBAC + DAC (patient consents) in Electronic Health Records (EHR) systems.
> - **Cloud (AWS IAM, Azure RBAC):** Direct commercial implementation of NIST RBAC.
> - **Mobile (Android, iOS):** Hybrid MAC/RBAC with SELinux policies and sandboxing.
>
> KTU expects you to *connect theory to engineering practice* — a guaranteed 2-3 mark bonus on any 14-mark answer.

---

**Section 2 Recap:** The depth of a trusted system lies in choosing the right access control model (DAC vs MAC vs RBAC), formalizing it with mathematical rules (Bell-LaPadula, Biba, Clark-Wilson), and ensuring the TCB enforces it via the reference monitor with complete mediation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Access Control Matrix State Transitions

The state of an access control matrix evolves through six primitive operations (Harrison-Ruzzo-Ullman, 1976). Let $M$ be the current matrix. We derive the conditions under which a state transition is *safe* with respect to a generic security policy $\mathcal{P}$.

### The Six Primitive Operations:

| # | Operation | Notation | Effect |
|---|-----------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Enter $r$ into $M[s,o]$ | $\text{enter } r \text{ into } M[s,o]$ | Grant right |
| 2 | Delete $r$ from $M[s,o]$ | $\text{delete } r \text{ from } M[s,o]$ | Revoke right |
| 3 | Create subject $s'$ | $\text{create subject } s'$ | Add row |
| 4 | Create object $o'$ | $\text{create object } o'$ | Add column |
| 5 | Destroy subject $s$ | $\text{destroy subject } s$ | Remove row |
| 6 | Destroy object $o$ | $\text{destroy object } o$ | Remove column |

### Derivation of the HRU Safety Condition:

**Definition (Safety):** A state $Q$ of the access control system is *safe* with respect to a right $r$ if, in every state reachable from $Q$, no leakage of $r$ to an unauthorized subject occurs.

**Theorem (HRU Result, 1976):** The safety problem for the HRU model is **undecidable in the general case** but becomes **decidable** under these restrictions:

$$
\text{Safety is decidable} \iff
\begin{cases}
\text{(i) System has only one subject, OR} \\
\text{(ii) Subjects cannot create new subjects, OR} \\
\text{(iii) No "create" primitive is allowed}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step-by-Step Proof Sketch:

**Step 1:** Define the security policy $\mathcal{P}$ as a predicate on the matrix state:
$$
\mathcal{P}(M) \iff \forall s \in S, \forall o \in O : M[s,o] \subseteq \text{Authorized}(s, o)
$$

**Step 2:** A state $M_0$ is *safe* with respect to $r$ if no sequence of primitive operations leads to a state where $r \in M[s, o]$ for an unauthorized pair $(s, o)$:
$$
\text{Safe}(M_0, r) \iff \forall M_k \in \text{Reach}(M_0) : \nexists (s,o) \text{ s.t. } r \in M_k[s,o] \land (s,o) \notin \text{Auth}
$$

**Step 3:** Reduction to the **halting problem of a Turing Machine**: HRU (1976) showed that any Turing Machine can be encoded in the access control matrix operations. Specifically, the **create subject** primitive allows the system to grow unboundedly, mimicking TM tape expansion.

**Step 4:** Conclude: Because the halting problem is undecidable, the safety of HRU in the general case is undecidable. ∎

**Practical Consequence:** Real-world systems (e.g., **Grsecurity RBAC**, **SELinux MLS**) restrict the **create** primitive to achieve decidable safety.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Bell-LaPadula No Read-Up / No Write-Down

**Problem:** In a system with classifications $\{ \text{Public} < \text{Confidential} < \text{Secret} < \text{TopSecret} \}$, subject $S_1$ has clearance `Secret` and subject $S_2$ has clearance `TopSecret`. Object $O_1$ is classified `Confidential` and object $O_2$ is `TopSecret`. Determine the legality of these operations:

(a) $S_1$ reads $O_1$
(b) $S_1$ writes to $O_2$
(c) $S_2$ reads $O_2$
(d) $S_1$ writes to $O_1$

### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Define the labels as numeric levels for computation.

$$
\text{Public} = 0, \quad \text{Confidential} = 1, \quad \text{Secret} = 2, \quad \text{TopSecret} = 3
$$

So:
$$
L(S_1) = 2, \quad L(S_2) = 3, \quad L(O_1) = 1, \quad L(O_2) = 3
$$

**Step 2 (a): $S_1$ reads $O_1$.**

Apply Simple Security Property:
$$
L(S_1) \ge L(O_1) \implies 2 \ge 1 \implies \textbf{TRUE} \implies \text{ALLOW}
$$

**Step 3 (b): $S_1$ writes to $O_2$.**

Apply Star Property:
$$
L(O_2) \ge L(S_1) \implies 3 \ge 2 \implies \textbf{TRUE} \implies \text{ALLOW}
$$

**Step 4 (c): $S_2$ reads $O_2$.**

Apply Simple Security Property:
$$
L(S_2) \ge L(O_2) \implies 3 \ge 3 \implies \textbf{TRUE} \implies \text{ALLOW}
$$

**Step 5 (d): $S_1$ writes to $O_1$.**

Apply Star Property:
$$
L(O_1) \ge L(S_1) \implies 1 \ge 2 \implies \textbf{FALSE} \implies \text{DENY}
$$

This is a *No Write Down* violation. Even though $S_1$ could read $O_1$ (both $\ge 1$), writing would move Secret information down to Confidential — a confidentiality leak.

**Final Tabulation:**

| Operation | Rule Applied | Condition | Result |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| (a) $S_1$ reads $O_1$ | Simple Security | $2 \ge 1$ | ✅ ALLOW |
| (b) $S_1$ writes $O_2$ | Star Property | $3 \ge 2$ | ✅ ALLOW |
| (c) $S_2$ reads $O_2$ | Simple Security | $3 \ge 3$ | ✅ ALLOW |
| (d) $S_1$ writes $O_1$ | Star Property | $1 \ge 2$ | ❌ DENY |

## 3.3 Worked Example: RBAC Permission Resolution with Hierarchy

**Problem:** In a hospital information system, define the role hierarchy:

$$
\text{ChiefDoctor} \succ \text{Doctor} \succ \text{Resident} \succ \text{Intern}
$$

The Permission-Role assignments are:

$$
PA = \{ (\text{read\_xray}, \text{Doctor}), \, (\text{prescribe}, \text{ChiefDoctor}), \, (\text{update\_chart}, \text{Resident}) \}
$$

User $U_1$ is assigned the role `Resident`. Determine which permissions $U_1$ has at session open.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Inherited roles of `Resident` from the hierarchy:

$$
\text{Resident}^\succ = \{ \text{Resident}, \text{Intern} \} \quad \text{(Resident inherits from Intern)}
$$

**Step 2:** Direct and inherited permissions of `Resident`:

$$
\text{Perms}(\text{Resident}) = PA(\text{Resident}) \cup PA(\text{Resident}^\succ) = \{\text{update\_chart}\} \cup \{\emptyset\} = \{\text{update\_chart}\}
$$

**Step 3:** At session open, $U_1$ is granted:

$$
\text{EffectivePerms}(U_1) = \text{Perms}(\text{Resident}) = \{\text{update\_chart}\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $U_1$ were elevated to `Doctor`, they would gain `read_xray` via inheritance — a key feature of RBAC that simplifies administration.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Model Analysis (For 14-Mark Answers)

| Feature | DAC | MAC | RBAC | Bell-LaPadula | Biba |
|---------|-----|-----|------|---------------|------|
| **Decision Authority** | Object owner | Central policy | Role assignment | Policy engine | Policy engine |
| **Policy Flexibility** | High | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **Trojan Resistance** | Weak | Strong | Medium | Strong | Strong |
| **Best For** | Collaborative environments | Military / Gov | Enterprise | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| **User Override** | Allowed | Forbidden | Forbidden | Forbidden | Forbidden |
| **Administration Cost** | High (per-object) | Medium | Low (per-role) | Medium | Medium |
| **Covert Channel Risk** | Low | High | Low | High | Low |
| **Enforcement Layer** | OS file system | Kernel + TCB | Application/Middleware | Security kernel | Security kernel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pro Tip for 14-mark questions:** Always end with a comparative table like this. KTU examiners award full marks when students can *contrast* models, not merely list them.

## 3.5 Derivation: Information Flow Lattice Lower-Bound

**Problem:** Given security classes $A = \{ \text{Medical} \}$, $B = \{ \text{Financial} \}$, derive the meet ($\otimes$) and join ($\oplus$) operations for the power-set lattice $SC = \mathcal{P}(\{A, B\})$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Enumerate all elements of the power set:

$$
SC = \{\emptyset, \{A\}, \{B\}, \{A, B\}\}
$$

**Step 2:** Define partial order $\le$ as set inclusion:

$$
\emptyset \le \{A\}, \quad \emptyset \le \{B\}, \quad \{A\} \le \{A, B\}, \quad \{B\} \le \{A, B\}
$$

This forms a diamond-shaped lattice (the smallest non-trivial lattice).

**Step 3:** Compute the meet (greatest lower bound) for any two elements $X, Y$:

$$
X \otimes Y = X \cap Y
$$

| $X \otimes Y$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{A\}$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ |
|----|------|------|------|------|
| $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |
| $\{A\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{A\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{A\}$ |
| $\{B\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{B\}$ |
| $\{A,B\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{A\}$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ |

**Step 4:** Compute the join (least upper bound):

$$
X \oplus Y = X \cup Y
$$

| $X \oplus Y$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{A\}$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ |
|----|------|------|------|------|
| $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{A\}$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ |
| $\{A\}$ | $\{A\}$ | $\{A\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ |
| $\{B\}$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ |
| $\{A,B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ | $\{A,B\}$ |

**Step 5:** Verify lattice properties:
- ✅ $\emptyset$ is the bottom (least element)
- ✅ $\{A, B\}$ is the top (greatest element)
- ✅ Every pair has a meet and a join
- ✅ Associativity, commutativity, idempotence, absorption all hold

This lattice is the **basis of the Bell-LaPadula model** for two compartments, and scaling it to $n$ compartments yields $2^n$ security classes — exponential complexity, a major KTU conceptual question.

---

**Section 3 Recap:** The mathematical backbone of access control rests on three pillars: the **HRU matrix** (with its decidability restrictions), the **Bell-LaPadula** no-read-up / no-write-down invariants, and the **lattice-based information flow** model. Mastering these derivations is the path to full marks.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Reference Monitor Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

This diagram maps the conceptual placement of the reference monitor between subjects and objects, showing all required enforcement points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[User Subject S1] -->|Access Request| RM
    U2[Process Subject S2] -->|Access Request| RM
    U3[Remote Subject S3] -->|Access Request| RM
    RM{Reference Monitor}
    RM -->|Request| Auth[Authentication Module]
    Auth -->|Identity Verified| AuthDB[(Identity Database)]
    Auth -->|Token Issued| RM
    RM -->|Lookup Policy| SPD[(Security Policy Database)]
    SPD -->|Decision Rule| RM
    RM -->|Grant or Deny| Log[Audit Log]
    Log -->|Persist| AuditStore[(Audit Store)]
    RM -->|Allowed| Obj1[Object File 1]
    RM -->|Allowed| Obj2[Object DB Record 2]
    RM -->|Allowed| Obj3[Object Device 3]
    TCBShield[TCB Boundary] -.Protects.-> RM
    TCBShield -.Protects.-> SPD
    TCBShield -.Protects.-> AuditStore
    TCBShield -.Protects.-> AuthDB
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Every subject request is forced through the single funnel of the Reference Monitor. The TCB Boundary (dashed line) encloses the reference monitor, policy database, and audit store, ensuring no external code can tamper with them.

## 4.3 Access Control Decision Flow (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([User Triggers Action]) --> Q1{Authenticated?}
    Q1 -->|No| Login[Redirect to Login]
    Login --> CredCheck[Validate Credentials]
    CredCheck -->|Fail| DenyLog[Log Failure and Deny]
    CredCheck -->|Pass| Issue[Issue Session Token]
    Issue --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Yes| BuildReq[Build Access Request Tuple]
    BuildReq --> Tuple[Subject, Object, Action, Context]
    Tuple --> PolicyCheck{Reference Monitor Decision}
    PolicyCheck -->|Check Matrix| AC[Lookup Access Control Matrix]
    PolicyCheck -->|Check Roles| RB[Evaluate RBAC Role and Hierarchy]
    PolicyCheck -->|Check Labels| ML[Evaluate MAC Labels Bell LaPadula]
    AC --> Combine[Combine Decisions]
    RB --> Combine
    ML --> Combine
    Combine --> Q2{All Checks Pass?}
    Q2 -->|No| DenyFinal[Deny Access and Audit]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Allow[Grant Access]
    Allow --> ActLog[Log Successful Access]
    DenyFinal --> End([End])
    ActLog --> End
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q3 -->|No Dynamic Attributes| ABAC[ABAC Attribute Based]
    DAC --> A1[Examples: File ACLs, Linux File Permissions]

```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Request
    Request --> SimpleCheck{Check No Read Up}
    SimpleCheck -->|Violation| DenyRead[Deny and Log]
    SimpleCheck -->|Pass| StarCheck{Check No Write Down}
    StarCheck -->|Violation| DenyWrite[Deny and Log]
    StarCheck -->|Pass| Grant[Grant Access and Audit]

    DenyWrite --> [*]
    Grant --> [*]
```
